blocks.cap: One address, a fistful of names. The green point sits three quarters of the way from \(0\) to \(1\). Cut the trip into \(4\) equal ticks and it lands on the 3rd, into \(8\) ticks and it lands on the 6th, into \(12\) ticks and it lands on the 9th, so \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), \(\tfrac{6}{8}\), and \(\tfrac{9}{12}\) are all the very same spot. You can multiply the top and bottom by anything to mint a new name for it, like \(\tfrac{15}{20}\). Out of all of them, only \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) is the lowest terms name tag.

blocks.x: An app stores \(\tfrac{2}{5}\); display only allows bottom \(\leq 40\). Allowed bottoms are \(5k\leq 40\). How many different names fit?
hints: Forget the top number for a moment. The only thing that decides whether a name fits is its bottom, and the bottom is always \(5k\). So you are really asking how many whole numbers \(k\) keep \(5k\) from going past \(40\). That is just counting multiples of \(5\), the kind of thing you did back in Chapter 3.
List the bottoms in order. With \(k = 1\) the bottom is \(5\), with \(k = 2\) it is \(10\), and so on. Keep going as long as the bottom stays at or below \(40\), then count how many you wrote down.
blocks.sol: Each allowed name is \(\tfrac{2k}{5k}\) for some whole number \(k\), and the rule is that the bottom \(5k\) must be at most \(40\). The top never causes trouble, so the whole question collapses into a single condition on the bottom, \(5k \le 40\). That is exactly a count of the multiples of \(5\) that land at or below \(40\), the move you learned in Chapter 3. Dividing the bound by the step gives \(40 \div 5 = 8\), so \(k\) can be \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\) and no further, since \(k = 9\) would push the bottom to \(45\) and overflow the label. Those eight values of \(k\) produce the bottoms \(5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40\), one legal name apiece. The neat idea here is that the whole family of names for a fraction is nothing more than a list of multiples, so counting names is counting multiples. There are \(\boxed{8}\) names.

blocks.x: When two fractions appear side by side, the fastest test gives both the same denominator: pieces of one size can be counted against each other. This shared-denominator move also powers fraction addition, so it pays to meet it now.
Slopes \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) over shared bottom \(24\): sixths \(\times 4\), eighths \(\times 3\). Compare tops. Write the steeper slope as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: You cannot compare the tops while the bottoms disagree. Force both fractions onto the same bottom of \(24\) first, then the bigger top wins. Multiplying top and bottom by the same number does not change a fraction's value, it just renames it.

Multiply \(\frac{5}{6}\) by \(\frac{4}{4}\) to get \(\frac{20}{24}\), and multiply \(\frac{7}{8}\) by \(\frac{3}{3}\) to get \(\frac{21}{24}\). Now both bottoms are \(24\), so compare the tops \(20\) and \(21\). The bigger top names the steeper ramp, and your answer should be its original unbuilt fraction.
blocks.sol: While the two slopes wear different bottoms, the numbers on top live on different scales, so \(5\) and \(7\) cannot be raced against each other yet. The fix is to give them a shared bottom. A bottom of \(24\) works for both, since \(6\times 4 = 24\) and \(8\times 3 = 24\). Renaming a fraction by multiplying its top and bottom by the same number keeps its value untouched, it only changes the costume. Build the east ramp up by \(\frac{4}{4}\): $$\frac{5}{6} = \frac{5\times 4}{6\times 4} = \frac{20}{24}.$$ Build the west ramp up by \(\frac{3}{3}\): $$\frac{7}{8} = \frac{7\times 3}{8\times 3} = \frac{21}{24}.$$ Now both ramps measure their rise in twenty-fourths, so the comparison is just counting. We have \(20\) twenty-fourths against \(21\) twenty-fourths, and \(21 > 20\), so \(\frac{21}{24}\) is the larger slope. That bigger slope is the renamed west ramp, whose original fraction is \(\frac{7}{8}\). The steeper ramp is the west ramp, with slope \(\boxed{7/8}\).

blocks.x: Is \(\tfrac{4}{6}=\tfrac{6}{9}\)? Compute \(4\times 9\) and \(6\times 6\). Equal products mean equal fractions. Enter the common product if equal, else \(0\).
hints: Multiplying \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) by \(6\times 9\) cancels the bottom \(6\) and leaves \(4\times 9\). Multiplying \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) by the same \(6\times 9\) cancels the bottom \(9\) and leaves \(6\times 6\). Now you only have to compare two whole-number products, no fractions left.
Work out \(4\times 9\) and \(6\times 6\) separately. If they land on the same number, that number is your answer. If they differ, the answer is \(0\).
blocks.sol: Follow the judge's move and clear the bars. He multiplies the two equal-or-not numbers \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) by the same thing, \(6\times 9\), which keeps any true equality true. On the left, \(\tfrac{4}{6}\times 6\times 9\) cancels the \(6\) on the bottom and leaves \(4\times 9\). On the right, \(\tfrac{6}{9}\times 6\times 9\) cancels the \(9\) on the bottom and leaves \(6\times 6\). Notice what survived. The top of the first fraction got paired with the bottom of the second, and the bottom of the first got paired with the top of the second. The fractions are equal exactly when these two crossed products match. Now just compute. \(4\times 9 = 36\) and \(6\times 6 = 36\). They agree, so \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) really are the same number, and the common value is \(36\). That crossing trick is no accident. For any \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) and \(\tfrac{c}{d}\), clearing the bars the same way leaves \(a\times d\) against \(b\times c\), so the fractions are equal exactly when \(a\times d = b\times c\). The judge found his reduce-free test, and the answer here is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: Cross-multiplication test. To check whether \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) and \(\tfrac{c}{d}\) are the same number, you do not have to reduce anything. Re-cut both to the shared bottom \(b\times d\). The first turns into \(a\times d\) of those tiny pieces and the second into \(c\times b\) of them, and since the pieces are now identical in size, the two fractions match exactly when those counts match. So just cross-multiply, top of each times the bottom of the other, and check whether \(ad=bc\). If they are not equal, the bigger product belongs to the bigger fraction, so this also tells you which name points further along the line.
Reducing shrinks the numbers written, not the amount. The gcd from Chapter 3 does the whole job in one step.
Master fraction \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). Three locks: \(\tfrac{12}{21}\), \(\tfrac{20}{35}\), \(\tfrac{16}{30}\). Cross-product test each. How many equal \(\tfrac{8}{14}\)?
hints: You do not need to simplify a single fraction here. For each stamped lock \(\tfrac{c}{d}\), build the two products \(8 \times d\) and \(14 \times c\) and just ask whether they land on the same number. Equal products mean the lock belongs, different products mean it does not.

Take them one at a time. For \(\tfrac{12}{21}\), compare \(8 \times 21\) with \(14 \times 12\). For \(\tfrac{20}{35}\), compare \(8 \times 35\) with \(14 \times 20\). For \(\tfrac{16}{30}\), compare \(8 \times 30\) with \(14 \times 16\). Count up how many of the three pairs come out matching.
blocks.sol:
The cross product test says \(\tfrac{c}{d}\) equals \(\tfrac{8}{14}\) exactly when \(8 \times d = 14 \times c\). So for each lock she builds two products and checks whether they match. No reducing required, which is the whole point of the trick.

First lock, \(\tfrac{12}{21}\). She computes \(8 \times 21 = 168\) and \(14 \times 12 = 168\). The two products match, so this lock truly equals \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). It stays.

Second lock, \(\tfrac{20}{35}\). She computes \(8 \times 35 = 280\) and \(14 \times 20 = 280\). Match again, so this lock belongs too.

Third lock, \(\tfrac{16}{30}\). She computes \(8 \times 30 = 240\) and \(14 \times 16 = 224\). These are close, only \(16\) apart, but close does not count. The products differ, so \(\tfrac{16}{30}\) is a near miss and does not equal \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). That lock gets tossed.

Two of the three pairs matched, so two of the stamped fractions are genuinely equal to the master number. The cross product test caught the impostor without her ever simplifying a thing.

\(\boxed{2}\)

blocks.x: Ticks \(\tfrac{n}{36}\) for \(n=1\ldots 36\). Gold plate only where \(\gcd(n,36)=1\). Since \(36=2^2\times 3^2\), \(n\) must miss multiples of \(2\) and \(3\). How many ticks are gold plated?
hints: You are really counting how many numbers from 1 to 36 are coprime to 36, meaning they have no factor of 2 and no factor of 3. It is faster to count the ones that get thrown away than to check all 36 by hand. How many multiples of 2 are in 1 to 36, and how many multiples of 3?

There are 18 multiples of 2 and 12 multiples of 3 in the range, but the multiples of 6 got counted in both groups, so subtract them back once. That removes \(18 + 12 - 6 = 24\) numbers. Whatever is left out of 36 is your answer.
blocks.sol: A tick gets gold plated when its top number \(n\) shares no factor with 36 except 1. Since \(36 = 2\times 2\times 3\times 3\), the only prime factors lurking in 36 are 2 and 3. So \(n\) is safe exactly when it is not a multiple of 2 and not a multiple of 3. Instead of testing all 36 numbers, count the ones that fail and subtract. Among 1 to 36 there are \(\tfrac{36}{2}=18\) multiples of 2 and \(\tfrac{36}{3}=12\) multiples of 3. Adding those gives 30, but numbers like 6, 12, 18 got counted twice, once as a multiple of 2 and once as a multiple of 3. Those double counted numbers are exactly the multiples of 6, and there are \(\tfrac{36}{6}=6\) of them. So the count of numbers that have at least one bad factor is \(18 + 12 - 6 = 24\). The survivors are \(36 - 24 = 12\). You can sanity check by listing them, since the safe top numbers are \(1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 25, 29, 31, 35\), and that is indeed twelve of them. The clockmaker gold plates \(\boxed{12}\) ticks.

review.x: A window box is split into \(3\) equal sections; flowers fill the first section (\(\tfrac{1}{3}\)). Re-scoring into \(12\) cells: how many cells do the flowers cover?
hints: The total number of cells went from 3 to 12, which is 4 times as many. Whatever you do to the bottom of a fraction, do the same to the top so the value stays equal.
Multiply both the top and the bottom of \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) by \(4\). The top tells you how many of the 12 cells are filled.
review.sol: Scoring each of the 3 sections into 4 cells multiplies the total count by \(4\), turning 3 parts into \(3 \times 4 = 12\) parts. The flowers never moved, so the same patch of box is still covered. To keep the fraction equal while the bottom grows by a factor of \(4\), the top has to grow by the same factor of \(4\). $$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{1 \times 4}{3 \times 4} = \frac{4}{12}$$ So the filled section is now described as \(4\) of the \(12\) cells. You can check it the other way too, since \(\tfrac{4}{12}\) reduces back to \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). The flowers cover \(\boxed{4}\) cells.

review.x: Five sections report harmony fractions: \(\tfrac{8}{12}\), \(\tfrac{6}{10}\), \(\tfrac{10}{15}\), \(\tfrac{14}{21}\), \(\tfrac{9}{12}\). How many equal \(\tfrac{2}{3}\)?
hints: Check each fraction one at a time against \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). You can cross multiply, so \(\tfrac{a}{b}=\tfrac{2}{3}\) exactly when \(3a = 2b\), or you can simplify each fraction to lowest terms and see if it becomes \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).

Test \(\tfrac{8}{12}\) with the cross multiply rule \(3 \times 8 = 24\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\), so those two products match and that fraction counts. Do the same product check for the other four, then count up how many pass.
review.sol:
Two fractions name the same point exactly when their cross products are equal, so \(\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{2}{3}\) precisely when \(3a = 2b\).

Check each section.

\(\tfrac{8}{12}\): \(3 \times 8 = 24\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{6}{10}\): \(3 \times 6 = 18\) and \(2 \times 10 = 20\). Not equal, so this does not match.

\(\tfrac{10}{15}\): \(3 \times 10 = 30\) and \(2 \times 15 = 30\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{14}{21}\): \(3 \times 14 = 42\) and \(2 \times 21 = 42\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{9}{12}\): \(3 \times 9 = 27\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\). Not equal, so this does not match.

Three of the five sections report a fraction equal to \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).

\(\boxed{3}\)

review.x: A hose covers \(\tfrac{2}{7}\) of a garden row (\(2\) of \(7\) beds). Every bed is re-cut into \(4\) patches. After the re-cut, how many equal patches make up the whole row?
hints: The hose has not moved, so the new fraction must still equal \(\tfrac{2}{7}\). Renaming a fraction with finer pieces means multiplying the top and the bottom by the same number.
Each of the 7 beds becomes 4 patches. How many patches does the whole row hold? That count is the new bottom number.
review.sol: Cutting every bed into smaller pieces does not move the hose, so the watered share is still \(\tfrac{2}{7}\). We are just renaming that same amount with finer patches. Each of the 7 beds splits into 4 patches, so the whole row now holds \(7 \times 4 = 28\) equal patches. That \(28\) is the new bottom number. To be sure the hose still covers the same strip, count its patches too. The 2 watered beds each split into 4, giving \(2 \times 4 = 8\) patches, so the watered share is \(\tfrac{8}{28}\). And \(\tfrac{8}{28}\) reduces right back to \(\tfrac{2}{7}\), which confirms nothing about the coverage changed, only the names of the pieces. The whole row is made of \(\boxed{28}\) equal patches.

review.x: A jar is \(\tfrac{10}{25}\) full. Reduce to lowest terms by dividing by the common divisor. Write the simplest name as \(a/b\).
hints: What is the largest number that divides evenly into both 10 and 25? Both end in either 0 or 5, so 5 goes into each.
Divide the top and the bottom by 5. Ten split into groups of five gives the new top, and twenty-five split into groups of five gives the new bottom.
review.sol: A fraction stays the same value when you divide the top and bottom by the same number, so I look for a factor they share. Both \(10\) and \(25\) are multiples of \(5\), and \(5\) is the largest number that divides both, so \(\gcd(10,25)=5\). Dividing each part by \(5\) gives \(10\div 5 = 2\) on top and \(25\div 5 = 5\) on the bottom. That leaves \(\tfrac{2}{5}\), and since \(2\) and \(5\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), it cannot shrink any further. The robot's shortest label is \(\boxed{2/5}\).

review.x: \(30\) yes votes out of \(42\) total. Find \(\gcd(30,42)\), divide top and bottom by it. Write \(\tfrac{30}{42}\) in lowest terms as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: First find the greatest common divisor. List what divides both \(30\) and \(42\), or use the factor pairs from Chapter 3. The largest number that goes into both is \(6\).
Now divide the top and bottom by that \(6\) at the same time. Compute \(30 \div 6\) for the new numerator and \(42 \div 6\) for the new denominator.
review.sol: The fastest way to lowest terms is to peel off the whole common factor at once instead of chipping away at it. So start by finding \(\gcd(30,42)\). Both numbers are even, and looking closer, \(6\) divides \(30\) since \(30 = 6 \times 5\), and \(6\) divides \(42\) since \(42 = 6 \times 7\). Nothing larger divides both, so \(\gcd(30,42) = 6\). Now divide the top and bottom by that \(6\) in one step. $$\frac{30}{42} = \frac{30 \div 6}{42 \div 6} = \frac{5}{7}$$ Since \(5\) and \(7\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), the fraction is fully reduced. \(\boxed{5/7}\)

review.x: A label reads \(\tfrac{15}{28}\). Before reducing, check: is it already in lowest terms? A fraction is simplest when \(\gcd(\text{top},\text{bottom})=1\). Find \(\gcd(15,28)\).
hints: List the divisors of \(15\). They are \(1\), \(3\), \(5\), and \(15\). Now check which of those also divide \(28\) evenly.

\(28\) breaks into \(2 \times 2 \times 7\), so its only divisors are \(1\), \(2\), \(4\), \(7\), \(14\), and \(28\). Compare that list with the divisors of \(15\) and find the largest number that shows up in both.
review.sol: Break each number into its prime factors. The top, \(15\), is \(3 \times 5\). The bottom, \(28\), is \(2 \times 2 \times 7\). Now look at the two lists of primes side by side. One uses \(3\) and \(5\), the other uses \(2\) and \(7\). They share no prime at all. When two numbers have no prime factor in common, the only positive whole number dividing both of them is \(1\), so \(\gcd(15, 28) = 1\). That tells you the label \(\tfrac{15}{28}\) is already in lowest terms, and there is nothing left to cancel. The gcd you were asked for is \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: A reading of \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) must be rewritten over \(48\). Find \(k\) where \(12\times k=48\), then scale the top by \(k\). What top number is stored over \(48\)?
hints: First figure out what you multiply the bottom by. Since \(12 \times k = 48\), you need \(k = 48 \div 12\).
Whatever you multiply the bottom by, you must multiply the top by the same amount, or the value changes. Multiply \(7\) by that same \(k\).
review.sol: The bottom has to grow from \(12\) to \(48\), so first find the scaling number \(k\). Since \(12 \times k = 48\), we get \(k = 48 \div 12 = 4\). The trick with equivalent fractions is that the value only stays the same if you scale the top and the bottom by the exact same number, so you multiply the top by \(4\) too. That gives a top of \(7 \times 4 = 28\), which means \(\tfrac{7}{12} = \tfrac{28}{48}\). You can check it by reducing \(\tfrac{28}{48}\), dividing both parts by \(4\) lands you right back at \(\tfrac{7}{12}\). The number stored over \(48\) is \(\boxed{28}\).

review.x: A battery shows \(\tfrac{56}{64}\) charged. Divide top and bottom by their gcd. What is \(\tfrac{56}{64}\) in lowest terms as \(a/b\)?
hints: Find the greatest common divisor of 56 and 64. Both are even, and you can keep pulling out factors of 2.
The gcd of 56 and 64 is \(8\). Divide the top by \(8\) and the bottom by \(8\).
review.sol: A fraction is in lowest terms when the top and bottom share no common divisor larger than \(1\). So the job is to find the greatest common divisor of \(56\) and \(64\), then divide both by it. Both numbers are even, and in fact both are built from a lot of \(2\)s. We have \(56 = 8 \times 7\) and \(64 = 8 \times 8\), so their greatest common divisor is \(8\). Dividing both parts by \(8\) gives $$\frac{56}{64} = \frac{56 \div 8}{64 \div 8} = \frac{7}{8}.$$ Now \(7\) is prime and \(8\) is not a multiple of \(7\), so they share no factor beyond \(1\). The battery is \(\boxed{7/8}\) charged.

review.x: Sensor 1: \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). Sensor 2: \(\tfrac{12}{21}\). Cross-multiply: compare \(8\times 21\) against \(14\times 12\). Do they name the same point? Enter yes or no.
hints: The cross-multiply test ignores the reduced forms and just multiplies across the diagonals. Work out \(8 \times 21\) for one diagonal and \(14 \times 12\) for the other.
Both products land on \(168\). When the two cross products match exactly, the fractions are equivalent, so the readings point to the very same spot.
review.sol: The cross-multiply test takes the top of one fraction times the bottom of the other and checks both diagonals against each other. Run the first diagonal, \(8 \times 21 = 168\). Now run the second diagonal, \(14 \times 12 = 168\). The two products are the same, \(168 = 168\), so the test says the readings are equivalent. You can feel why by reducing each one. Dividing \(\tfrac{8}{14}\) by \(2\) gives \(\tfrac{4}{7}\), and dividing \(\tfrac{12}{21}\) by \(3\) also gives \(\tfrac{4}{7}\). Both sensors are really reporting the same fraction in disguise, so they do name the same point on the beam. \(\boxed{yes}\)

review.x: A dial stores \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) as \(\tfrac{x}{40}\). Cross-multiplying gives \(5\times 40=8\times x\). What is \(x\)?
hints: Cross-multiplying \(\dfrac{x}{40} = \dfrac{5}{8}\) means the diagonal products match, so \(8 \times x = 5 \times 40\). Work out the right side first.
You now have \(8 \times x = 200\). To free \(x\), undo the multiplication by dividing both sides by \(8\).
review.sol: Equivalent fractions point to the same spot, so \(\dfrac{x}{40}\) and \(\dfrac{5}{8}\) have to be equal. Cross-multiplying lines up the two diagonals, giving \(8 \times x = 5 \times 40\). The right side is \(5 \times 40 = 200\), so \(8 \times x = 200\). Dividing both sides by \(8\) leaves \(x = \dfrac{200}{8} = 25\). A quick check confirms it, since \(\dfrac{25}{40}\) reduces by \(5\) to \(\dfrac{5}{8}\), the value we started with. So the stored top number is \(\boxed{25}\).

review.x: A scanner counted \(162\) clean jars out of \(252\) total. Reduce \(\tfrac{162}{252}\) all the way to simplest form \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: Break both \(162\) and \(252\) all the way down into primes. Once you see which primes appear in both lists, you can pair them up to find the greatest common divisor (gcd).
The shared primes are one \(2\) and two \(3\)s, so the gcd is \(2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18\). Divide the top and bottom by \(18\).
review.sol:
Start by breaking each number all the way into primes.

$$162 = 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 2 \times 3^4$$

$$252 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 7 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 7$$

Now line up the primes the two numbers share. Both have a single \(2\) to spare, and both carry at least two \(3\)s. So the shared primes are one \(2\) and two \(3\)s. Multiply those together to get the greatest common divisor (gcd).

$$\gcd(162, 252) = 2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18$$

Divide the numerator and denominator by \(18\).

$$\frac{162}{252} = \frac{162 \div 18}{252 \div 18} = \frac{9}{14}$$

Since \(9 = 3^2\) and \(14 = 2 \times 7\) share no primes, the fraction is fully reduced. The clean-jar share is \(\boxed{9/14}\).

review.x: A machine stamps fractions equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) with bottom \(\leq 80\). Allowed bottoms are multiples of \(8\). How many such fractions fit?
hints: Every fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) comes from multiplying both \(3\) and \(8\) by the same whole number, so the bottom is always a multiple of \(8\). Which multiples of \(8\) are no bigger than \(80\)?
List them and count: \(8, 16, 24, \dots\) up to \(80\). Or just notice that the multiples of \(8\) up to \(80\) are \(8\times 1\) through \(8\times ?\).
review.sol: A fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is built by multiplying the top and the bottom by the same whole number \(k\), giving \(\tfrac{3k}{8k}\). Since \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is already in lowest terms, the bottom of any equal fraction is exactly a multiple of \(8\), namely \(8k\). We need \(8k\) to be a whole number no larger than \(80\), so \(8k \le 80\), which means \(k \le 10\). The allowed values are \(k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, 10\), giving the bottoms \(8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80\). That is \(10\) values of \(k\), so there are \(\boxed{10}\) such fractions.

review.x: Prize wheel: \(30\) slots labelled \(\tfrac{n}{30}\). Sticker when \(\gcd(n,30)=1\). \(30=2\times 3\times 5\), so \(n\) must miss multiples of \(2\), \(3\), \(5\). How many slots get stickers?
hints: You only need to keep the values of \(n\) that share no factor with 30. Since \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), cross off every even number, every multiple of 3, and every multiple of 5 from the list 1 through 30.

Start with all 30 numbers, then remove the 15 evens, then remove the multiples of 3 that are still left, then the multiples of 5 that are still left. Count what survives. You can also just list the survivors directly and they will all be numbers ending in 1, 3, 7, or 9 that are not multiples of 3.
review.sol: A slot wins when \(\gcd(n, 30) = 1\), and because \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), the winning \(n\) cannot be a multiple of 2, of 3, or of 5. The cleanest way is to just hunt for the survivors. Any even number is out, so I only look at odd numbers. Any multiple of 5 is out, which removes 5, 15, 25. Any multiple of 3 is out, which removes 3, 9, 15, 21, 27. What is left from 1 to 30 is \(1, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 29\). Counting those gives 8 winners. There is also a slick shortcut once you notice the factors. Of the 30 numbers, exactly half are even, so 15 survive the "no 2" rule. Of those that survive, one third would be multiples of 3 and one fifth would be multiples of 5, and the pattern of crossing-out leaves a fraction \(\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{5}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} = \tfrac{8}{30}\) of the whole, and \(\tfrac{8}{30}\) of 30 is \(8\). Both roads land in the same place. $$\boxed{8}$$

blocks.x: A baker gives her apprentice \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of a dough strip; he uses half of that for a braid. The braid is a part of a part, visibly smaller than either. What single fraction of the whole strip names it?
A garden bed has \(12\) equal squares; \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) are covered. Split \(12\) into \(3\) groups, keep \(2\). How many squares end up covered?
hints: The bottom number of \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) tells you how many equal groups to split the 12 squares into. Make the groups first, before you worry about how many to keep.
blocks.sol: The word of here is a quiet instruction. Two thirds of 12 means we want \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the 12 squares, and the way to find a fraction of an amount is to split, then keep. The bottom number, the 3, says split the 12 squares into three equal groups. \(12 \div 3 = 4\), so each group is 4 squares. The top number, the 2, says keep two of those groups. Two groups of 4 squares is \(2 \times 4 = 8\) squares under the cloth. Notice what just happened. Finding \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of 12 came out exactly the same as \(\tfrac{2}{3} \times 12 = \tfrac{2 \times 12}{3} = \tfrac{24}{3} = 8\). The little word of behaved precisely like a multiply sign, and it will keep doing that even when the amount you take a part of is itself a fraction. $$\boxed{8}$$

blocks.x: A worker uses \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of a netting roll, then patches \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of that panel. The patch is \(1\) cell in the grid of equal cells. Write it as \(a/b\) of the whole roll.
hints: You do not need to multiply anything yet, just count slivers. Each of the 4 equal panels gets cut into 3 equal slivers. Lay the slivers end to end down the whole roll and count how many there are in total.
There are \(4 \times 3 = 12\) equal slivers covering the whole roll, and they are all the same size. Her patch is one of those 12 equal pieces, so it is one part out of 12 of the whole.
blocks.sol: Start with the panel she actually unrolled. It is one of 4 equal panels, so it is \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of the whole roll. She does not use the whole panel though. She cuts that panel into 3 equal slivers and keeps 1, so she uses \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of that quarter. The question is really asking for \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of the roll. Here is the picture that nails it. When you carry the 3-way cut across all 4 panels, the second cut tripled the number of pieces. You went from 4 equal panels to \(4 \times 3 = 12\) equal slivers, and every sliver is identical. Her patch is exactly one of those 12 equal slivers, so it is \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) of the whole roll. That matches the rule for a unit fraction of a unit fraction. Taking \(\tfrac{1}{a}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) means cutting the whole into \(a\) parts and then each part into \(b\) parts, which leaves \(a \times b\) equal pieces, so the result is \(\tfrac{1}{ab}\). With \(a = 4\) and \(b = 3\) that gives \(\tfrac{1}{4 \times 3} = \tfrac{1}{12}\). $$\boxed{1/12}$$

blocks.x: Of means multiply. Taking a part of a part is cutting, then cutting again inside one of the pieces. The first cut splits the whole into \(a\) pieces, and cutting one of those into \(b\) pieces chops the whole into \(a\times b\) tiny equal pieces, of which you keep one. That is why \(\tfrac{1}{a}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) lands on \(\tfrac{1}{ab}\), and it is the secret the word of has held all along. Of means multiply, the same as when you took two thirds of \(12\). A fraction of a fraction always comes out smaller, since each cut leaves a thinner sliver.

The bottom-left block where both tints overlap is a 2 by 3 patch of 6 cells filled in gold, labeled 6 of 15 cells.">

  <rect x="70" y="143.2" width="200" height="124.8" fill="var(--gold2)"/>

  <!-- label counting 6 of 15 on the gold block -->

blocks.cap: Start with one whole square. Slicing the width into 3 equal columns and keeping the left 2 marks off \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Slicing the height into 5 equal rows and keeping the bottom 3 marks off \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Together the cuts break the square into \(3\times 5 = 15\) little cells. Taking \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) strip means keeping only the part of that strip that also sits in the left \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), and that overlap is the gold block, \(2\times 3 = 6\) cells. So \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{6}{15}\). The tops multiplied to count the gold cells, and the bottoms multiplied to count all the cells.

blocks.x: A \(3\times 5\) mosaic: pale glass fills \(2\) of \(3\) columns; gold fills \(3\) of \(5\) rows. What fraction of the panel does the overlap cover? Reduce fully, write as \(a/b\).
hints: The grid is \(3\) columns wide and \(5\) rows tall, so the whole panel is cut into \(3 \times 5\) equal cells. The overlap is the block that is both in the pale \(2\) columns and in the gold \(3\) rows. How many columns wide is that block, and how many rows tall? Those two counts are the tops of your fractions.

Multiply tops with tops and bottoms with bottoms. Two thirds of three fifths is \(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 5} = \frac{6}{15}\). Now find \(\gcd(6,15)\) and divide both numbers by it.
blocks.sol: Read the picture as a count. The panel is sliced into \(3\) columns and \(5\) rows, so there are \(3 \times 5 = 15\) equal cells, and each cell is \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) of the whole. The pale glass fills the left \(2\) columns, and the gold leaf fills the bottom \(3\) rows. The overlap is the rectangle that is \(2\) columns wide and \(3\) rows tall, so it holds \(2 \times 3 = 6\) cells. That is why multiplying fractions multiplies the tops and multiplies the bottoms, the tops count how wide and tall the overlap block is, and the bottoms count how the whole was cut up. So \(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 5} = \frac{6}{15}\). Now reduce. The gcd of \(6\) and \(15\) is \(3\), and dividing both by \(3\) gives \(\frac{6 \div 3}{15 \div 3} = \frac{2}{5}\). The overlap covers \(\frac{2}{5}\) of the panel, which makes sense, two thirds of three fifths should land a little under half. $$\boxed{2/5}$$

blocks.x: Fraction multiplication rule. To multiply two fractions, multiply the tops to get the new top and the bottoms to get the new bottom, $$\frac{a}{b}\times\frac{c}{d}=\frac{a\times c}{b\times d}.$$ Read off a grid, the new top counts the overlap, so many cells across times so many up, and the new bottom counts the whole square, columns times rows. This one rule swallows everything before it. A whole number is just itself over \(1\), and taking a fraction of an amount is this same multiply.
One batch needs \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) cup of cherries; \(6\) batches are made. Compute \(6\times\tfrac{5}{8}\). Leave your answer as an improper fraction.
hints: Six batches means you lay down \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup, then another \(\frac{5}{8}\), and so on, 6 times over. The eighths are all the same size, so think about how many eighths you end up with once you have collected 6 of these scoops.

Write the 6 as \(\frac{6}{1}\) and multiply straight across, tops times tops and bottoms times bottoms. The bottom stays 8 because each scoop is still an eighth of a cup, only the count of eighths changed. So you get \(\frac{6 \times 5}{1 \times 8}\), then simplify.
blocks.sol:
Six batches is just \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup repeated 6 times. The size of each scoop never changes, so every piece is still one eighth of a cup. What grows is how many eighths you have collected.

Each batch brings 5 eighths. After 6 batches you have \(6 \times 5 = 30\) eighths of a cup. The bottom of the fraction stays 8 the whole time, because an eighth is still an eighth no matter how many you pile up.

You can see this as the whole number 6 written as \(\frac{6}{1}\), then multiplied across.
$$\frac{6}{1} \times \frac{5}{8} = \frac{6 \times 5}{1 \times 8} = \frac{30}{8}$$

Now simplify. Both 30 and 8 share a factor of 2, so \(\frac{30}{8} = \frac{15}{4}\).

The troop needs $$\boxed{15/4}$$ cups of dried cherries in all.

blocks.x: Cancel before multiplying. When you multiply fractions, every top piles into one big top and every bottom into one big bottom, so it does not matter which fraction a number started in. If some number up top and some number down low share a factor, you can divide both by it right now, before multiplying anything, even when one sits in the left fraction and the other in the right. A matched factor like \(4\) over \(4\) would only cancel to \(1\) anyway, so pulling it out early spares you from wrestling with huge numbers. It is the same self-cancelling from 4.2, except the answer lands already reduced.
Compute \(\tfrac{14}{15}\times\tfrac{25}{21}\): prime-factor each number, cancel any prime appearing in a top and a bottom, multiply what remains. Fully reduced result as \(a/b\)?
hints: Before you multiply anything, look across the two fractions for shared factors. The 14 on top and the 21 on the bottom both hold a 7. The 25 on top and the 15 on the bottom both hold a 5. A factor on any top cancels with the same factor on any bottom, no matter which fraction it sits in.

Write it as \(\dfrac{2\cdot 7}{3\cdot 5}\times\dfrac{5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 7}\). Cross out the matching \(7\) (from 14 and 21) and one matching \(5\) (from 25 and 15). What survives on top is \(2\cdot 5\) and on the bottom is \(3\cdot 3\). Multiply each side.
blocks.sol: Multiplying tops and bottoms straight away gives \(\frac{14\times 25}{15\times 21}=\frac{350}{315}\), and now you are stuck reducing a big fraction. The trick is to cancel before you multiply, because in a product of fractions every top factor is paired against every bottom factor. \nBreak each number into primes. \(14=2\cdot 7\), \(25=5\cdot 5\), \(15=3\cdot 5\), and \(21=3\cdot 7\). So the product is $$\frac{2\cdot 7}{3\cdot 5}\times\frac{5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 7}=\frac{2\cdot 7\cdot 5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 5\cdot 3\cdot 7}.$$\nNow strike out a factor on top against the same factor on the bottom. The \(7\) (from \(14\)) cancels the \(7\) (from \(21\)), and one \(5\) (from \(25\)) cancels the \(5\) (from \(15\)). What remains on top is \(2\cdot 5=10\), and what remains on the bottom is \(3\cdot 3=9\). \nThat leaves \(\frac{10}{9}\), already in lowest terms since \(\gcd(10,9)=1\). The same answer you would get from \(\frac{350}{315}\) after a lot more work, with none of the mess. \(\boxed{10/9}\)

blocks.x: \(\tfrac{3}{4}\times\tfrac{4}{5}\times\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\): each bottom is the next top. Most cancel. What single reduced fraction remains?
hints: Do not multiply the tops into one giant number and the bottoms into another. Instead, write all four numerators on one line over all four denominators on one line, like \(\dfrac{3\times 4\times 5\times 6}{4\times 5\times 6\times 7}\), and then hunt for numbers that appear in both rows.

A factor on top cancels the same factor on the bottom. The \(4\) shows up on top and on bottom, so it vanishes. So does the \(5\). So does the \(6\). Cross all three pairs out and read off whatever is still standing on top and on bottom.
blocks.sol:
The slow way is to multiply everything out, but the chain is begging us not to. Stack all four numerators over all four denominators in one big fraction,
$$\frac{3}{4}\times\frac{4}{5}\times\frac{5}{6}\times\frac{6}{7}=\frac{3\times 4\times 5\times 6}{4\times 5\times 6\times 7}.$$\nNow the staircase pays off. The \(4\) sits on top and also on the bottom, so that pair cancels to \(1\). The \(5\) does the same. The \(6\) does the same. Three matching pairs quietly delete themselves,
$$\frac{3\times \cancel{4}\times \cancel{5}\times \cancel{6}}{\cancel{4}\times \cancel{5}\times \cancel{6}\times 7}=\frac{3}{7}.$$\nWhat survives is exactly the first numerator and the last denominator, the two ends of the chain that had no partner to cancel against. Everything in the middle was both a top and a bottom, so it washed out. And \(\tfrac{3}{7}\) is already in lowest terms, since \(3\) and \(7\) share no factor bigger than \(1\). The colony passes along \(\;\boxed{\dfrac{3}{7}}\;\) of the spores it started with.

blocks.x: A telescoping product collapses like a folding spyglass: each bottom matched the next top, cancelled, leaving only the first top and last bottom standing.

The word "of" means multiply. Every time you hear the word of you can swap in a times sign, so a long string of "two thirds of three fifths of..." is one big multiplication. To do it, multiply across the tops and across the bottoms, and make life easy by cancelling any factor a top shares with a bottom before you ever multiply. A whole number is welcome too, showing up as itself over \(1\). And here is the quiet shock that keeps returning. Multiplying two proper fractions gives an answer smaller than both, because taking a part of an already partial amount can only leave less.

Every "of" shrinks. Division asks the reverse: how many small things fit in one big one? That is multiplication running backward. The tool that flips division back into multiplication is the reciprocal.
A machine scales by \(\tfrac{7}{12}\); a second machine must undo it so the combined product is \(1\). What fraction times \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) gives \(1\)? Write as \(a/b\).
hints: Multiplying fractions multiplies the tops together and the bottoms together. For the result to be \(1\), the top of the product and the bottom of the product have to come out equal. Picture what the second fraction's top and bottom would need to be so that the \(7\) and the \(12\) end up matched.

The \(7\) sits on top of \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), so to cancel it you need a \(7\) on the bottom of your fraction. The \(12\) sits on the bottom, so to cancel it you need a \(12\) on top. Put those together.
blocks.sol: You are hunting for a fraction \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) so that \(\tfrac{7}{12}\times\tfrac{a}{b}=1\). Multiplying fractions just multiplies tops and bottoms, which gives \(\tfrac{7\times a}{12\times b}\). A fraction equals \(1\) exactly when its top and bottom are the same number, so you want \(7\times a\) to match \(12\times b\) in the cleanest possible way. The simplest move is to make the top a \(12\) and the bottom a \(7\), because then the new top is \(7\times 12\) and the new bottom is \(12\times 7\), and those are the same value. So flipping \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) upside down hands you \(\tfrac{12}{7}\). Checking, \(\tfrac{7}{12}\times\tfrac{12}{7}=\tfrac{84}{84}=1\), which is the unchanged stick you wanted. The partner that reverses a fraction's scaling is always that fraction turned over, and it is called the reciprocal. \(\boxed{12/7}\)

blocks.x: Reciprocal. The partner that multiplies with a fraction to land on exactly \(1\) is that same fraction turned upside down, so the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) is \(\tfrac{b}{a}\). The flip always works, because multiplying gives \(a\times b\) sitting over the very same \(a\times b\), and any number over itself is \(1\). Three small things fall out. The number \(1\) flips to itself and so does \(-1\), and \(0\) has no reciprocal at all, since nothing you multiply by \(0\) can ever climb up to \(1\), the same reason you can never divide by \(0\).

blocks.cap: Flipping a fraction sends a point on one side of \(1\) to a partner on the other side, and the two always multiply to \(1\). Here \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) sits left of \(1\) and its reciprocal \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) sits right of \(1\), and \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{3}\) straddle \(1\) the same way. Only \(1\) sits on the pivot and partners with itself, while \(0\) has no partner because nothing reaches all the way up to \(1\) from \(0\).

blocks.x: Five builders share a \(3\) m plank: \(3\div 5 = 3\times\tfrac{1}{5}\). How long is each share in meters? Write as \(a/b\).
hints: Splitting the plank into five equal pieces and keeping one piece is exactly taking one fifth of the plank. So instead of \(3 \div 5\), you can ask what is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of \(3\) meters.
Taking \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of something means multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\), so each share is \(3 \times \tfrac{1}{5}\). Multiply the \(3\) by the top of the fraction and keep the \(5\) on the bottom.
blocks.sol: Each builder gets the plank shared equally among five people, which is \(3 \div 5\). The cleanest way to see what that number is comes from the meaning of division itself. Splitting the plank into \(5\) equal pieces and keeping one piece is the same as taking one fifth of the plank, and taking \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of a length means multiplying that length by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\). So each share is $$3 \times \tfrac{1}{5}.$$ Picture three separate one meter rods laid end to end. If you cut every rod into five equal slivers, each sliver is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of a meter, and one builder collects one sliver from each of the three rods, which is three of those fifth meter slivers. Three copies of \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). This is the quiet truth hiding behind division by a whole number. Dividing \(3\) by \(5\) is multiplying \(3\) by the reciprocal \(\tfrac{1}{5}\), and the answer is simply the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Each builder's share is \(\dfrac{3}{5}\) of a meter, so $$\boxed{3/5}.$$

blocks.x: Division by a fraction. Dividing always asks the same thing, how many of the divisor fit. Splitting something into \(b\) equal shares and keeping one is just taking \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) of it, so dividing by \(b\) and multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) are the same move. And asking how many quarter cups fit in \(3\) cups gives \(12\), since each cup holds \(4\) of them, and that \(4\) is exactly the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). So to divide by any fraction, flip it to its reciprocal and multiply, $$a\div\frac{p}{q}=a\times\frac{q}{p}.$$ Every division hands itself straight back to the multiplying you already know.
A trim piece is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) m. You want pieces of \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) m each. Keep the first fraction, flip the divisor, and multiply. What is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\div\tfrac{2}{9}\) in lowest terms?
hints: Only the divisor flips. The \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) stays exactly as it is, and \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) turns into \(\tfrac{9}{2}\). Now the \(\div\) becomes a \(\times\), so you are really finding \(\tfrac{5}{6} \times \tfrac{9}{2}\).

Before you multiply across, look for a shared factor. The \(9\) on top and the \(6\) on the bottom both have a factor of \(3\), so the \(9\) becomes \(3\) and the \(6\) becomes \(2\). Then multiply straight across, \(5 \times 3\) over \(2 \times 2\).
blocks.sol: Asking how many \(\tfrac{2}{9}\)-meter pieces fit inside \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of a meter is a division question, \(\tfrac{5}{6} \div \tfrac{2}{9}\). To divide by a fraction, keep the first one and flip the second, then multiply. The \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) stays put and \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) flips to \(\tfrac{9}{2}\), so $$\frac{5}{6} \div \frac{2}{9} = \frac{5}{6} \times \frac{9}{2}.$$ Now cancel before multiplying. The \(9\) on top and the \(6\) on the bottom share a factor of \(3\), so the \(9\) shrinks to \(3\) and the \(6\) shrinks to \(2\). That leaves $$\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{9}{2} = \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{5 \times 3}{2 \times 2} = \frac{15}{4}.$$ So \(\tfrac{15}{4}\) of those pieces fit, which is \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) pieces. You get three full pieces and three quarters of another one left over, which matches the "how many fit" feeling. The fully reduced answer is \(\boxed{15/4}\).

font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#475569" text-anchor="middle"> <text x="40" y="114">0</text><text x="200" y="114">1</text><text x="360" y="114">2</text><text x="520" y="114">3</text> </g> <text x="280" y="152" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#8a5a08">3 ÷ ¼ = 12</text> <text x="280" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#475569">a piece smaller than 1 fits many times, so the answer is bigger than 3</text> </svg>
blocks.cap: Dividing asks how many of the divisor fit inside. \(3\div\tfrac14\) counts the quarter-cups in 3 cups: each cup holds 4, so \(3\times4=12\). Dividing by a piece smaller than 1 gives an answer bigger than 3.

blocks.x: A \(5\) L carboy is poured into \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) L jars. One liter fills \(3\) jars; the carboy holds \(5\) liters. How many \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) L jars does the full carboy fill?
hints: Forget the division symbol for a second and just count. Each single liter breaks into how many of these little third liter jars? Then you have 5 whole liters to pour, not just one.
Three thirds fit in every liter, so one liter fills \(3\) jars. With \(5\) liters lined up, you fill \(5\) groups of \(3\) jars. Multiply \(5\times 3\).
blocks.sol: The question hiding inside this is \(5\div\tfrac{1}{3}\), which asks how many third liter jars fit into \(5\) whole liters. Start with a single liter. It splits cleanly into \(3\) jars, because \(\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}=1\). Now line up all \(5\) liters from the carboy. Each one gives \(3\) jars, so the total is \(5\) groups of \(3\), which is \(5\times 3=15\). Here is the part worth sitting with. We divided by \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) and the answer jumped up to \(15\), far bigger than the \(5\) we started with. That feels backward until you remember what the jars are doing. They are tiny, smaller than a whole liter, so a great many of them fit into the same amount of water. Dividing by a number below \(1\) always grows the answer for exactly this reason. The full carboy fills \(\boxed{15}\) jars.

blocks.x: A fundraiser raised \(32\) (thousands). That is \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the goal. Multiply \(32\) by \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) to recover the whole. What is the full goal, in thousands?
hints: You are not asked for two thirds of something here. You already know the two thirds part, it is the 32, and you are hunting for the whole. Dividing by a fraction is how you walk a multiplication backward, so think about \(32 \div \tfrac{2}{3}\).

Dividing by \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) is the same as multiplying by the flipped fraction \(\tfrac{3}{2}\). So compute \(32 \times \tfrac{3}{2}\), and notice the 32 and the 2 are begging to cancel before you multiply.
blocks.sol: The forward story is goal \(\times\,\tfrac{2}{3} = 32\). To find the goal you reverse that step, so you divide both sides by \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Dividing by a fraction is multiplying by its reciprocal, which gives goal \(= 32 \times \tfrac{3}{2}\). Before multiplying, cancel the 32 against the 2 in the bottom, since \(32 = 16 \times 2\). That leaves \(16 \times 3 = 48\). Check it forward, two thirds of 48 is \(48 \div 3 = 16\), then \(16 \times 2 = 32\), exactly the amount in the jar. The full goal is \(\boxed{48}\) thousand dollars.

blocks.x: Finding the whole from a part. The word of multiplies a number by a fraction and shrinks it into a part. So when a problem hands you the part and hides the whole, all that happened was that multiplication run forward. To climb back to the whole, multiply the known part by the reciprocal, the flipped fraction, because that is exactly what undoes the original times. If \(\tfrac{p}{q}\) of a mystery number is \(k\), the number is \(k\times\tfrac{q}{p}\). For instance, if two thirds of a number is \(32\), the number is \(32\times\tfrac{3}{2}=48\), and this same flip works every time.
Feed \(12\) into a machine: first multiply by \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), then divide by \(\tfrac{8}{15}\). What is the result?
hints: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal. So the second setting, divide by \(\tfrac{8}{15}\), is really multiply by \(\tfrac{15}{8}\). Once both steps are multiplications, the student's claim is easy to test, because two multiplications in a row are just one multiplication by their product.

The single fraction is \(\tfrac{4}{9}\times\tfrac{15}{8}\). Before multiplying straight across, cancel across the fractions. The \(4\) and the \(8\) share a factor of \(4\), and the \(15\) and the \(9\) share a factor of \(3\). That leaves \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times\tfrac{5}{2}=\tfrac{5}{6}\). Now compute \(12\times\tfrac{5}{6}\).
blocks.sol: Start by turning the divide step into a multiply step, since that is the move that makes everything line up. Dividing by \(\tfrac{8}{15}\) is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{15}{8}\). Now both settings are multiplications, so the whole machine does \[\text{size}\;\times\;\tfrac{4}{9}\;\times\;\tfrac{15}{8}.\] Two multiplications back to back are just one multiplication by their product, which is exactly the student's point. So the single fraction hiding inside the machine is \(\tfrac{4}{9}\times\tfrac{15}{8}\). Multiply it out by cancelling across the top and bottom first. The \(4\) and the \(8\) share a factor of \(4\), and the \(15\) and the \(9\) share a factor of \(3\): $$\frac{4}{9}\times\frac{15}{8}=\frac{\cancel{4}^{\,1}}{\cancel{9}^{\,3}}\times\frac{\cancel{15}^{\,5}}{\cancel{8}^{\,2}}=\frac{1\times 5}{3\times 2}=\frac{5}{6}.$$ So the two settings really are one setting, multiply by \(\tfrac{5}{6}\), and the student was right. Now feed in the part of size \(12\): $$12\times\frac{5}{6}=\frac{12\times 5}{6}=\frac{60}{6}=10.$$ Notice what just happened. A multiply and a divide, two separate operations, folded into a single clean fraction \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) with no remainder left over and no decimal in sight. That is the quiet reason fraction arithmetic stays so tidy. Multiply fractions, divide fractions, chain them as long as you like, and the result is always just another fraction. You never escape the fractions. \(\boxed{10}\)

blocks.x: Percent = Latin per centum; "30 percent of" always meant \(\times\tfrac{30}{100}\). Egyptians used reciprocals for 3000+ years; the Rhind Papyrus is a long unit-fraction table.

review.x: \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of a parking lot is shaded; \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) of shaded spaces are reserved. What fraction of the whole lot is shaded-reserved? Write in lowest terms as \(a/b\).
hints: The word of is a multiply sign. You want \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), so set up \(\tfrac{2}{9}\times\tfrac{3}{4}\) and look for factors to cancel before you multiply.
A \(3\) on top and the \(9\) on the bottom share a factor of \(3\), and the \(2\) on top and the \(4\) on the bottom share a factor of \(2\). Cancel both, then multiply what is left.
review.sol: The phrase two ninths of three quarters is a multiplication, \(\tfrac{2}{9}\times\tfrac{3}{4}\). Before multiplying, hunt for shared factors across the tops and bottoms, the same cancelling trick from 4.2. The \(3\) on top and the \(9\) on the bottom share a \(3\), leaving \(1\) and \(3\). The \(2\) on top and the \(4\) on the bottom share a \(2\), leaving \(1\) and \(2\). Now multiply the trimmed tops and bottoms, $$\frac{2}{9}\times\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1\times 1}{3\times 2}=\frac{1}{6}.$$ You can check without cancelling, \(\tfrac{2\times 3}{9\times 4}=\tfrac{6}{36}=\tfrac{1}{6}\), the same point. So the shaded staff spaces make up \(\boxed{1/6}\) of the whole lot.

review.x: Three quarters of \(20\) students ride bikes. Split \(20\) into \(4\) equal groups, keep three. How many students ride bikes?
hints: The word of is the signal to multiply. You want \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of \(20\), so first break \(20\) into four equal groups.
Each of the four groups holds \(20 \div 4 = 5\) students. Three quarters means you keep three of those groups, so add up three fives.
review.sol: The word of tells you to multiply, so you want \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of \(20\). The quiet trick with a fraction of a whole is to handle the bottom number first. The denominator \(4\) says split the \(20\) into four equal groups, and \(20 \div 4 = 5\), so each quarter is \(5\) students. The numerator \(3\) says keep three of those quarters, which is \(3 \times 5 = 15\). You can also write it as one multiplication, \(\tfrac{3}{4} \times 20 = \tfrac{3 \times 20}{4} = \tfrac{60}{4} = 15\), and it lands in the same place. Either way, \(\boxed{15}\) students ride bikes.

review.x: Light through two panes: outer passes \(\tfrac{2}{3}\); inner passes \(\tfrac{6}{7}\). Cancel \(3\) and \(6\) first. What fraction gets through?
hints: Cancel before multiplying. The 6 in the numerator and the 3 in the denominator both divide by 3, turning 6 into 2 and 3 into 1.
After cancelling you have \(\tfrac{2}{1}\times\tfrac{2}{7}\). Multiply the tops and the bottoms straight across.
review.sol: Set up the product of the two pass-through fractions, \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\). Instead of multiplying right away, look for a shared factor across the diagonal. The \(6\) on top and the \(3\) on the bottom both divide by \(3\), so the \(6\) becomes \(2\) and the \(3\) becomes \(1\). That leaves \(\tfrac{2}{1}\times\tfrac{2}{7}\). Now multiply straight across, \(2\times 2 = 4\) on top and \(1\times 7 = 7\) on the bottom, giving \(\tfrac{4}{7}\). Since \(4\) and \(7\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), it is already in lowest terms. Cancelling first kept the numbers small and meant there was nothing left to reduce at the end. \(\boxed{4/7}\)

review.x: Each of \(8\) ornaments uses \(\tfrac{3}{10}\) m of wire. Total wire is \(8\times\tfrac{3}{10}\). How many meters are needed? Give as an improper fraction \(a/b\) in lowest terms.
hints: Eight copies of \(\tfrac{3}{10}\) means you scale the top of the fraction by 8 and keep the bottom the same. So the numerator becomes \(8\times 3\) and the denominator stays \(10\).
Once you have \(\tfrac{24}{10}\), look for a number that divides both 24 and 10. They are both even, so divide each by 2.
review.sol: Multiplying by 8 is the same as laying down 8 copies of \(\tfrac{3}{10}\) and adding them up. When you multiply a whole number by a fraction, you scale the numerator and leave the denominator alone, since the size of each piece does not change, only how many pieces you have. So \(8\times\tfrac{3}{10}=\tfrac{8\times 3}{10}=\tfrac{24}{10}\). Now reduce. Both 24 and 10 share a factor of 2, and \(\gcd(24,10)=2\), so divide top and bottom by 2 to get \(\tfrac{24}{10}=\tfrac{12}{5}\). The 8 ornaments use \(\boxed{12/5}\) meters of wire.

review.x: Compute \(\dfrac{9}{14}\times\dfrac{7}{12}\). Cancel \(7\) from \(7\) and \(14\), and \(3\) from \(9\) and \(12\). What fraction \(a/b\) in lowest terms do you get?
hints: Cancel across the two fractions, not just up and down inside one. Replace the \(7\) and \(14\) by \(1\) and \(2\), and replace the \(9\) and \(12\) by \(3\) and \(4\).
After both cancellations the top numbers are \(3\) and \(1\), and the bottom numbers are \(2\) and \(4\). Multiply straight across.
review.sol: Cancel the crossing pairs first. The \(7\) and the \(14\) share a \(7\), so they become \(1\) and \(2\). The \(9\) and the \(12\) share a \(3\), so they become \(3\) and \(4\). The problem turns into $$\frac{3}{2}\times\frac{1}{4}.$$ Now multiply straight across. The tops give \(3\times1=3\) and the bottoms give \(2\times4=8\), so the product is \(\tfrac{3}{8}\). Since \(\gcd(3,8)=1\), it is already in lowest terms. $$\boxed{3/8}$$

review.x: A jug is \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) full. You pour off \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) of the juice inside. The two \(5\)s cancel. What fraction of the full jug did you pour out?
hints: The word of tells you to multiply. Set up \(\tfrac{2}{5}\times\tfrac{5}{8}\) and look for a number on top that matches a number on the bottom.
A 5 sits in the bottom of the first fraction and in the top of the second. Cancel that pair to 1 first, then multiply the small numbers that are left.
review.sol: Pouring off \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) of what is in the jug, while \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) is in there, is \(\tfrac{2}{5}\times\tfrac{5}{8}\). Instead of multiplying straight across into \(\tfrac{10}{40}\) and reducing later, cancel first. The 5 on the bottom of \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) and the 5 on the top of \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) cancel to 1, leaving \(\tfrac{2}{1}\times\tfrac{1}{8}=\tfrac{2}{8}\). One more pair, the 2 against the 8, gives \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). So you poured out \(\boxed{1/4}\) of the full jug.

review.x: Chain: \(\tfrac{4}{5}\times\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\times\tfrac{7}{8}\). Most tops and bottoms cancel in pairs. What single fraction \(a/b\) does the chain equal?
hints: Multiply across without simplifying yet. The top becomes \(4\times5\times6\times7\) and the bottom becomes \(5\times6\times7\times8\). Now hunt for numbers that show up in both rows.
The \(5\), \(6\), and \(7\) appear on top and on bottom, so each of those pairs cancels to \(1\). After they vanish, only the very first top and the very last bottom are left standing.
review.sol: Write the product as one big fraction by collecting all the tops and all the bottoms. $$\frac{4}{5}\times\frac{5}{6}\times\frac{6}{7}\times\frac{7}{8}=\frac{4\times5\times6\times7}{5\times6\times7\times8}.$$ Now look down the two rows. The \(5\) on top cancels the \(5\) on the bottom, the \(6\) cancels the \(6\), and the \(7\) cancels the \(7\). Each scaling handed its new number straight to the next one, so every middle piece pairs off and disappears. $$\frac{4\times\cancel{5}\times\cancel{6}\times\cancel{7}}{\cancel{5}\times\cancel{6}\times\cancel{7}\times8}=\frac{4}{8}.$$ Only the first top, \(4\), and the last bottom, \(8\), survive. That is the telescoping idea, the chain collapses to first-top over last-bottom. Finally \(\dfrac{4}{8}\) reduces by dividing both parts by \(4\), giving \(\dfrac{1}{2}\). $$\boxed{1/2}$$

review.x: A stretch setting multiplies by \(\tfrac{9}{4}\). What single fraction \(a/b\) multiplies with \(\tfrac{9}{4}\) to give exactly \(1\)?
hints: Two fractions that multiply to \(1\) are called reciprocals. To build a reciprocal, you keep the same two numbers but swap which one is on top.
Flip \(\tfrac{9}{4}\) over so the \(4\) is on top and the \(9\) is on the bottom, then check that the product really is \(1\).
review.sol:
You want the number that multiplies with \(\tfrac{9}{4}\) to land on exactly \(1\). That partner is the reciprocal, and you find it by swapping the top and bottom. Flipping \(\tfrac{9}{4}\) gives \(\tfrac{4}{9}\).

Check it by multiplying straight across. \[\frac{9}{4}\times\frac{4}{9}=\frac{9\times 4}{4\times 9}=\frac{36}{36}=1.\] The product is \(1\), so the flip really does undo the stretch. The undo setting is \(\boxed{4/9}\).

review.x: A \(4\) L drum is emptied with a \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) L dispenser. How many full pumps empty the drum?
hints: You are asking how many \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) liter scoops fit inside \(4\) liters, which is the division \(4 \div \tfrac{1}{3}\).
Dividing by \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) is the same as multiplying by \(3\), so count \(3\) pumps in each of the \(4\) liters.
review.sol: The question is how many \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) liter pumps fit inside \(4\) liters, so we compute \(4 \div \tfrac{1}{3}\). Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal, and the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) is \(3\). So \(4 \div \tfrac{1}{3} = 4 \times 3 = 12\). You can also see it directly. One liter holds \(3\) of the small pumps, and there are \(4\) liters, which gives \(4 \times 3 = 12\). The drum empties after \(\boxed{12}\) full pumps.

review.x: A tank is \(\tfrac{7}{10}\) full. Each truck carries \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) of a full tank. How many truckloads does the current water fill? Give as \(a/b\) in lowest terms.
hints: You are asking how many half-tanks fit inside seven tenths of a tank, so this is \(\tfrac{7}{10} \div \tfrac{1}{2}\).
Dividing by \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) is the same as multiplying by its flip, \(\tfrac{2}{1}\).
review.sol:
The water sitting in the tank is \(\tfrac{7}{10}\) of a full tank, and each truck hauls away \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) of a full tank. Counting truckloads means counting how many half-tanks fit inside seven tenths, which is the division \(\tfrac{7}{10} \div \tfrac{1}{2}\).

To divide by a fraction, flip it and multiply. The flip of \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) is \(\tfrac{2}{1}\), so \(\tfrac{7}{10} \div \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{7}{10} \times \tfrac{2}{1}\).

Multiply straight across to get \(\tfrac{7 \times 2}{10 \times 1} = \tfrac{14}{10}\). Both numbers share a factor of \(2\), and dividing top and bottom by \(2\) gives \(\tfrac{7}{5}\).

That makes sense, since a bit more than one full half-tank should come out, and \(\tfrac{7}{5}\) is just past \(1\).

$$\boxed{7/5}$$

review.x: Tray A holds \(\tfrac{10}{21}\) of a ream; Tray B holds \(\tfrac{5}{14}\). Compute \(\tfrac{10}{21}\div\tfrac{5}{14}\) and give the result as \(a/b\) in lowest terms.
hints: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal. Flip \(\tfrac{5}{14}\) to get \(\tfrac{14}{5}\), then multiply \(\tfrac{10}{21}\times\tfrac{14}{5}\).
Before you multiply across, cancel. The \(10\) and the \(5\) share a factor of \(5\), and the \(14\) and the \(21\) share a factor of \(7\).
review.sol: Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal, so flip the divisor. \(\tfrac{10}{21}\div\tfrac{5}{14}=\tfrac{10}{21}\times\tfrac{14}{5}\). Now cancel before multiplying. The \(10\) on top and the \(5\) on the bottom share a factor of \(5\), which turns them into \(2\) and \(1\). The \(14\) on top and the \(21\) on the bottom share a factor of \(7\), which turns them into \(2\) and \(3\). That leaves \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{2}{1}=\tfrac{4}{3}\). Multiplying straight across without canceling gives \(\tfrac{140}{105}\), and since \(\gcd(140,105)=35\), this also reduces to \(\tfrac{4}{3}\), so both paths agree. $$\boxed{4/3}$$

review.x: Priya is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) through her novel, which is \(27\) pages. Since \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) of the whole \(=27\), multiply \(27\) by \(\tfrac{5}{3}\). How many pages total?
hints: The phrase three fifths of the whole book is a multiplication. So \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) times the unknown total equals \(27\). To get the total back by itself, multiply both sides by the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{3}{5}\).
The reciprocal of \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) is \(\tfrac{5}{3}\), so compute \(27\times\tfrac{5}{3}\). Notice the \(3\) on the bottom divides evenly into the \(27\) on top before you multiply.
review.sol: The clue is the word of. Three fifths of the whole book is the same as \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) times the total, and that product is the 27 pages Priya has read. So the total is whatever number, when shrunk to three fifths of itself, lands on 27. To undo multiplying by \(\tfrac{3}{5}\), multiply by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{5}{3}\). $$27\times\frac{5}{3}$$ Write \(27\) as \(\tfrac{27}{1}\) and cancel before multiplying. The \(3\) in the bottom divides the \(27\) on top, since \(27\div 3=9\). That leaves \(9\times 5=45\). A quick sanity check: three fifths of 45 is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\times 45 = 27\), which matches her journal. The whole book is \(\boxed{45}\) pages long.

review.x: A spool holds \(\tfrac{15}{4}\) m; each bow needs \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) m. Compute \(\tfrac{15}{4}\div\tfrac{3}{8}\). How many whole bows does the ribbon make?
hints: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its flip. Turn \(\div\tfrac{3}{8}\) into \(\times\tfrac{8}{3}\) and keep \(\tfrac{15}{4}\) as it is.
Before you multiply across, look for shared factors. The \(8\) on top and the \(4\) on the bottom share a \(4\), and the \(15\) on top and the \(3\) on the bottom share a \(3\).
review.sol:
Start by rewriting the division as multiplication by the reciprocal. Flip \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) to \(\tfrac{8}{3}\):
$$\frac{15}{4}\div\frac{3}{8}=\frac{15}{4}\times\frac{8}{3}.$$
Now cancel before multiplying. The \(8\) and the \(4\) share a factor of \(4\), so they become \(2\) and \(1\). The \(15\) and the \(3\) share a factor of \(3\), so they become \(5\) and \(1\):
$$\frac{15}{4}\times\frac{8}{3}=\frac{5}{1}\times\frac{2}{1}=10.$$
The quotient is a whole number, which means the ribbon divides evenly with nothing left over. So you can tie exactly \(\boxed{10}\) bows.

review.x: Five eighths of a \(24\) g block is measured out and split into jars of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) g each. How many jars does the merchant fill?
hints: First find how much saffron she measures out. Take \(24\) grams and multiply by \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). That single number is the total weight you will be splitting into jars.

Now you have a total weight, and each jar holds \(\tfrac34\) gram. Splitting into equal portions is division, so divide your total by \(\tfrac34\). Dividing by \(\tfrac34\) is the same as multiplying by \(\tfrac43\).
review.sol:
This problem hides two steps, a multiply and a divide, inside one story. Take them in order.

First, how much saffron does she measure out? That is five eighths of \(24\) grams, so \(24 \times \tfrac{5}{8}\). Since \(24 \div 8 = 3\), this is \(3 \times 5 = 15\) grams.

Now she splits those \(15\) grams into jars holding \(\tfrac34\) gram each. Asking how many \(\tfrac34\) gram scoops fit into \(15\) grams is a division, \(15 \div \tfrac34\). Flip the divisor and multiply, so \(15 \times \tfrac43 = \tfrac{60}{3} = 20\).

The two operations pull in opposite directions, multiplying by a fraction less than \(1\) shrank the amount, then dividing by a fraction less than \(1\) grew the count back up. She fills \(\boxed{20}\) jars.

review.x: Three filters: \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\times\tfrac{7}{8}\). The chain telescopes. Divide that result by \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). What single number is the answer?
hints: Tackle the three filters first. Write \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\times\tfrac{7}{8}\) as one big fraction with everything on top and everything on the bottom, then look for matching numbers to cancel before you multiply anything out.

Dividing by \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) is the same as multiplying by \(\tfrac{8}{5}\). Once you flip it, line up every number on top against every number on the bottom and cancel pair by pair. If nothing is left, the value is \(1\).
review.sol: Start with the three filters. Multiplying straight across, \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\times\tfrac{7}{8}=\dfrac{5\times 6\times 7}{6\times 7\times 8}\). The \(6\) and the \(7\) each appear once on top and once on the bottom, so they cancel and leave \(\dfrac{5}{8}\). This is a telescoping chain, where each filter's bottom is swallowed by the next filter's top. Now divide by the baseline. Dividing by \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) means multiplying by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{8}{5}\), so we have \(\dfrac{5}{8}\times\dfrac{8}{5}=\dfrac{5\times 8}{8\times 5}\). The \(5\) cancels with the \(5\) and the \(8\) cancels with the \(8\), so nothing is left but the number itself. Any nonzero fraction times its own reciprocal is \(1\), which makes sense because the surviving signal turned out to be exactly the baseline. $$\boxed{1}$$

blocks.x: A researcher writes a fox count as \(\tfrac{84}{120}\), honest but bulky, while a teammate records his night over a denominator of \(45\). Two troubles are tangled here. One single fraction wants its smallest, cleanest name, and two fractions sliced into different-sized pieces need a shared unit before they can be compared. Both are the same Chapter 3 idea aimed in opposite directions.
The fuel reading is \(\tfrac{84}{120}\). Find \(\gcd(84,120)\), divide top and bottom by it in one move. Write \(\tfrac{84}{120}\) in simplest form as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: The whole trick is one number, the \(\gcd\) of \(84\) and \(120\). One way to find it is to list the prime factors of each and collect every prime they both carry. \(84 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 7\) and \(120 = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5\). Which primes show up in both, and how many times?

Both numbers carry two \(2\)s and one \(3\), so \(\gcd(84,120) = 2 \times 2 \times 3 = 12\). Now make the single move, divide the top by \(12\) and the bottom by \(12\). What is \(84 \div 12\) over \(120 \div 12\)?
blocks.sol: First find the biggest factor the top and bottom share. Break each number into primes. \(84 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 7\) and \(120 = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5\). The primes they both carry are two \(2\)s and one \(3\), so the \(\gcd\) is \(2 \times 2 \times 3 = 12\). That \(12\) is the single biggest chunk hiding in both numbers. Now divide top and bottom by it in one clean move. \(84 \div 12 = 7\) and \(120 \div 12 = 10\), which gives \(\tfrac{7}{10}\). Check that we are truly done. The new top is \(7\), a prime, and \(10 = 2 \times 5\) shares none of its factors, so the only thing they have in common is \(1\). The fraction is in simplest form, and we got there in one step instead of three. $$\boxed{7/10}$$

blocks.x: Simplest form via the gcd. A fraction is in simplest form when the only number its top and bottom still share is \(1\), since there is nothing bigger left to divide out. Dividing out a shared factor is always fair, because that factor sits on the top and the bottom at once, so cutting it from both scales them by the same amount and leaves the point untouched. The quickest path is to find the single biggest thing the top and bottom share, their \(\gcd\), and divide both by it one time. That one cut sweeps out every shared factor at once, because the biggest already carried all the smaller ones along with it.
