blocks.cap: Twelve is \(2\times 2\times 3\) and eighteen is \(2\times 3\times 3\). They share one \(2\) and one \(3\), and that shared \(6\) is the gcd. Cancelling it from top and bottom leaves \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).

blocks.x: Divide \(\tfrac{9}{20}\div\tfrac{3}{8}=\tfrac{9}{20}\times\tfrac{8}{3}\). Cancel before multiplying. Write the result as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) in simplest form.
hints: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal, so rewrite \(\tfrac{9}{20}\div\tfrac{3}{8}\) as \(\tfrac{9}{20}\times\tfrac{8}{3}\). Once it is a multiplication, any top number can cancel with any bottom number.

Cancel before multiplying. Replace the \(9\) and \(3\) with \(3\) and \(1\), since both lose a factor of \(3\). Replace the \(8\) and \(20\) with \(2\) and \(5\), since both lose a factor of \(4\). Now multiply the new tops and the new bottoms straight across.
blocks.sol: Start by turning the division into a multiplication. Dividing by \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{8}{3}\), so \(\tfrac{9}{20}\div\tfrac{3}{8}=\tfrac{9}{20}\times\tfrac{8}{3}\). The flip is what makes the simplifying easy, because in a multiplication any top factor is free to cancel with any bottom factor. Look across the two fractions. The \(9\) on top and the \(3\) on a bottom both carry a factor of \(3\), so divide each by \(3\) to get \(3\) and \(1\). The \(8\) on top and the \(20\) on a bottom both carry a factor of \(4\), so divide each by \(4\) to get \(2\) and \(5\). The product is now \(\tfrac{3}{5}\times\tfrac{2}{1}\). Multiply straight across, \(3\times2=6\) on top and \(5\times1=5\) on the bottom, which gives \(\tfrac{6}{5}\). Since \(6\) and \(5\) share no common factor, it is already in simplest form. So a little over one small batch fits, \(\tfrac{6}{5}\) of a batch to be exact. \(\boxed{6/5}\)

blocks.x: Evaluate \(\tfrac{3}{4}\times\tfrac{4}{5}\times\cdots\times\tfrac{8}{9}\). Every interior bottom is the next top and cancels. Write the collapsed result as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) in simplest form.
hints: You do not have to multiply this out. Write the whole product as one giant fraction, with all six tops on top and all six bottoms underneath. Now hunt for matching numbers. Does anything on top also appear on the bottom?

Every middle number shows up once on top and once on the bottom, so each of those pairs cancels to \(1\). Cross out the \(4\), \(5\), \(6\), \(7\), and \(8\) wherever they appear. The only top with no matching bottom is the very first \(3\), and the only bottom with no matching top is the very last \(9\). What single fraction is left?
blocks.sol: Stack all the tops over all the bottoms in one fraction instead of multiplying step by step. That gives $$\frac{3\times4\times5\times6\times7\times8}{4\times5\times6\times7\times8\times9}.$$ Now compare the two rows. The top runs \(3,4,5,6,7,8\). The bottom runs \(4,5,6,7,8,9\). Every number from \(4\) up to \(8\) appears once on top and once on the bottom, and any number divided by itself is \(1\). So each of those pairs quietly cancels, the \(4\) with the \(4\), the \(5\) with the \(5\), and so on through the \(8\). The cancellation cascades through the whole chain from the inside out. When the dust settles, the only top with no partner is the \(3\) that started the chain, and the only bottom with no partner is the \(9\) that ended it. Everything between them has folded shut. What remains is \(\tfrac{3}{9}\), and since \(3\) and \(9\) share a factor of \(3\), that simplifies to \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). The relay covers exactly a third of the first runner's reference leg. \(\boxed{\dfrac{1}{3}}\)

blocks.x: Cancel first, multiply second. Whenever fractions get multiplied, or divided once you have flipped the second one, every top is about to pile onto one big top and every bottom onto one big bottom. So the smart move is never to multiply across and then clean up the mess. Scan each top against every bottom, find every shared factor no matter which fraction it came from, and lift it off both floors before you multiply a single thing. Do that and the answer is born already in simplest form, with no reducing step waiting at the end and no giant numbers to wrestle in the middle.
Egyptians wrote every fraction as distinct unit fractions: \(\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{4}\). The Rhind Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) tabulates these splits.
\(1{,}485=3^3\times 5\times 11\) and \(1{,}890=2\times 3^3\times 5\times 7\). Cancel shared primes, read what remains. Write \(\tfrac{1{,}485}{1{,}890}\) in simplest form as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: Line the two prime lists up factor by factor. Both the top and the bottom carry three \(3\)s and one \(5\). Those are exactly the primes you are allowed to cross off, one from the top for each one you cross off the bottom.

Strike the shared \(3\times 3\times 3\times 5\) from both floors. On top you are left with just \(11\). On the bottom you are left with \(2\times 7\). Multiply each leftover pile back up and write the fraction.
blocks.sol: Stack the prime factorizations so matching primes sit above each other. The top is \(1{,}485 = 3\times 3\times 3\times 5\times 11\) and the bottom is \(1{,}890 = 2\times 3\times 3\times 3\times 5\times 7\). The shared primes are the ones in both lists, namely \(3\times 3\times 3\) and a single \(5\). Cancel each shared prime from the top against its twin on the bottom. $$\frac{1{,}485}{1{,}890}=\frac{\cancel{3}\times \cancel{3}\times \cancel{3}\times \cancel{5}\times 11}{2\times \cancel{3}\times \cancel{3}\times \cancel{3}\times \cancel{5}\times 7}=\frac{11}{2\times 7}$$ Whatever survives on top is the new numerator and whatever survives on the bottom is the new denominator. On top only \(11\) is left, and on the bottom \(2\times 7 = 14\) is left. Since \(11\) is prime and shares no factor with \(14\), the fraction cannot shrink any further. The slow part, spotting that the hidden \(\gcd\) was \(3\times 3\times 3\times 5 = 135\), became automatic once the prime trees laid every common factor side by side. $$\boxed{\dfrac{11}{14}}$$

blocks.x: Simplify \(\dfrac{k^4\times 10}{k^2\times 15}\): cancel two \(k\)s and the shared factor \(5\). Result is \(\dfrac{2k^2}{3}\). Now set \(k=3\). What single whole number does the fraction equal?
hints: You already simplified to \(\frac{2k^2}{3}\). Now \(k^2\) just means \(k\times k\), so with \(k=3\) that piece is \(3\times 3\). Build the top first, then divide by \(3\).
With \(k=3\), \(k^2=9\). The top is \(2\times 9=18\). Then \(\frac{18}{3}\) is the final number. Divide it out.
blocks.sol: Start by counting copies of each factor instead of multiplying everything out. The top holds four \(k\)s and the bottom holds two \(k\)s. Every \(k\) on the bottom can pair off with a \(k\) on the top and cancel, since a factor over the same factor is just \(1\). Two pairs cancel, which uses up both bottom \(k\)s and two of the top \(k\)s, leaving \(k\times k\) upstairs and a clean \(1\) downstairs from those letters. Next handle the plain numbers. \(\frac{10}{15}\) shares a factor of \(5\), and dividing both by \(5\) gives \(\frac{2}{3}\). Put the two pieces together and the fraction is \(\frac{2\times k\times k}{3}\), that is \(\frac{2k^2}{3}\). All of that was done without knowing \(k\) at all. Now plug in \(k=3\). Then \(k^2=3\times 3=9\), the top becomes \(2\times 9=18\), and \(\frac{18}{3}=6\). $$\boxed{6}$$

blocks.x: Simplify \(\dfrac{a^4 b^2}{a^2 b^5}\): cancel \(a\)s and \(b\)s in pairs. Then set \(a=3\) and \(b=2\). What single fraction in simplest form does the whole expression equal?
hints: The two letters never mix, so deal with them separately. There are four \(a\)s on top and two on the bottom, so two \(a\)s upstairs survive. There are two \(b\)s on top and five on the bottom, so three \(b\)s downstairs survive. Write what is left as a tidy fraction before you put numbers in.

After cancelling, you are left with \(\dfrac{a\times a}{b\times b\times b}\), which is \(\dfrac{a^2}{b^3}\). Now \(a=3\) gives \(a^2=3\times 3=9\), and \(b=2\) gives \(b^3=2\times 2\times 2=8\). Put the top over the bottom.
blocks.sol: Treat each letter as its own little pile and never let them mix. Start with the \(a\)s. There are four of them on top and two on the bottom. Two of those top \(a\)s pair off with the two bottom \(a\)s and cancel, leaving \(4-2=2\) extra \(a\)s upstairs. Now the \(b\)s. There are two on top and five on the bottom. The two top \(b\)s cancel with two of the bottom ones, leaving \(5-2=3\) extra \(b\)s downstairs. Nothing else is left to cancel, so the simplified fraction is $$\frac{a\times a}{b\times b\times b}=\frac{a^2}{b^3}.$$ This is already in simplest form as far as the letters go, and notice it stayed a fraction, with the \(a\)s on top and the \(b\)s on the bottom. Now make it a number. With \(a=3\), the top is \(a^2=3\times 3=9\). With \(b=2\), the bottom is \(b^3=2\times 2\times 2=8\). So the value is \(\dfrac{9}{8}\). Since \(9\) and \(8\) share no common factor, this is already simplest form, and it really is a fraction, not a whole number. $$\boxed{9/8}$$

blocks.x: Cancelling powers. A power is just a tally of copies. For each kind of factor, count how many copies sit on top and how many on the bottom, then strike them off in pairs, one from upstairs against one from downstairs, until one side runs out. Whatever copies are left stay on the side that started with more, and that is the simplified answer. A letter plays by the exact same rules, since it is only a factor you have not put a number to yet, so cancel the matching copies first and fill in its value at the very end.
Panels generated \(168\) kWh; building used \(480\) kWh. Divide \(\gcd(168,480)\) into both top and bottom once. Write \(\tfrac{168}{480}\) in simplest form as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: You only need one division to finish, but it has to be division by the right number. Ask what the largest number is that goes evenly into both \(168\) and \(480\). Find that \(\gcd\) first, then divide once.

Break each number into prime factors. \(168 = 2^3 \cdot 3 \cdot 7\) and \(480 = 2^5 \cdot 3 \cdot 5\). The shared part is \(2^3 \cdot 3 = 24\), so the \(\gcd\) is \(24\). Now divide both \(168\) and \(480\) by \(24\) and read off the answer.
blocks.sol:
The whole job is to write \(\tfrac{168}{480}\) so that nothing more cancels. The fastest honest way is to find the largest number that divides both, the \(\gcd\), and divide by it once.

Factor both numbers into primes. We get \(168 = 2^3 \cdot 3 \cdot 7\) and \(480 = 2^5 \cdot 3 \cdot 5\). The factors they have in common are three twos and one three, so \(\gcd(168, 480) = 2^3 \cdot 3 = 24\).

Now divide top and bottom by \(24\) in one move. \(168 \div 24 = 7\) and \(480 \div 24 = 20\), which gives \(\tfrac{7}{20}\). Since \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(20\), nothing is left to cancel, so this really is simplest form.

The caretaker can post that the panels supplied \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) of the building's electricity, a little over a third of the day's power. \(\boxed{\tfrac{7}{20}}\)

blocks.x: Simplest form is the smallest private denominator. Every reducing problem is secretly the same move. You take one fraction and squeeze its denominator down as small as it can go, and the tool that does the squeezing is always the \(\gcd\), because pulling out the biggest factor the top and bottom share is exactly what drops the denominator as low as it will drop. What you land on is that fraction's smallest private name, the tidiest denominator it can wear all on its own. Hold onto that word, private, because a simplest form belongs to that one fraction and nobody else, which is why it acts like a name tag.

Simplest form gives one fraction its private name. But \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) are both already simplest, and you still cannot say which is larger, because sixths and eighths are different-sized cuts and you cannot count one kind against the other. Here the clever move flips. Instead of shrinking each denominator toward its private name, we grow them until they agree on one shared size of piece.
The lcd of \(6\) and \(8\) is \(24\). Eighths \(\times 3\): \(\tfrac{7}{8}=\tfrac{21}{24}\). Do the same for \(\tfrac{5}{6}\). What does \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) become over \(24\)?
hints: You want to rebuild \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) so the bottom reads \(24\) instead of \(6\). Ask yourself what you multiply \(6\) by to land on \(24\), then remember the equivalent fraction rule says the top has to get the exact same treatment.
Since \(6\times 4=24\), the multiplier is \(4\). Multiply the top by \(4\) as well, so \(5\times 4=20\), and the rebuilt fraction is \(\tfrac{20}{24}\).
blocks.sol: The goal is to keep the same amount of ribbon but describe it in twenty fourths instead of sixths. First figure out the multiplier that turns the denominator \(6\) into \(24\). Since \(6\times 4=24\), the multiplier is \(4\). The equivalent fraction move says whatever you do to the bottom you must do to the top, so multiply the numerator by \(4\) too. That gives \(5\times 4=20\) on top, and the rebuilt fraction is \(\tfrac{20}{24}\). A quick gut check confirms it is the same amount, because \(\tfrac{20}{24}\) just means twenty of the little pieces while \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) meant five of the big pieces, and each big piece was split into \(4\) little ones. Both ribbons are now measured in matching twenty fourths, \(\tfrac{20}{24}\) and \(\tfrac{21}{24}\), so they are finally ready to count side by side. $$\tfrac{5}{6}=\boxed{20/24}$$

blocks.x: To add \(\tfrac{1}{6}+\tfrac{4}{9}\) find a shared denominator. \(6\times 9=54\) works but overshoots; \(6\) and \(9\) share \(3\). What is \(\operatorname{lcm}(6,9)\)?
hints: You are hunting for the smallest number that both \(6\) and \(9\) divide into evenly. Try walking up the multiples of the bigger one, \(9, 18, 27, \dots\), and stop at the first that \(6\) also divides.
List the multiples of \(9\): \(9, 18, 27\). Is \(9\) a multiple of \(6\)? No. Is \(18\) a multiple of \(6\)? Yes, \(18=6\times 3\). That first match is your answer.
blocks.sol: The product \(6\times 9=54\) works, but it overshoots because \(6\) and \(9\) overlap. Both carry a factor of \(3\), so multiplying them counts that \(3\) twice. The lcm fixes this by counting each prime only as many times as it is truly needed. Break each denominator into primes, \(6=2\times 3\) and \(9=3\times 3\). To be a multiple of \(6\) you need one \(2\) and one \(3\). To be a multiple of \(9\) you need two \(3\)s. So the smallest number that covers both needs one \(2\) and two \(3\)s, which is \(2\times 3\times 3=18\). You can check it the plain way too. The multiples of \(9\) are \(9, 18, 27, \dots\), and the very first one that \(6\) also divides is \(18\), since \(18=6\times 3\). Notice \(18\) is exactly \(\tfrac{54}{3}\), smaller than the product by the shared factor of \(3\). The least common denominator of \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{4}{9}\) is \(\operatorname{lcm}(6,9)=\boxed{18}\).

blocks.x: Least common denominator (lcd). To give two fractions a common denominator, find a number that every denominator divides into evenly, a shared multiple, and rewrite each fraction over it as an equivalent fraction. Any shared multiple works, and just multiplying the denominators together always hands you one, but it is usually bigger and clumsier than it needs to be. The smallest shared multiple, the \(\operatorname{lcm}\) of the denominators, keeps the numbers as gentle as possible, and that one earns its own name, the least common denominator. Where simplest form reached for the \(\gcd\), a common denominator reaches for the \(\operatorname{lcm}\).

blocks.cap: Seven tenths and four fifteenths have different-size pieces. Re-slicing both to the LCD \(30\) makes the pieces match: \(\tfrac{7}{10}=\tfrac{21}{30}\) and \(\tfrac{4}{15}=\tfrac{8}{30}\).

blocks.x: \(\operatorname{lcm}(4,6,9)\): primes are \(2^2\) and \(3^2\). Multiply the highest powers. What is the lcd of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), \(\tfrac{5}{6}\), \(\tfrac{7}{9}\)?
hints: Line up the prime factorizations, \(4 = 2^2\), \(6 = 2 \times 3\), \(9 = 3^2\). The lcd has to be divisible by every one of them, so it needs enough \(2\)s for the \(4\) and enough \(3\)s for the \(9\) at the same time.
Take the highest power of each prime seen anywhere. The biggest stack of \(2\)s is \(2^2\) and the biggest stack of \(3\)s is \(3^2\). The lcd is \(2^2 \times 3^2 = 4 \times 9\). Just multiply.
blocks.sol: Write each denominator as primes so nothing stays hidden, \(4 = 2^2\), \(6 = 2^1 \times 3^1\), and \(9 = 3^2\). The least common denominator must be a number that \(4\), \(6\), and \(9\) all divide into evenly, so it must carry every prime that shows up, and carry enough copies to cover the greediest denominator for that prime. For the prime \(2\), the greediest is the \(4\) with two of them, so we need \(2^2\). For the prime \(3\), the greediest is the \(9\) with two of them, so we need \(3^2\). Take the highest power of each and multiply, \(2^2 \times 3^2 = 4 \times 9 = 36\). Check that all three fit, \(36 \div 4 = 9\), \(36 \div 6 = 6\), and \(36 \div 9 = 4\), all whole. And \(36\) really is the smallest such number, because dropping even one factor would leave some denominator unable to divide in. The least common denominator is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: lcd is \(36\). \(\tfrac{1}{4}\to\tfrac{9}{36}\). \(\tfrac{7}{9}\to\tfrac{28}{36}\). What does \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) become over \(36\)?
hints: Ask the bottom first. What times \(6\) lands on \(36\)? Whatever that number is, the top has to ride along with the exact same multiplier.
The bottom \(6\) needs \(\times 6\) to become \(36\), so multiply the top by \(6\) too. That is \(5\times 6\) over \(6\times 6\).
blocks.sol: Start with the bottom, since that is what decides the multiplier. You want \(6\) to become \(36\), and \(6\times 6 = 36\), so the scaling factor here is \(6\). The whole point of building up a fraction is that the top must climb by the same factor, otherwise you would be changing the value instead of just renaming it. So multiply the top by \(6\) as well, \(5\times 6 = 30\). Putting them together, \(\tfrac{5}{6} = \tfrac{5\times 6}{6\times 6} = \tfrac{30}{36}\). Notice this is the exact same rhythm you used for \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{9}\), look at what the bottom needs, then copy that move onto the top. Three fractions now share the floor \(36\), reading \(\tfrac{9}{36}\), \(\tfrac{30}{36}\), and \(\tfrac{28}{36}\), all ready to be compared or combined. The one you were asked for is \(\boxed{30/36}\).

blocks.x: \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) vs \(\tfrac{7}{12}\). lcd is \(36\). Build each to \(36\) and compare tops. Which is larger? Write it as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: The two fractions are hard to compare because they count different-sized pieces. Rewrite each one over \(36\). What do you multiply the top and bottom of \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) by to land on \(36\)? What about \(\tfrac{7}{12}\)?

Since \(9 \times 4 = 36\), \(\tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{20}{36}\). Since \(12 \times 3 = 36\), \(\tfrac{7}{12} = \tfrac{21}{36}\). Now both count thirty-sixths, so the one with more of them wins. Compare \(20\) and \(21\).
blocks.sol: As long as the denominators differ, the numerators are counting pieces of different sizes, so \(5\) ninths against \(7\) twelfths is not a fair race. Make the pieces match first. The least common denominator of \(9\) and \(12\) is \(36\). Grow \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(4\), which gives \(\tfrac{5 \times 4}{9 \times 4} = \tfrac{20}{36}\). Grow \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(3\), which gives \(\tfrac{7 \times 3}{12 \times 3} = \tfrac{21}{36}\). Now both are stacks of thirty-sixths, identical in size, so whichever has more pieces is the bigger share. Since \(21 > 20\), the second claim wins, and the comparison that was invisible at the start is now obvious. The larger fraction is \(\boxed{\tfrac{7}{12}}\).

blocks.x: Growing to a shared denominator. To set two fractions side by side, pick a number that every bottom divides into, the smallest being their \(\operatorname{lcm}\), and rebuild each fraction over it by scaling the top by exactly the amount its own bottom was stretched. Once that is done, every fraction is sliced into pieces of one identical size, and pieces of the same size can finally be counted straight against each other, so the larger share jumps out. Growing the denominators never moves a single amount, the same way shrinking them never did. It only hands the fractions a shared set of names so they can speak to each other at last.

<svg viewBox="0 0 520 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="The point three quarters of the way from 0 to 1 has equal names three quarters, six eighths, nine twelfths, and seventy-five hundredths."> <defs><filter id="sh2" x="-30%" y="-30%" width="160%" height="160%"><feDropShadow dx="0" dy="5" stdDeviation="6" flood-color="#1f3a66" flood-opacity="0.14"/></filter></defs> <!-- names callout --> <rect x="120" y="26" width="300" height="50" rx="14" fill="#fff" stroke="#dbe6fa" filter="url(#sh2)"/> <text x="270" y="57" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="19" font-weight="700" fill="#2257c5">3/4 = 6/8 = 9/12 = 75/100</text> <!-- pointer down to the point --> <line x1="367" y1="78" x2="367" y2="146" stroke="#bcd0ea" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="2 4"/> <path d="M 367 152 l -6 -8 l 12 0 z" fill="#e0a52a"/> <!-- number line --> <line x1="60" y1="170" x2="470" y2="170" stroke="#9aa6b6" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linecap="round"/> <!-- quarter ticks --> <g stroke="#9aa6b6" stroke-width="2"> <line x1="60" y1="162" x2="60" y2="178"/><line x1="162" y1="164" x2="162" y2="176"/><line x1="265" y1="164" x2="265" y2="176"/><line x1="470" y1="162" x2="470" y2="178"/> </g> <!-- the 3/4 tick highlighted --> <line x1="367" y1="158" x2="367" y2="182" stroke="#e0a52a" stroke-width="3"/> <circle cx="367" cy="170" r="8" fill="#fcd76a" stroke="#e0a52a" stroke-width="2"/> <!-- labels --> <text x="60" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#475569">0</text> <text x="470" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#475569">1</text> <text x="367" y="204" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#8a5a08">3/4</text> </svg>
blocks.cap: The single point three quarters of the way to \(1\) answers to \(\tfrac34,\tfrac68,\tfrac{9}{12},\) and \(\tfrac{75}{100}\) alike. Multiplying top and bottom by the same number renames it without moving it.

blocks.x: Shrink versus grow. The right denominator was never a single number, it was a direction. When a fraction works alone and just wants its cleanest name, you shrink it, dividing top and bottom by their \(\gcd\) until the denominator drops to the smallest private name it can carry. When fractions need to meet, to be compared or added, you grow them instead, lifting each to the \(\operatorname{lcm}\) of the denominators where they settle on the smallest shared name. Two tools, the \(\gcd\) and the \(\operatorname{lcm}\), pulling in opposite directions, both from Chapter 3, yet both reaching for the easiest denominator the moment asks for.

review.x: A contact sheet has \(24\) thumbnails; \(18\) are keepers. Reduce \(\tfrac{18}{24}\) by dividing by \(\gcd(18,24)\). Write the answer as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: Reducing in one step means finding the largest number that divides both \(18\) and \(24\). List what divides each and look for the biggest one they share.
The divisors \(18\) and \(24\) share are \(1, 2, 3, 6\), so the greatest is \(\gcd(18,24)=6\). Divide both the top and the bottom by \(6\).
review.sol: You could chip away at this fraction in stages, dividing by \(2\) and then by \(3\), but the cleanest path is to find the biggest factor the numerator and denominator share and remove it all at once. That factor is the \(\gcd\). The numbers that divide \(18\) are \(1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18\), and the numbers that divide \(24\) are \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\). The largest one in both lists is \(6\), so \(\gcd(18,24)=6\). Now divide the top and the bottom by \(6\) in a single step. $$\frac{18}{24}=\frac{18\div 6}{24\div 6}=\frac{3}{4}$$ Since \(3\) and \(4\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), the fraction cannot shrink any further. So \(18\) keepers out of \(24\) thumbnails is exactly \(\boxed{3/4}\) of the sheet, which means three of every four pictures made the cut.

review.x: A big batch uses \(360\) g of one ingredient per \(504\) g of another. Simplify \(\tfrac{360}{504}\) to simplest form and write as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: Knocking off small factors one at a time works, but it is slow with numbers this size. The shortcut is to find \(\gcd(360,504)\) and divide both numbers by it in a single move.

Use the subtract-and-repeat idea. Since \(504-360=144\), the gcd of \(360\) and \(504\) is the same as the gcd of \(360\) and \(144\). Keep going until the two numbers agree, then divide top and bottom by that shared value.
review.sol: The slow road would be to peel off factors of \(2\) and \(3\) over and over. The fast road is to find the greatest common divisor once and divide both numbers by it together. To get \(\gcd(360,504)\), I chase the difference. Since \(504-360=144\), the gcd of \(360\) and \(504\) equals the gcd of \(360\) and \(144\). Then \(360-2\cdot 144 = 72\), so it equals the gcd of \(144\) and \(72\), and \(72\) divides \(144\) evenly, so \(\gcd(360,504)=72\). Now divide top and bottom by \(72\). We get \(360\div 72 = 5\) and \(504\div 72 = 7\). Since \(5\) and \(7\) share no common factor, the fraction is fully reduced. \(\tfrac{360}{504}=\tfrac{5}{7}\), so the answer is \(\boxed{5/7}\).

review.x: Compute \(\tfrac{8}{21}\times\tfrac{7}{12}\). Cancel shared factors across tops and bottoms before multiplying. Write the product as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) in simplest form.
hints: You do not have to multiply first and simplify later. Look diagonally. The 7 on top of the second fraction shares a factor with the 21 on the bottom of the first, and the 8 on top of the first shares a factor with the 12 on the bottom of the second.
Cancel 7 against 21 to leave a 3 underneath, and cancel 4 out of both 8 and 12 to leave 2 over 3. Now the only numbers left are a small product on top and a small product on the bottom.
review.sol: Write the product as one big fraction, \(\tfrac{8 \times 7}{21 \times 12}\), and hunt for shared factors before you multiply anything out. The \(7\) on top and the \(21\) on the bottom share a factor of \(7\), since \(21 = 3 \times 7\). Cancelling that turns the \(7\) into \(1\) and the \(21\) into \(3\). Next, the \(8\) on top and the \(12\) on the bottom share a factor of \(4\), since \(8 = 4 \times 2\) and \(12 = 4 \times 3\). Cancelling that leaves a \(2\) on top and a \(3\) on the bottom. Now the leftovers are \(\tfrac{2 \times 1}{3 \times 3} = \tfrac{2}{9}\). Because every common factor was already removed, the fraction is in lowest terms with nothing left to simplify, so the product is \(\boxed{2/9}\).

review.x: Divide \(\tfrac{15}{16}\div\tfrac{25}{8}\). Flip the divisor, cancel, then multiply. Write the quotient as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) in simplest form.
hints: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal. Flip \(\tfrac{25}{8}\) to \(\tfrac{8}{25}\), so the problem becomes \(\tfrac{15}{16}\times\tfrac{8}{25}\).

Before multiplying straight across, look for shared factors you can cancel. The \(8\) and the \(16\) share a factor, and the \(15\) and the \(25\) share a factor. Cancel those first to keep the numbers small.
review.sol: Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal, so flip the divisor. \(\tfrac{15}{16}\div\tfrac{25}{8}=\tfrac{15}{16}\times\tfrac{8}{25}\). Now cancel across before multiplying. The \(8\) on top and the \(16\) on the bottom share a factor of \(8\), leaving \(1\) on top and \(2\) on the bottom. The \(15\) on top and the \(25\) on the bottom share a factor of \(5\), leaving \(3\) on top and \(5\) on the bottom. That turns the product into \(\tfrac{3}{2}\times\tfrac{1}{5}=\tfrac{3\times 1}{2\times 5}=\tfrac{3}{10}\). Since \(3\) and \(10\) share no common factor other than \(1\), this is already in simplest form. So \(\tfrac{a}{b}=\boxed{\tfrac{3}{10}}\).

review.x: \(\tfrac{2310}{2730}\): top is \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 11\), bottom is \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 13\). Cancel shared primes. Write the reduced fraction as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: Write the two prime lists right above each other so matching primes line up. The top reads \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 11\) and the bottom reads \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 13\). Which primes appear in both lists?
The primes \(2\), \(3\), \(5\), and \(7\) sit in both the top and the bottom, so each of those pairs cancels to \(1\). Whatever prime is left on top goes over whatever prime is left on the bottom.
review.sol: Stacking the two factorizations makes the shared primes easy to spot. The top is \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 11\) and the bottom is \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 13\). Every prime in the bottom except \(13\) also shows up in the top, so the part the two numbers have in common is \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7=210\). That common chunk is exactly the \(\gcd\), and dividing it out of both numbers cancels it cleanly. On top, \(2\times 3\times 5\times 7\) cancels and only \(11\) survives. On the bottom, the same four primes cancel and only \(13\) survives. So the whole fraction collapses to \(\tfrac{11}{13}\). Since \(11\) and \(13\) are both prime and different, they share no factors, which tells us this is already in lowest terms. $$\frac{2310}{2730}=\frac{2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 11}{2\times 3\times 5\times 7\times 13}=\boxed{\frac{11}{13}}$$

review.x: \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{3}{4}\times\tfrac{4}{5}\times\tfrac{5}{6}\times\tfrac{6}{7}\). Cancel matching pairs. Write the result as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) in simplest form.
hints: Multiplying straight across, every top number except the very first also shows up as a bottom number somewhere earlier. Matching tops and bottoms cancel to \(1\).
Once all the middle numbers cancel, only the first top \(2\) and the last bottom \(7\) are left standing.
review.sol: Multiply across the tops and across the bottoms, but keep the factors lined up so the pattern shows. $$\frac{2\times 3\times 4\times 5\times 6}{3\times 4\times 5\times 6\times 7}$$ The bottom carries a \(3,4,5,6\), and so does the top. Each of those pairs cancels to \(1\), since a number over itself is \(1\). What never gets a partner is the \(2\) at the very front of the top and the \(7\) at the very end of the bottom. Everything between them folds away, leaving $$\frac{2}{7}.$$ The fraction \(\tfrac{2}{7}\) is already in simplest form, because \(2\) and \(7\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\). So the entire chain collapses to \(\boxed{2/7}\).

review.x: Simplify \(\dfrac{m^5}{m^2\times 4}\): cancel \(m\)s, leaving \(\dfrac{m^3}{4}\). Set \(m=2\). What single number does the expression equal?
hints: The top has five copies of \(m\) and the bottom has two copies. Two copies on top pair off with the two on the bottom and disappear, leaving \(m\times m\times m\) over \(4\).
So the fraction is \(\dfrac{m^3}{4}\). Put in \(m=2\), which makes the top \(2\times 2\times 2=8\), and finish the division.
review.sol: Cancelling shared factors works for letters exactly like it works for numbers. The top is five copies of \(m\) multiplied together, and the bottom is two copies of \(m\) times \(4\). Each \(m\) on the bottom cancels one \(m\) on top. Two of the five top copies pair off and vanish, so three copies of \(m\) survive on top and only the \(4\) is left underneath. That gives \(\dfrac{m^3}{4}\). Now set \(m=2\). The top becomes \(2\times 2\times 2=8\), so the fraction is \(\dfrac{8}{4}=2\). The whole expression equals \(\boxed{2}\).

review.x: A measuring strip must mark both \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) in and \(\tfrac{1}{10}\) in tick marks. Find the smallest shared bottom: \(\operatorname{lcm}(8,10)\). What is that least common denominator?
hints: The least common denominator is the smallest number that both \(8\) and \(10\) divide into evenly. Walk through the multiples of the larger bottom, \(10, 20, 30, 40, \dots\), and stop at the first one that \(8\) also divides.

Break each bottom into primes, \(8 = 2\times 2\times 2\) and \(10 = 2\times 5\). The \(\operatorname{lcm}\) takes the most of each prime that shows up in either one, so \(2\times 2\times 2\times 5\). Multiply that out.
review.sol: The shared bottom has to be a number that both \(8\) and \(10\) divide into cleanly, and we want the smallest one so the strip is not cut finer than it needs to be. That is the \(\operatorname{lcm}\) of \(8\) and \(10\). Break each into primes, \(8 = 2\times 2\times 2\) and \(10 = 2\times 5\). The least common multiple takes the most of each prime that appears in either number, which is three \(2\)s and one \(5\), so $$\operatorname{lcm}(8,10) = 2\times 2\times 2\times 5 = 40.$$ Check that both bottoms fit, \(40 \div 8 = 5\) and \(40 \div 10 = 4\), both whole, so a strip ruled into \(40\)ths shows \(\tfrac{3}{8} = \tfrac{15}{40}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{10} = \tfrac{4}{40}\) exactly. The least common denominator is \(\boxed{40}\).

review.x: Three rhythm loops land every \(4\), \(10\), and \(15\) beats. The next beat where all three align is \(\operatorname{lcm}(4,10,15)\). What is that number?
hints: Break each number into its prime building blocks first. \(4=2^2\), \(10=2\cdot 5\), and \(15=3\cdot 5\). The least common multiple needs to cover every prime that shows up.
For each prime, keep the highest power you see. You need \(2^2\) (from the \(4\)), one \(3\) (from the \(15\)), and one \(5\) (from the \(10\) and \(15\)). Multiply those together.
review.sol: Start by writing each number as a product of primes. \(4=2^2\), \(10=2\cdot 5\), and \(15=3\cdot 5\). The least common multiple has to be divisible by all three, so it must include enough copies of each prime to cover whichever number uses it most. The \(4\) demands two \(2\)s, the \(15\) brings in a \(3\), and the \(5\) appears in both \(10\) and \(15\) so we need one \(5\). Putting those together gives \(2^2\cdot 3\cdot 5 = 4\cdot 3\cdot 5 = 60\). A quick check confirms it. \(60\div 4=15\), \(60\div 10=6\), and \(60\div 15=4\), all whole numbers, and nothing smaller works for all three. So the loops realign, and the common denominator is \(\boxed{60}\).

review.x: Rewrite \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) as an equivalent fraction over \(60\). Find what \(12\) is multiplied by to reach \(60\), then scale the top. What is the new numerator?
hints: The denominator jumped from \(12\) to \(60\). Ask yourself what single number times \(12\) gives \(60\).
Since \(12 \times 5 = 60\), you multiplied the bottom by \(5\). To keep the fraction equal, multiply the top by \(5\) too.
review.sol: An equivalent fraction comes from multiplying the top and bottom by the same number, so the fraction keeps its value while wearing a new outfit. Start with the denominators. To turn \(12\) into \(60\), solve \(12 \times ? = 60\), which gives \(60 \div 12 = 5\). So the scaling factor is \(5\). Now apply that same factor to the numerator so the fraction stays equal. \(7 \times 5 = 35\). That means \(\tfrac{7}{12} = \tfrac{35}{60}\), and you can check it since both reduce back to \(\tfrac{7}{12}\). The missing top is \(\boxed{35}\).

review.x: A track is \(\tfrac{4}{9}\) done. Rewrite over the common denominator \(45\). Find what \(9\) is multiplied by to reach \(45\), then scale the top. What is the new numerator over \(45\)?
hints: The denominator went from \(9\) to \(45\). Ask yourself what number times \(9\) gives \(45\).
Since \(9 \times 5 = 45\), you multiplied the bottom by \(5\). Multiply the top by \(5\) too so the value stays the same.
review.sol:
The denominator changed from \(9\) to \(45\). To find the scaling factor, see what \(9\) was multiplied by. Since \(9 \times 5 = 45\), the bottom was scaled by \(5\).

To keep the fraction equal to \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), the top has to grow by the same factor. So \(4 \times 5 = 20\), which gives \(\tfrac{20}{45}\).

You can check it. \(\tfrac{20}{45}\) divides back down by \(5\) to \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), so the value never changed. The new numerator is \(\boxed{20}\).

review.x: Maya finishes \(\tfrac{4}{7}\) of a loop; Theo finishes \(\tfrac{5}{9}\). Grow both to the lcd of \(7\) and \(9\), compare the tops. Which is larger? Write the answer as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: The least common denominator of \(7\) and \(9\) is their \(\operatorname{lcm}\). Since \(7\) and \(9\) share no common factor, that \(\operatorname{lcm}\) is just \(7 \times 9 = 63\).
Multiply the top and bottom of each fraction so both land on \(63\). \(\tfrac{4}{7} = \tfrac{4 \times 9}{63}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{5 \times 7}{63}\). Whichever has the bigger numerator wins.
review.sol: Both fractions have different bottoms, so they cannot be compared at a glance. The fix is to give them a shared denominator. Since \(7\) and \(9\) have no common factor, the least common denominator is \(7 \times 9 = 63\). Now grow each fraction to that denominator. For \(\tfrac{4}{7}\), multiply top and bottom by \(9\), which gives \(\tfrac{4 \times 9}{7 \times 9} = \tfrac{36}{63}\). For \(\tfrac{5}{9}\), multiply top and bottom by \(7\), which gives \(\tfrac{5 \times 7}{9 \times 7} = \tfrac{35}{63}\). With equal denominators the comparison is just the numerators, and \(36 > 35\), so \(\tfrac{36}{63}\) is the larger amount. That came from \(\tfrac{4}{7}\), which means Maya covered more of the loop. Reported in its original form, the larger fraction is \(\boxed{\tfrac{4}{7}}\).

review.x: A turbine produced \(280\) MWh out of \(630\) regional MWh. Break both into primes, cancel shared factors, and write \(\tfrac{280}{630}\) in simplest form.
hints: Factor both numbers into primes. \(280 = 2\times 2\times 2\times 5\times 7\) and \(630 = 2\times 3\times 3\times 5\times 7\). Which primes appear in both lists?
They share one \(2\), one \(5\), and one \(7\), so \(\gcd(280,630) = 2\times 5\times 7 = 70\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(70\).
review.sol: The share is \(\tfrac{280}{630}\), and the fast road to simplest form is the \(\gcd\). Break both into primes, \(280 = 2\times 2\times 2\times 5\times 7\) and \(630 = 2\times 3\times 3\times 5\times 7\). The primes they both carry are one \(2\), one \(5\), and one \(7\), so $$\gcd(280,630) = 2\times 5\times 7 = 70.$$ Divide top and bottom by that single number, \(280 \div 70 = 4\) and \(630 \div 70 = 9\), which gives \(\tfrac{4}{9}\). Check we are done, \(4 = 2\times 2\) and \(9 = 3\times 3\) share no prime, so the only common factor left is \(1\). The turbine's share in simplest form is \(\boxed{4/9}\).

review.x: Rope 1 reaches \(\tfrac{11}{15}\) of a wall; Rope 2 reaches \(\tfrac{7}{9}\). The lcd is \(45\). Rename both over \(45\) and compare. Which fraction is larger? Write it as \(\tfrac{a}{b}\).
hints: You cannot compare \(\tfrac{11}{15}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) by eye because the pieces are different sizes. Find a common denominator first. The smallest number both \(15\) and \(9\) divide into is \(45\).
Grow each fraction to forty-fifths. Multiply \(\tfrac{11}{15}\) by \(\tfrac{3}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) by \(\tfrac{5}{5}\), then compare the two new numerators. The bigger numerator wins.
review.sol: The two fractions have different denominators, so rename them over a shared one before comparing. The least common multiple of \(15\) and \(9\) is \(45\), since \(45 = 15 \times 3 = 9 \times 5\). Grow each fraction to forty-fifths. For the first rope, \(\tfrac{11}{15} = \tfrac{11 \times 3}{15 \times 3} = \tfrac{33}{45}\). For the second, \(\tfrac{7}{9} = \tfrac{7 \times 5}{9 \times 5} = \tfrac{35}{45}\). Now the pieces are the same size, so I just compare numerators. Since \(35 > 33\), the second rope reaches farther, which means \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) is the larger fraction. The larger fraction is \(\boxed{7/9}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\tfrac{12}{25}\times\tfrac{35}{18}\div\tfrac{7}{15}\). Flip the last fraction, then cancel every shared factor across all tops and bottoms. What single number unlocks the box?
hints: Dividing by \(\tfrac{7}{15}\) is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{15}{7}\). Rewrite the whole expression as \(\tfrac{12}{25}\times\tfrac{35}{18}\times\tfrac{15}{7}\) so everything is one product.

Before multiplying, hunt for a top and a bottom that share a factor and cancel them. For example \(35\) and \(7\) share a \(7\), and \(15\) and \(25\) share a \(5\). Keep cancelling until nothing large is left.
review.sol: First turn the division into multiplication by flipping the last fraction. Dividing by \(\tfrac{7}{15}\) means multiplying by \(\tfrac{15}{7}\), so the whole expression becomes \[\frac{12}{25}\times\frac{35}{18}\times\frac{15}{7}.\] Now cancel shared factors across all the tops and bottoms before multiplying anything. The \(35\) on top and the \(7\) on the bottom share a \(7\), leaving \(5\) up top. That \(5\) pairs with the \(25\) on the bottom, which becomes \(5\). Next, \(15\) on top and that remaining \(5\) on the bottom leave \(3\) up top. Now look at \(12\) and \(18\), which share a \(6\), becoming \(2\) and \(3\). The \(3\) on the bottom cancels the \(3\) we still had on top. After all the cancelling, the top holds a single \(2\) and the bottom holds a single \(1\). So the product is \(\tfrac{2}{1}\), and the box opens with \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: A robot at \(0\) steps a third of the way to \(1\), then half of the way, and rolls to a stop past the middle. It is tempting to stack the two fractions, top on top and bottom on bottom, and read off the landing spot, but the two steps are not the same length and that matters. Adding fractions is just stepping right along the line and subtracting is stepping left, so the whole lesson is one question. When do two steps stack cleanly, and what do you do when they do not?
A counter on a strip notched into ninths slides right \(2\) notches, then \(5\) more. Every notch is the same width. What is \(\tfrac{2}{9}+\tfrac{5}{9}\), written as a fraction?
hints: The two slides are made of the same ninth-sized notches, so you never have to change the notch size partway through. You only need to count how many of those equal notches the counter has crossed by the end.
Add the number of notches, 2 then 5, to get the total notches crossed, and keep the notch size the same. That total goes on top, and the 9 that names the notch size stays on the bottom.
blocks.sol: Because the strip is cut into 9 equal notches, each notch is exactly \(\tfrac{1}{9}\) of the whole strip. That number, the 9 on the bottom, is the name of the step size, and it does not change as the counter moves. The first slide of 2 notches covers \(\tfrac{2}{9}\), which is just 2 copies of that one ninth-sized step. The second slide of 5 notches covers \(\tfrac{5}{9}\), another 5 copies of the exact same step. Since every step is the same size, adding the two slides is nothing more than counting all the notches the counter crossed, \(2+5=7\) of them. Seven equal ninth-sized notches from 0 lands the counter on \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) of the strip. The step size 9 stays put on the bottom, and only the count of steps, 7, lands on top. So \(\tfrac{2}{9}+\tfrac{5}{9}=\tfrac{2+5}{9}=\tfrac{7}{9}\), which is already in simplest form since 7 and 9 share no common factor. \(\boxed{7/9}\)

blocks.x: A marker at \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) steps left by \(\tfrac{3}{8}\). Count remaining eighth-notches, then reduce. What is \(\tfrac{7}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}\) in simplest form?
hints: Both fractions are built from the same eighth-sized notches, so subtracting them is really just counting notches. Start at 7 notches and step back 3. How many notches remain, and over what bottom number?
Seven eighths minus three eighths leaves \(\tfrac{4}{8}\). Now do not stop there. The top is 4 and the bottom is 8, and they share a factor of 4. Divide both by 4 and write what you get.
blocks.sol: Because both fractions are measured in the same eighth-sized notches, the bottom number stays put and only the count of notches changes. You begin at 7 notches and step left by 3, so \(7-3=4\) notches are left. That gives \(\tfrac{7}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}=\tfrac{4}{8}\). Now comes the habit worth building. The number \(\tfrac{4}{8}\) is a real answer, but its top and bottom still share a factor, since 4 divides into both. Dividing the top and bottom by 4 gives \(\tfrac{4}{8}=\tfrac{4\div 4}{8\div 4}=\tfrac{1}{2}\). If you glance back at the bench, landing on the fourth notch out of eight is exactly the halfway point, which is a nice check that \(\tfrac{4}{8}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) name the same spot. In simplest form the marker rests at \(\boxed{1/2}\).

blocks.x: Adding and subtracting with equal denominators. When two fractions are made of the very same size piece, the bottom number is just the name of that piece, telling you how big one piece is and nothing more. That name does not change when you gather more pieces or hand some back, so you add or subtract only the top numbers, the count of pieces, and copy the bottom down exactly as it was. You never add the bottoms, because you are totaling pieces, not names. After you land, check whether the count and the size still share a common factor, since a sum or difference is often not in simplest form yet. That is how \(\tfrac{7}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}\) quietly becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2}\).

font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="var(--gold-deep)">7 ninths covered, just 2 and 5 counted</text></svg>
blocks.cap: Because every cell is the identical ninth, the two arrows lock end to end with no gap and no overlap. So you just add the counts, 2 then 5, and the size word "ninth" on the bottom never changes. That is the whole reason \(\tfrac{2}{9}+\tfrac{5}{9}=\tfrac{7}{9}\).

blocks.x: A friend adds \(\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{2}{5}\) (tops and bottoms). But \(\tfrac{2}{5}<\tfrac{1}{2}\), less than one step. Find the correct sum \(\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{2}\) over the lcd.
hints: The two fractions are different sizes, so you cannot add them yet. Find a denominator that both 3 and 2 fit into evenly. The smallest one is the lcd of 2 and 3.

The lcd is \(6\). Rename each fraction over 6, since the pieces have to match before you count them: \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{2}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{6}\). Now the denominators agree, so keep the \(6\) on the bottom and add only the top numbers.
blocks.sol: The friend's shortcut fails because \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) are pieces of different sizes, and you can only add counts of the same size piece. So the first job is to make the two steps match. The lcd of \(2\) and \(3\) is \(6\), which means both moves can be measured in sixths. Rewrite each one over \(6\). For the third, multiply top and bottom by \(2\): \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{2}{6}\). For the half, multiply top and bottom by \(3\): \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{6}\). Now the robot's two moves are \(2\) sixths and then \(3\) sixths, all the same size step. Counting them together gives \(2+3=5\) of those sixths, so the total is $$\tfrac{2}{6}+\tfrac{3}{6}=\tfrac{5}{6}.$$ Check it against the picture. Since \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is more than \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{6}\), the robot does land to the right of halfway, just like the half-step move promised. And \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is already in simplest form, because \(\gcd(5,6)=1\). \(\boxed{5/6}\)

blocks.x: Never add the denominators. Adding the tops and adding the bottoms is a trap, because it pretends a half and a third are the same size when they are not, so the count it spits out means nothing. The bottom number is a size name, not a thing you count, so you never add it. To actually add two fractions, first rebuild both out of pieces that match, which is all a common denominator does. Once \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) are both rewritten as a number of equal sixths, you are back to plain counting.
A glider burns \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) then \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of a charge. Find \(\operatorname{lcm}(4,6)\), rewrite, add. How much burned total? Write in simplest form.
hints: You can only add numerators once the bottoms match, so first find the lcd of \(4\) and \(6\). The smallest number both divide into is \(12\). Now think about what each fraction becomes when its denominator is \(12\).

Use the build-up move from 4.4. Since \(4\times 3=12\), multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{3}{12}\). Since \(6\times 2=12\), multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) by \(2\) to get \(\tfrac{2}{12}\). Add the numerators over the common bottom \(12\), then check whether the result can be reduced.
blocks.sol: The two fractions have different bottoms, so before adding anything you need pieces of the same size. That is the whole reason for the lcd. The lcd of \(4\) and \(6\) is the smallest number both divide into, which is \(12\). Now do the build-up move from 4.4 on each fraction. For \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), since \(4\times 3=12\), multiply top and bottom by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{12}\). For \(\tfrac{1}{6}\), since \(6\times 2=12\), multiply top and bottom by \(2\) to get \(\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{2}{12}\). Both flights are now measured in twelfths, so the numerators are countable. The first hop is \(3\) twelfths and the second is \(2\) twelfths, which is \(3+2=5\) twelfths in total. That gives \(\tfrac{3}{12}+\tfrac{2}{12}=\tfrac{5}{12}\). Finally check for reducing. Since \(\gcd(5,12)=1\), the fraction is already in simplest form. So the two hops burned \(\boxed{5/12}\) of a charge.

The top strip is cut into four equal parts with the first part shaded gold, labelled one quarter, and a faint overlay slices each of the four parts into three so the strip becomes twelve fine cells with three shaded, relabelled three twelfths. The middle strip is cut into six equal parts with the first part shaded gold, labelled one sixth, and a faint overlay slices each of the six parts into two so the strip becomes twelve fine cells with two shaded, relabelled two twelfths.
