sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">3 and 2 make 5 twelfths</text></svg>
blocks.cap: A quarter and a sixth refuse to line up because their pieces are different widths, so there is nothing to count yet. Recut both over the same twelfths and the mismatch vanishes. The quarter, cut into \(3\) thinner cells, becomes \(\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{12}\), and the sixth, cut into \(2\) thinner cells, becomes \(\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{2}{12}\). Now every cell is one twelfth wide, so the two runs slide together end to end and you just count, \(3\) cells and \(2\) cells make \(5\) cells, which is \(\tfrac{3}{12}+\tfrac{2}{12}=\tfrac{5}{12}\).

blocks.x: Nothing about subtraction is new. If adding is a step right along the line, subtracting is a step left, and the rule about sizes does not care which way you walk. You still cannot count pieces until they match, so the same three moves carry you through a difference just as they did a sum. Match the sizes, count the pieces, simplify. The next problem asks for \(\tfrac{5}{6}-\tfrac{3}{8}\), so watch how little really changes once the denominators agree.
Timer is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) used; shortcut saves \(\tfrac{3}{8}\). Find \(\operatorname{lcm}(6,8)\), rename, subtract. Find \(\tfrac{5}{6}-\tfrac{3}{8}\) in simplest form.
hints: The two slices are different sizes, so first give them a common bottom. List multiples of \(6\) and of \(8\) and find the smallest number both reach. That smallest shared multiple is your lcd, and every sixth and every eighth can be re-cut into pieces of that size.

The lcd is \(24\). Rename \(\tfrac{5}{6}=\tfrac{20}{24}\) since \(6\times 4=24\) and \(5\times 4=20\), and rename \(\tfrac{3}{8}=\tfrac{9}{24}\) since \(8\times 3=24\) and \(3\times 3=9\). Now the bottoms match, so subtract the tops: \(20-9\) over \(24\). Check whether the result can be reduced.
blocks.sol: The trouble is that a sixth of the ring and an eighth of the ring are different lengths, so the \(5\) and the \(3\) are counting pieces of different sizes. Subtraction only works once every piece is the same size, so first rebuild both fractions over a common denominator. The least common denominator of \(6\) and \(8\) is the smallest number both divide into. Walk the multiples of \(8\), which are \(8, 16, 24\), and \(24\) is the first one that \(6\) also reaches, so the lcd is \(24\). Now re-cut each fraction into twenty-fourths. Since \(6\times 4=24\), multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{20}{24}\). Since \(8\times 3=24\), multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{9}{24}\). With matching bottoms the subtraction is easy. You have \(20\) twenty-fourths used and you claw back \(9\) twenty-fourths, leaving \(20-9=11\) twenty-fourths over the same denominator, so \(\tfrac{20}{24}-\tfrac{9}{24}=\tfrac{11}{24}\). Last, check for simplifying. The number \(11\) is prime and does not divide \(24\), so \(\gcd(11,24)=1\) and the fraction is already in lowest terms. After the shortcut, the round is still \(\tfrac{11}{24}\) used. $$\boxed{11/24}$$

blocks.x: Three-step rule. Adding and subtracting fractions is three moves in the same order every time. First make the pieces the same size by finding the lcd and renaming both fractions, because counting only works when every piece counts as the same amount. Once the bottoms match, add or subtract the top numbers and keep that shared bottom, since the size of a piece never changes when you count more or fewer of them. Then check whether the answer still shares a factor top and bottom, and reduce it if it does. Match the sizes, count, simplify, and the only genuinely new part is the easy counting step in the middle.
Mixer pours \(\tfrac{5}{12}\) then \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). The lcd is \(12\). Add the tops, then reduce, since the sum shares a factor. What fraction was poured in simplest form?
hints: The lcd of \(12\) and \(4\) is \(12\), since \(12\) is already a multiple of \(4\). The \(\tfrac{5}{12}\) is fine as is. Rename \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) so its bottom is \(12\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(3\).
You should have \(\tfrac{5}{12}+\tfrac{3}{12}=\tfrac{8}{12}\). That is correct but not reduced. Both \(8\) and \(12\) divide by \(4\), so split both by \(4\) to land on your final fraction.
blocks.sol: Start by finding a common bottom. The denominators are \(12\) and \(4\), and \(12\) is already a multiple of \(4\), so the lcd is just \(12\). That means \(\tfrac{5}{12}\) needs no change at all. Only \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) gets renamed. Multiply its top and bottom by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{12}\), the same amount measured in twelfths. Now both portions are in twelfths, so add the counts on top and keep the bottom. \(\tfrac{5}{12}+\tfrac{3}{12}=\tfrac{8}{12}\). This is a true total, but pause here. The top \(8\) and the bottom \(12\) are both even, and in fact both are divisible by \(4\). Their \(\gcd\) is \(4\), so the fraction is not in simplest form yet. Divide top and bottom by \(4\). \(\tfrac{8}{12}=\tfrac{8\div 4}{12\div 4}=\tfrac{2}{3}\). Now the top \(2\) and the bottom \(3\) share no common factor besides \(1\), so it is fully reduced. The mixer poured out \(\boxed{2/3}\) of the can.

blocks.x: The \(+\) sign appeared in European arithmetic in the late 1400s, likely from writing the Latin et ("and"). The fraction bar is older, from Arabic c. 1100s.
Dial reads \(\tfrac{3}{8}\); step left by \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) crosses \(0\). Common denominator, subtract in order, keep the sign. What is \(\tfrac{3}{8}-\tfrac{5}{6}\) in simplest form?
hints: The two denominators are \(8\) and \(6\). Their least common denominator is \(24\), since \(24\) is the smallest number both \(8\) and \(6\) divide into. Rewrite each fraction with \(24\) on the bottom before you try to subtract anything.

Multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{9}{24}\), and multiply \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{20}{24}\). Now the subtraction is \(\tfrac{9}{24}-\tfrac{20}{24}\). Since \(9\) is smaller than \(20\), the count \(9-20\) comes out negative. Work out \(9-20\), keep it over \(24\), and check whether the result can be reduced.
blocks.sol: Start by getting both fractions over one common bottom. The denominators are \(8\) and \(6\), and the least common denominator is \(24\), because \(24\) is the smallest number that both \(8\) and \(6\) divide into evenly. Rename each fraction. For \(\tfrac{3}{8}\), multiply top and bottom by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{9}{24}\). For \(\tfrac{5}{6}\), multiply top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{20}{24}\). The subtraction becomes $$\frac{9}{24}-\frac{20}{24}.$$ Now both pieces are the same size, so you just subtract the top counts and keep the bottom. The order matters here. You are taking \(20\) twenty-fourths away from only \(9\) twenty-fourths, and you cannot, so the count drops below zero. \(9-20=-11\), which gives \(\tfrac{-11}{24}\). This is the dial walking left past \(0\) and stopping on the negative side, just like the points you placed left of \(0\) in 4.1. Last, check whether it simplifies. The number \(11\) is prime and \(24\) is not a multiple of \(11\), so \(\gcd(11,24)=1\) and the fraction is already in lowest terms. The reading lands at $$\boxed{-11/24}.$$

blocks.x: Negative results. A difference between fractions does not have to land above zero. If you step left by more than you started with, you sail past \(0\) and the answer comes out negative, which is the same number line from 4.1 and changes nothing about how you work. You still match the sizes, subtract the counts, and keep the minus sign, so order matters and \(\tfrac{3}{8}-\tfrac{5}{6}\) is the opposite of \(\tfrac{5}{6}-\tfrac{3}{8}\). And whether you add or subtract, the combining step never checks whether its answer is tidy, so the last move is always to hunt for a shared factor and divide it out.
Spool holds \(4\) m; cut \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) m off. Rewrite \(4=\tfrac{12}{3}\), subtract. How much cord remains? Find \(4-\tfrac{2}{3}\) as a single fraction.
hints: You cannot take thirds away from something that is still written in whole meters. First turn the \(4\) into thirds. Since \(4=\tfrac{4}{1}\) and you want a denominator of \(3\), multiply the top and bottom by \(3\). How many thirds are in 4 whole meters?

Four whole meters is \(\tfrac{4\times 3}{1\times 3}=\tfrac{12}{3}\), so you have 12 thirds on the spool. Now both amounts are measured in the same size pieces, and the subtraction becomes \(\tfrac{12}{3}-\tfrac{2}{3}\). Keep the denominator and subtract the tops, \(12-2\).
blocks.sol: The snippet is measured in thirds, but the spool is measured in whole meters, so the two amounts are not yet speaking the same language. The fix is to notice that \(4\) is already a fraction wearing a disguise, \(4=\tfrac{4}{1}\). To give it thirds, multiply the top and bottom by the same number \(3\), which does not change its value, only its appearance. $$4=\frac{4}{1}=\frac{4\times 3}{1\times 3}=\frac{12}{3}.$$ So four whole meters is exactly twelve thirds of a meter. That makes sense, each meter splits into 3 thirds, and \(4\times 3=12\). Now both amounts wear the same denominator, and the subtraction is just counting backward in thirds. $$\frac{12}{3}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{12-2}{3}=\frac{10}{3}.$$ You start with 12 thirds, step back 2 thirds, and 10 thirds remain. Check whether it simplifies. The top is \(10\) and the bottom is \(3\), and since \(\gcd(10,3)=1\), the fraction is already in simplest form. The spool has \(\tfrac{10}{3}\) of a meter of cord left. $$\boxed{10/3}$$

blocks.x: Spring: \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) planted; path takes \(\tfrac{2}{3}\); \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) replanted. Find lcd for \(4,3,6\), combine in one sweep. What fraction ends up in use?
hints: The smallest number that \(4\), \(3\), and \(6\) all divide into is \(12\). Rewrite every fraction with \(12\) on the bottom before you touch the top numbers, and keep the plus and minus signs attached to the right pieces.

Over \(12\) the three fractions are \(\tfrac{9}{12}\), \(\tfrac{8}{12}\), and \(\tfrac{2}{12}\). The expression \(\tfrac{3}{4}-\tfrac{2}{3}+\tfrac{1}{6}\) becomes \(\tfrac{9-8+2}{12}\). Work left to right on top, then simplify the fraction you get.
blocks.sol: The trap here is doing it in pairs, where it is easy to lose a sign halfway through. The cleaner move is to put everything over one common denominator first, then handle all three numerators together. Since \(4\), \(3\), and \(6\) all divide \(12\), use \(12\). Rewrite each fraction by scaling top and bottom by the same factor, so the value never changes. \(\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{9}{12}\), \(\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{8}{12}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{2}{12}\). Now the whole expression sits over a single denominator and the signs travel with their numbers. $$\frac{3}{4}-\frac{2}{3}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{9}{12}-\frac{8}{12}+\frac{2}{12}=\frac{9-8+2}{12}.$$ Sweep across the top in order. \(9-8=1\), then \(1+2=3\), giving \(\tfrac{3}{12}\). Both \(3\) and \(12\) share a factor of \(3\), so divide each by \(3\) to land on \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). So a quarter of the plot ends up in use, which makes sense, the path removed almost everything the spring planting added, and the summer bit nudged it back up a little. $$\boxed{1/4}$$

blocks.x: Rider: \(+\tfrac{7}{10}-\tfrac{1}{5}+\tfrac{1}{2}\). lcd for \(10,5,2\) is \(10\). Combine in one pass. What fraction of the loop is her total progress?
hints: You need one denominator that all three fractions can share. Ask yourself the smallest number that \(10\), \(5\), and \(2\) all divide into evenly. Since \(5\) and \(2\) both already divide \(10\), that shared denominator is just \(10\).

Rewrite each fraction over \(10\). The first is already \(\tfrac{7}{10}\). For \(\tfrac{1}{5}\), multiply top and bottom by \(2\) to get \(\tfrac{2}{10}\). For \(\tfrac{1}{2}\), multiply top and bottom by \(5\) to get \(\tfrac{5}{10}\). Now combine the numerators in order, keeping the minus sign on the second one, so \(7-2+5\) over \(10\).
blocks.sol: The trap here is reaching for a fresh common denominator without checking whether you already have one. Look at \(10\), \(5\), and \(2\). Both \(5\) and \(2\) divide evenly into \(10\), so \(10\) is itself the least common denominator. No bigger number is needed. Now rewrite all three fractions over \(10\) in one move. The first term stays as \(\tfrac{7}{10}\). For the second, \(\tfrac{1}{5}=\tfrac{1\cdot 2}{5\cdot 2}=\tfrac{2}{10}\). For the third, \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{1\cdot 5}{2\cdot 5}=\tfrac{5}{10}\). The signed sum becomes $$\frac{7}{10}-\frac{2}{10}+\frac{5}{10}.$$ With a shared bottom, you combine the top numbers in a single pass, carrying each sign exactly as written, so \(7-2+5=10\). That gives \(\tfrac{10}{10}\), which is one whole. As a sanity check, the backtrack of \(\tfrac{2}{10}\) cancels part of the first stretch and the final \(\tfrac{5}{10}\) carries her the rest of the way, landing her right at the start point of a fresh loop. Her total progress is exactly one full loop, so the answer is \(\boxed{1}\).

blocks.x: Summary. Every move in this lesson came from one sentence. You can only add or subtract pieces when they are the same size, and the denominator is just the name of that size. So if the sizes already match, you count, adding or subtracting the tops and keeping the bottom as it is. If they do not match, you make them match first by renaming both fractions over a common denominator, the same lcd you already know how to find, and then you count. A whole number joins in wearing the disguise \(\tfrac{n}{1}\), and three or more fractions all go over one shared bottom at once. Whatever you land on, keep the sign honest, even when a difference falls left of zero, and reduce so the answer wears its simplest name.

review.x: A counter on a sevenths number line steps right \(\tfrac{1}{7}\) then \(\tfrac{3}{7}\). Count the notches. Write \(\tfrac{1}{7}+\tfrac{3}{7}\) in simplest form.
hints: The bottoms are already the same, both sevenths. One step covers \(1\) notch and the next covers \(3\) notches, so how many notches has the counter moved in all?
Keep the \(7\) on the bottom and just add the tops. Count \(1+3\) sevenths, then check whether the fraction can be reduced.
review.sol: Both fractions are already measured in the same unit, sevenths, so no new common denominator is needed. The first step moves the counter \(1\) notch and the second step moves it \(3\) more, which is \(1+3=4\) notches from \(0\). Stack that over the shared bottom and you get \(\tfrac{1}{7}+\tfrac{3}{7}=\tfrac{1+3}{7}=\tfrac{4}{7}\). Now check for simplifying. The top is \(4\) and the bottom is \(7\), and \(\gcd(4,7)=1\), so nothing cancels and the fraction is already as simple as it gets. The counter lands at \(\boxed{4/7}\).

review.x: A counter at \(\tfrac{9}{10}\) slides left by \(\tfrac{3}{10}\). Subtract and reduce. What is \(\tfrac{9}{10}-\tfrac{3}{10}\) in simplest form?
hints: Both fractions already share the same bottom number, so the tenths just subtract straight across. Keep the denominator and work out \(9-3\) on top.
You should land on \(\tfrac{6}{10}\). Now find the largest number that divides both \(6\) and \(10\), and divide top and bottom by it.
review.sol: The two fractions are already same size pieces, both counted in tenths, so the subtraction happens only on top. Nine tenths take away three tenths leaves six tenths, \(\tfrac{9}{10}-\tfrac{3}{10}=\tfrac{6}{10}\). Now reduce. The greatest common divisor of \(6\) and \(10\) is \(2\), so divide both by \(2\) to get \(\tfrac{6\div 2}{10\div 2}=\tfrac{3}{5}\). Since \(3\) and \(5\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is as low as it goes. \(\boxed{3/5}\)

review.x: A classmate wrote \(\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{2}{7}\) by stacking tops and bottoms. But \(\tfrac{2}{7}<\tfrac{1}{3}\), so a sum came out smaller than a part, which is impossible. Find the correct sum over the lcd, in simplest form.
hints: The lcd of \(3\) and \(4\) is \(12\). Rewrite each fraction with \(12\) on the bottom before you add anything.
\(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{12}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{12}\). Now the pieces are the same size, so add only the numerators and keep the \(12\).
review.sol: The classmate's mistake is adding tops and bottoms straight across, which treats thirds and fourths as if they were the same size. They are not, so the first job is to give both fractions a common denominator. The lcd of \(3\) and \(4\) is \(12\), since that is the smallest number both divide into. Rename each fraction over \(12\). For \(\tfrac{1}{3}\), multiply top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{4}{12}\). For \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), multiply top and bottom by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{3}{12}\). Now both are counted in twelfths, so the pieces finally match. Add the numerators and keep the denominator, \(\tfrac{4}{12}+\tfrac{3}{12}=\tfrac{7}{12}\). Check whether it reduces. Since \(\gcd(7,12)=1\), the fraction is already in simplest form. And notice it passes the sanity test that fooled your classmate, \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) is larger than \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{12}\), exactly as a sum should be. \(\boxed{7/12}\)

review.x: "Sunset" blend: \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) mango, \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) pineapple. Find \(\operatorname{lcm}(5,3)\), rename, add. Total fruit share \(\tfrac{2}{5}+\tfrac{1}{3}\) in simplest form?
hints: The least common denominator of \(5\) and \(3\) is \(15\), since that is the smallest number both \(5\) and \(3\) divide into. Rewrite each fraction with \(15\) on the bottom.

Multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{6}{15}\), and multiply top and bottom of \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) by \(5\) to get \(\tfrac{5}{15}\). Now the pieces are the same size, so add the numerators.
review.sol: The two fractions count different-sized pieces, so they cannot be added until they share a denominator. The least common denominator of \(5\) and \(3\) is \(15\). Rename each fraction over \(15\). For the mango share, \(\tfrac{2}{5}=\tfrac{2\times 3}{5\times 3}=\tfrac{6}{15}\). For the pineapple share, \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{1\times 5}{3\times 5}=\tfrac{5}{15}\). Now both count fifteenths, so add the numerators and keep the common denominator. \(\tfrac{6}{15}+\tfrac{5}{15}=\tfrac{11}{15}\). Since \(11\) is prime and does not divide \(15\), the fraction is already in lowest terms. So the Sunset blend is \(\boxed{11/15}\) fruit juice by volume.

review.x: A candle is \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) of its original height; it burns \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) more. Find the lcd of \(8\) and \(3\), rename, subtract. What is \(\tfrac{7}{8}-\tfrac{1}{3}\) in simplest form?
hints: The denominators are \(8\) and \(3\). Their lcd is \(8\times 3=24\), since \(8\) and \(3\) share no common factor.
Rewrite each fraction over \(24\). Multiply \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) by \(\tfrac{3}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) by \(\tfrac{8}{8}\), then subtract the numerators.
review.sol: The two parts are measured against the same whole, the original height, so we can subtract them once they share a denominator. The denominators \(8\) and \(3\) have no common factor, so the lcd is just their product, \(8\times 3=24\). Rename each fraction over \(24\). \(\tfrac{7}{8}=\tfrac{7\times 3}{8\times 3}=\tfrac{21}{24}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{1\times 8}{3\times 8}=\tfrac{8}{24}\). Now subtract the numerators over the common denominator. \(\tfrac{21}{24}-\tfrac{8}{24}=\tfrac{13}{24}\). Since \(13\) is prime and does not divide \(24\), the fraction is already in lowest terms. So the candle keeps \(\boxed{13/24}\) of its original height.

review.x: A bottle is \(\tfrac{7}{10}\) full; the hiker drinks \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) of a bottle. Find the lcd, rename, subtract. What fraction of a bottle is left, in simplest form?
hints: You cannot subtract tenths and halves directly, the pieces are different sizes. The smallest shared bottom for \(10\) and \(2\) is \(10\), so rewrite \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) over \(10\) first.
Rewrite \(\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{5}{10}\), then subtract over the shared bottom, \(\tfrac{7}{10} - \tfrac{5}{10}\). After you get the answer, check whether the top and bottom still share a factor.
review.sol: The two amounts are measured in different sized pieces, so first give them a common denominator. The smallest bottom that both \(10\) and \(2\) divide into is \(10\), and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) renamed over \(10\) is \(\tfrac{5}{10}\), since multiplying top and bottom by \(5\) does not move the point. Now both are tenths, so subtract the tops and keep the bottom, $$\frac{7}{10} - \frac{5}{10} = \frac{7-5}{10} = \frac{2}{10}.$$ That is a correct answer but not yet the simplest one. The top \(2\) and the bottom \(10\) share a factor of \(2\), so divide both by it, \(\tfrac{2}{10} = \tfrac{1}{5}\). The bottle is \(\boxed{1/5}\) full.

review.x: A probe is at \(+\tfrac{1}{6}\) then sinks by \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), crossing \(0\). Compute \(\tfrac{1}{6}-\tfrac{3}{4}\) over a common denominator. What is the result, with sign, in simplest form?
hints: The lcd of \(6\) and \(4\) is \(12\). Rewrite both fractions with denominator \(12\) before you subtract.
You get \(\tfrac{2}{12}-\tfrac{9}{12}\). Since you are taking away more than you have, the numerator goes negative. Subtract \(2-9\) and keep the sign over \(12\).
review.sol: The two denominators are \(6\) and \(4\). Their least common denominator is \(12\), since \(12\) is the smallest number both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into. Rename each fraction over \(12\). For the first, \(\tfrac{1}{6}=\tfrac{1\times 2}{6\times 2}=\tfrac{2}{12}\). For the second, \(\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{3\times 3}{4\times 3}=\tfrac{9}{12}\). Now subtract with a common denominator. \(\tfrac{2}{12}-\tfrac{9}{12}=\tfrac{2-9}{12}=\tfrac{-7}{12}\). The probe started only \(\tfrac{2}{12}\) above zero and dropped \(\tfrac{9}{12}\), so it overshoots the surface and the reading is negative, which matches our sign. Check that \(\tfrac{-7}{12}\) is already in simplest form. Since \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(12\), the numerator and denominator share no common factor, so nothing cancels. The final reading is \(\boxed{-7/12}\).

review.x: A pitcher holds \(5\) cups; \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of a cup is added. Rewrite \(5=\tfrac{5}{1}\) over denominator \(3\), then combine. Find \(5+\tfrac{2}{3}\) as a single fraction.
hints: A whole number is already a fraction in disguise. Write 5 as \(\tfrac{5}{1}\), then ask what you multiply top and bottom by to make the denominator 3.
Multiplying \(\tfrac{5}{1}\) by \(\tfrac{3}{3}\) gives \(\tfrac{15}{3}\). Now both pieces share a denominator, so add the numerators.
review.sol: The two amounts have different forms, so first give them a shared denominator. Start by writing the whole number as a fraction, \(5=\tfrac{5}{1}\). To turn the denominator into 3, multiply top and bottom by 3, which keeps the value the same. That gives \(\tfrac{5}{1}\times\tfrac{3}{3}=\tfrac{15}{3}\). Now both pieces are measured in thirds, so you can simply add the numerators. \(\tfrac{15}{3}+\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{15+2}{3}=\tfrac{17}{3}\). Check whether it reduces. Since 17 is prime and shares no factor with 3, the fraction is already as simple as it gets. So the pitcher holds \(\boxed{17/3}\) cups.

review.x: A ribbon is \(2\) m; \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) m is cut off. Rewrite \(2=\tfrac{2}{1}\) over eighths, then subtract. What is \(2-\tfrac{3}{8}\) as a single fraction?
hints: Rewrite the whole number as a fraction with denominator \(8\). Since \(2=\tfrac{2}{1}\) and \(1\times 8=8\), multiply top and bottom by \(8\) to get \(2=\tfrac{16}{8}\).
Now both pieces are in eighths, so subtract the numerators over the same bottom. Work out \(\tfrac{16}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}\).
review.sol: The trouble with \(2-\tfrac{3}{8}\) is that \(2\) and \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) are not in matching pieces yet. So turn the whole number into eighths first. Write \(2\) as \(\tfrac{2}{1}\), then multiply top and bottom by \(8\) so the denominator becomes \(8\). That gives \(2=\tfrac{16}{8}\), because \(16\) eighths really is two whole meters. Now both amounts are measured in eighths, so the subtraction is clean. Take \(\tfrac{16}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}=\tfrac{16-3}{8}=\tfrac{13}{8}\). Check whether it can be reduced. The top is \(13\), which is prime, and \(13\) does not divide \(8\), so \(\gcd(13,8)=1\) and the fraction is already in lowest terms. The tailor is left with \(\tfrac{13}{8}\) of a meter of ribbon, which is a little more than one and a half meters, and that fits the picture since they only snipped off a small piece. So \(2-\tfrac{3}{8}=\boxed{13/8}\).

review.x: Three bursts: \(\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{4}+\tfrac{1}{8}\) of a fuel cell. lcd for \(2,4,8\) is \(8\). Total fraction used, in simplest form?
hints: The denominators are 2, 4, and 8. One of them already works as a common denominator for all three, since 2 and 4 both divide into 8.
Rewrite every fraction over 8. You get \(\tfrac{4}{8}\), \(\tfrac{2}{8}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{8}\). Now add the top numbers.
review.sol: When you have three fractions to add, you do not have to combine them in pairs. Look for one denominator that works for all of them at once. The denominators here are 2, 4, and 8, and 8 is a multiple of both 2 and 4, so 8 is the common denominator for the whole group. Rewrite each fraction over 8. Since \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{4}{8}\), \(\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{2}{8}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{8}\) is already there, the sum becomes \(\tfrac{4}{8}+\tfrac{2}{8}+\tfrac{1}{8}\). Now add the numerators in one sweep, \(4+2+1=7\), keeping the denominator 8, which gives \(\tfrac{7}{8}\). Check whether it simplifies. The numerator 7 is prime and does not divide 8, so the fraction is already in lowest terms. The machine uses \(\boxed{7/8}\) of a fuel cell over the shift.

review.x: Kite: \(+\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}-\tfrac{1}{5}\). lcd for \(2,3,5\) is \(30\). How far above ground, in simplest form?
hints: The denominators are \(2\), \(3\), and \(5\). Since they share no common factor, the smallest bottom that all three divide into is just their product. Rewrite all three fractions over that one shared bottom.
Over a bottom of \(30\) the moves are \(\tfrac{15}{30}\), \(\tfrac{10}{30}\), and \(\tfrac{6}{30}\). Combine the tops with the right signs, \(15 + 10 - 6\), and keep the \(30\).
review.sol: Each move is measured in a different sized piece, so the first job is one shared bottom. The denominators \(2\), \(3\), and \(5\) are all primes with nothing in common, so the smallest number all three divide into is their product, \(2\times 3\times 5 = 30\). This is a case where the least common denominator really is the product, not just the largest denominator. Rewrite each move over \(30\), $$\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{15}{30}, \qquad \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{10}{30}, \qquad \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{6}{30}.$$ Two of the moves go up and one goes down, so combine the tops with those signs over the shared bottom, $$\frac{15}{30} + \frac{10}{30} - \frac{6}{30} = \frac{15 + 10 - 6}{30} = \frac{19}{30}.$$ Since \(19\) is prime and does not divide \(30\), the top and bottom share no factor, so this is already simplest. The kite sits \(\boxed{19/30}\) of the way to the marker.

review.x: A tank starts \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) full; pipes draw \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) then \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). Find \(\tfrac{11}{12}-\tfrac{1}{2}-\tfrac{1}{4}\) over the lcd. What fraction remains, in simplest form?
hints: The denominators are 12, 2, and 4. The smallest number all three divide into is 12, so rewrite every fraction over 12 before you combine anything.
Once everything is in twelfths you have \(\tfrac{11}{12}-\tfrac{6}{12}-\tfrac{3}{12}\). Subtract the top numbers in one sweep, then check whether the result can be reduced.
review.sol: The three fractions live over denominators 12, 2, and 4. Twelve is a multiple of all three, so use it as the single common denominator and convert each fraction once. The \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) is already there. For \(\tfrac{1}{2}\), multiply top and bottom by 6 to get \(\tfrac{6}{12}\). For \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), multiply top and bottom by 3 to get \(\tfrac{3}{12}\). Now the whole chain reads \(\tfrac{11}{12}-\tfrac{6}{12}-\tfrac{3}{12}\). Because the bottoms match, combine the top numbers in one go, \(11-6-3=2\), which gives \(\tfrac{2}{12}\). That is not in lowest terms. Both 2 and 12 share a factor of 2, so divide each by 2 to land on \(\tfrac{1}{6}\). The tank ends the day one sixth full. \(\boxed{1/6}\)

blocks.x: Seven quarter-hops carry you across a creek from \(0\). After \(4\) hops your boot lands on \(1\), and three more put you past \(1\) but short of \(2\). You could call this spot seven quarters, \(\tfrac{7}{4}\), or just as fairly one whole plank and three quarters more, \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\). Same boot, same point, two names that are quietly equal. So how do you write a number that has slipped past a whole without landing on the next one, and how do you flip between the two names?
Your boot is at \(\tfrac{7}{4}\), past \(1\) but short of \(2\). How many whole planks have you completely crossed? Give that count.
hints: A whole plank only counts as finished if your boot is fully past its far end. You sailed past the mark at \(1\), but you stopped before reaching the mark at \(2\), so the second plank is only partly crossed. How many planks did you finish all the way?

Count up the quarter-hops. Four quarter-hops carry you across one whole plank to the mark at \(1\). You took seven hops, which is four to reach \(1\) and three more past it. Those three extra hops have not yet reached \(2\), so only the planks behind the mark at \(1\) are truly finished.
blocks.sol: Each whole plank is split into four quarter-hops, so the marks fall at \(0\), \(\tfrac{4}{4}=1\), and \(\tfrac{8}{4}=2\). Your boot sits on the seventh hop, at \(\tfrac{7}{4}\). To find the finished planks, ask which whole-number mark your boot has fully cleared. The first four hops carry you exactly to the mark at \(1\), finishing one full plank. The next three hops, the fifth, sixth, and seventh, push you out onto the second plank but stop at \(\tfrac{7}{4}\), which is short of the mark at \(2\). So the second plank is only partly crossed and does not count yet. The largest whole-number mark your boot has passed is \(1\), so exactly one plank is finished. That whole \(1\) sitting behind your boot is the whole part of the mixed number. The three leftover quarter-hops are the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), and together they name the very same point as \(\tfrac{7}{4}\), written as the mixed number \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\). The question asks only for the count of finished planks. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: Mixed number. A mixed number like \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\) is a quiet way of writing \(1+\tfrac{3}{4}\), so you scoop up the whole pieces first and then name only the leftover. It is not a new amount. It is the same point as the improper fraction \(\tfrac{7}{4}\), only dressed differently, since bundling pieces into wholes never adds or loses a single hop. And because the whole part sits right out front, a mixed number tells you at a glance that it lives between that whole number and the next one up.

blocks.cap: One dot, two names. The mixed number \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\) wears its neighborhood on its sleeve. It has to sit between \(1\) and \(2\), exactly where \(\tfrac{7}{4}\) already sits. The first whole unit is shaded gold to show one full plank already gathered up, and the leftover \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) carries you three quarter-cells into the next unit, landing on the same point both names share.

blocks.x: A stretch is \(23\) beads; every \(5\) beads make one full band (\(\tfrac{23}{5}\)). Divide \(23\) by \(5\): whole bands and leftover. Write the length as a mixed number.
hints: The bottom number 5 is your group size, so you are really asking how many full fives fit inside 23. Count up in fives, 5, 10, 15, 20, and notice how far you can go before passing 23.
Divide 23 by 5. The quotient is the number of whole bands, and the remainder is the loose beads left over, which sit on top of 5 in the fraction part.
blocks.sol: The fraction \(\tfrac{23}{5}\) means 23 beads cut into groups of 5, so do that division. \(23 \div 5\) gives \(4\) with a remainder, because \(5 \times 4 = 20\) and that leaves \(23 - 20 = 3\) beads still hanging. So 23 beads make 4 complete bands with 3 loose beads dangling past the last one. Those 3 beads are 3 parts of a band that needs 5, so they read as \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) of a band. Stack the whole bands next to that leftover fraction and the length is \(4\tfrac{3}{5}\) bands. A quick check, four full bands is \(4 \times 5 = 20\) beads, plus the 3 loose beads makes \(23\), exactly the strand you started with. $$\frac{23}{5} = 4\tfrac{3}{5}$$ \(\boxed{4\tfrac{3}{5}}\)

blocks.x: Improper fraction to mixed number. A fraction bar has always meant divide, so to read \(\tfrac{23}{5}\) as a mixed number, divide \(23\) by \(5\). The quotient counts the whole groups you finished, and that number stands out front, while the remainder counts the pieces that could not fill another group and stays over the same bottom, because no piece ever changed size. You only sorted the pieces into full bundles and a leftover, so \(\tfrac{23}{5}\) and \(4\tfrac{3}{5}\) are one place on the line. When the remainder lands on zero, the bundles came out even and you were holding a whole number all along.
Order slip: \(3\tfrac{2}{7}\) bands. Three whole bands = \(21\) beads; add \(2\) loose. Write \(3\tfrac{2}{7}\) as an improper fraction in sevenths.
hints: A whole band is not really one thing to the machine, it is 7 beads. So how many single beads hide inside the 3 whole bands before you even touch the loose ones?
Three whole bands are \(3\times 7=21\) beads. Add the 2 loose beads to get the total bead count, then write that total over 7, since each bead is one seventh of a band.
blocks.sol: The trick is to stop seeing the wholes as wholes and start counting the actual beads inside them. The machine measures everything in single beads, and a single bead is one seventh of a band, so I want one total bead count sitting over 7. Each whole band is 7 beads, and there are 3 of them, so the wholes carry \(3\times 7=21\) beads. On top of that sit the 2 loose beads. Altogether that is \(21+2=23\) beads. Every one of those beads is a seventh of a band, so the length is \(\dfrac{23}{7}\) of a band. As a quick sanity check, \(23\div 7\) is 3 with a remainder of 2, which lands right back on \(3\tfrac{2}{7}\), exactly where we started. $$3\tfrac{2}{7}=\frac{3\times 7+2}{7}=\frac{21+2}{7}=\boxed{\dfrac{23}{7}}$$

blocks.x: Converting both ways. Both moves are just bundling run forward or in reverse, so neither one moves the point. To read an improper fraction as a mixed number you divide the top by the bottom, and the quotient is how many whole groups you bundled while the leftover stays as a fraction over the same bottom. To go the other way you smash each whole back into pieces, so a whole is worth bottom-many pieces, and \(\text{whole}\times\text{bottom}+\text{top}\) counts every piece from \(0\) over that same bottom. Same spot on the line, two ways of saying it, one that gathers pieces into wholes and one that counts every piece.

blocks.cap: Going right you bundle loose thirds into whole groups of three, going left you smash each whole back into three loose thirds. It is the same fourteen pieces the whole time, just written two ways, \(\tfrac{14}{3}=4\tfrac{2}{3}\). The four full rings are the whole part and the two stragglers are the fraction. Carrying and borrowing later are just this picture run in the two directions.

blocks.x: A lookout sits at \(\tfrac{47}{6}\) miles. Convert to a mixed number. Give the nearest post behind the hiker, the largest whole number below \(\tfrac{47}{6}\).
hints: A mixed number is really a division in disguise. How many whole groups of \(6\) fit inside \(47\), and how much is left over? That whole count is the milepost she has already passed.

Divide \(47\) by \(6\). It goes in \(7\) times because \(6\times 7 = 42\), with \(5\) left over, so \(\frac{47}{6} = 7\tfrac{5}{6}\). The \(7\) is the whole part, so the lookout sits past post \(7\) but not yet at post \(8\).
blocks.sol: Start by asking how many full miles are buried inside \(\frac{47}{6}\). Each whole mile is \(\frac{6}{6}\), so the real question is how many \(6\)'s fit into \(47\). Since \(6\times 7 = 42\) and \(6\times 8 = 48\) would overshoot, exactly \(7\) whole miles fit, and \(47 - 42 = 5\) sixths are left over. That gives \(\frac{47}{6} = 7\tfrac{5}{6}\). The whole part \(7\) tells you she has fully passed post \(7\), and the leftover \(\frac{5}{6}\) of a mile means she has not yet reached post \(8\). So the lookout sits between posts \(7\) and \(8\), and the largest whole number still less than \(\frac{47}{6}\) is the post right behind her. $$\frac{47}{6} = 7\tfrac{5}{6}\quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{7}$$

blocks.x: In \(7\tfrac{5}{6}\) the space means "and" (\(+\)), a surveyors' shorthand. Once algebra arrives, juxtaposition means multiply (\(2x\)), so mixed-number notation is quietly retired.
Sensor at \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\): the minus covers the whole quantity, so the sensor is a third below \(-7\). Give \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\) as an improper fraction.
hints: First locate where 7 1/3 lives on the positive side. It sits a third of the way past 7, between 7 and 8. Now reflect that whole spot across 0. Reflecting flips left and right, so whatever was just past 7 going up lands just past -7 going down.

The minus out front belongs to the entire amount, so -7 1/3 means -(7 1/3) = -(7 + 1/3) = -7 - 1/3. That is a third below -7, landing between -8 and -7. To get the improper fraction, write 7 1/3 = (7 times 3 + 1)/3 = 22/3, then attach the minus.
blocks.sol: The trap is treating the minus as if it only touches the \(7\) and leaves \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) positive. It does not. The minus sign belongs to the whole amount, so \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\) is shorthand for \(-\left(7\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = -\left(7 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right) = -7 - \tfrac{1}{3}\). Notice that second sign flipped too. So the sensor sits a third below \(-7\), not above it. The attendant marked it a third toward \(-6\), which is the wrong direction. On the line, \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\) lands between \(-8\) and \(-7\), a little past \(-7\) on the way down. A clean way to see it is by mirroring. The point \(7\tfrac{1}{3}\) sits a third past \(7\) heading up toward \(8\). Reflect that across \(0\) and up becomes down, so it must sit a third past \(-7\) heading down toward \(-8\). For the exact value, first build the improper fraction from the positive part. \(7\tfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{7 \times 3 + 1}{3} = \dfrac{22}{3}\). The mirror image across \(0\) just attaches the minus, giving \(-\dfrac{22}{3}\). As a check, \(-\dfrac{22}{3} = -7.333\ldots\), which is indeed a third below \(-7\). $$-7\tfrac{1}{3} = -\left(7 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right) = -\frac{22}{3}$$ $$\boxed{-\dfrac{22}{3}}$$

blocks.x: Negative mixed numbers. A minus sign in front of a mixed number grabs the whole quantity, not just the whole part, so \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\) means \(-7-\tfrac{1}{3}\), a third below \(-7\) and resting between \(-8\) and \(-7\). The safe move is to find where the positive version \(7\tfrac{1}{3}\) sits, then mirror that whole distance across \(0\), or just hang the minus on the single improper fraction. That keeps \(-7\tfrac{1}{3}\) from getting confused with \(-7+\tfrac{1}{3}\), a completely different number living between \(-7\) and \(-6\).
Potter uses \(2\tfrac{5}{6}+1\tfrac{5}{6}\) scoops. The fraction parts sum past \(1\), so carry a whole. Total scoops as a mixed number?
hints: Keep the two columns apart for a moment. The whole scoops add to \(2+1=3\). Now add the sixths on their own, \(\tfrac{5}{6}+\tfrac{5}{6}\). That fraction sum is going to be bigger than \(1\), which is the whole point of this problem.

\(\tfrac{5}{6}+\tfrac{5}{6}=\tfrac{10}{6}\), and \(\tfrac{10}{6}\) is one whole scoop plus \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) left over, which simplifies to \(1\tfrac{2}{3}\). Hand that extra whole scoop up to the whole count. You started with \(3\) wholes, the carry makes it \(4\), and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of a scoop stays behind.
blocks.sol: Split each mixed number into its whole part and its fraction part and add the matching kinds together. The whole scoops give \(2+1=3\). The fraction parts give \(\tfrac{5}{6}+\tfrac{5}{6}=\tfrac{10}{6}\). So far the total looks like \(3\) wholes and \(\tfrac{10}{6}\), but \(\tfrac{10}{6}\) is more than a whole scoop, so it is not finished. Pull the whole scoop out of \(\tfrac{10}{6}\). Since \(\tfrac{6}{6}\) is exactly one scoop, \(\tfrac{10}{6}=\tfrac{6}{6}+\tfrac{4}{6}=1\tfrac{4}{6}\), and \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) simplifies to \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), so \(\tfrac{10}{6}=1\tfrac{2}{3}\). That hidden whole scoop has to leave the fraction column and join the whole count. The \(3\) wholes become \(3+1=4\) wholes, and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of a scoop stays behind. The total is \(4\tfrac{2}{3}\) scoops, which you can check as the improper fraction \(\tfrac{17}{6}+\tfrac{11}{6}=\tfrac{28}{6}=\tfrac{14}{3}=4\tfrac{2}{3}\). What you just did has a name, you carried, exactly like carrying a ten in column addition, except here a full group of \(\tfrac{6}{6}\) carries instead of a full group of ten. \(\boxed{4\tfrac{2}{3}\text{ scoops}}\)

blocks.x: Rail is \(5\tfrac{1}{4}\) spans; cut \(2\tfrac{3}{4}\). \(\tfrac{1}{4}<\tfrac{3}{4}\): trade one whole for \(4\) quarters. Finish and give what remains as a mixed number.
hints: The trade does not change how much rail there is, it just changes how it is written. One whole span is the same as \(\tfrac{4}{4}\), so trading a whole into quarters turns \(5\) wholes and \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) into \(4\) wholes and a bigger pile of quarters. How many quarters are in that pile now?

After the trade you are looking at \(4\tfrac{5}{4}\) minus \(2\tfrac{3}{4}\). Subtract the quarters first, \(\tfrac{5}{4}-\tfrac{3}{4}\), then subtract the wholes, \(4-2\). Put the two results together and simplify the fraction.
blocks.sol: The plan is sound, the only trouble is that the quarters column will not subtract as written, since \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) is less than \(\tfrac{3}{4}\). So we rewrite the rail without changing its length. One whole span is exactly \(\tfrac{4}{4}\), so take a single whole out of the \(5\) and turn it into quarters. That leaves \(4\) wholes, and the quarters become \(\tfrac{4}{4}+\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{5}{4}\). In other words \(5\tfrac{1}{4}\) and \(4\tfrac{5}{4}\) are two names for the same amount of rail. Now the columns cooperate. Quarters give \(\tfrac{5}{4}-\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{2}{4}\), and wholes give \(4-2=2\), so what remains is \(2\tfrac{2}{4}\). Halving top and bottom of \(\tfrac{2}{4}\) gives \(\tfrac{1}{2}\), so the leftover rail is $$2\tfrac{2}{4}=\boxed{2\tfrac{1}{2}}$$ hand-spans.

blocks.x: Mixed number arithmetic. Adding or subtracting mixed numbers is two small jobs side by side, the wholes with the wholes and the fraction parts with the fraction parts, once the fractions share a bottom. If the fraction parts add up past one whole, that spare whole climbs into the whole count and only the leftover fraction stays put, so \(2\tfrac{5}{6}+3\tfrac{5}{6}\) becomes \(5\) and \(\tfrac{5}{3}\), which is \(6\tfrac{2}{3}\). If you have to take away more fraction than you own, you break one whole back into pieces and hand them down, turning a whole into something like \(\tfrac{4}{4}\) so the subtraction can finish. Carrying and borrowing are the same bundling trade, one read forward and the other backward.
Legs: \(1\tfrac{1}{2}+2\tfrac{1}{3}\) laps. Halves and thirds differ; find lcd, rename fractions, then add. Check for carry. Total as a mixed number?
hints: Split each amount into its whole part and its fraction part. The wholes are \(1\) and \(2\), so those give \(3\) laps right away. The fractions \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) cannot be added as they stand because the pieces are different sizes, so first find a denominator that both \(2\) and \(3\) divide into.

Use \(6\) as the shared bottom. Rename \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{2}{6}\), then add to get \(\tfrac{3}{6}+\tfrac{2}{6}=\tfrac{5}{6}\). Since \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is less than one whole lap, there is nothing to carry, so just attach it to the \(3\) whole laps.
blocks.sol: Pull each mixed number apart into a whole part and a fraction part. The wholes are \(1\) and \(2\), and \(1+2=3\) laps. The fraction parts are \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}\), and these are pieces of different sizes, so they cannot be combined until they share a bottom number. The smallest number that both \(2\) and \(3\) divide into is \(6\), so rename each one over sixths. A half is three of those sixths, \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{6}\), and a third is two of them, \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{2}{6}\). Now the pieces match, so \(\tfrac{3}{6}+\tfrac{2}{6}=\tfrac{5}{6}\). Check whether this spills past a whole lap. Since \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is still under \(1\), there is no extra whole to carry over, so the \(3\) whole laps stay as \(3\). Putting the parts back together gives \(3\) whole laps and \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of a lap, which is \(3\tfrac{5}{6}\) laps. As a single improper fraction that is \(\tfrac{23}{6}\), a handy way to confirm the work. \(\boxed{3\tfrac{5}{6}}\)

blocks.x: \(4\) planters each need \(2\tfrac{5}{8}\) bags. Convert to an improper fraction, multiply by \(4\), convert back. Total bags as a mixed number?
hints: Rewrite \(2\tfrac{5}{8}\) as a single improper fraction before you touch the \(4\). Eighths times eighths stays in eighths, so the multiply step is clean.
\(2\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{21}{8}\). Now \(4\times\tfrac{21}{8}=\tfrac{84}{8}\). Simplify that fraction, then split it into a whole number and a remaining fraction.
blocks.sol: First fold the mixed number into a single fraction. The whole part \(2\) is \(\tfrac{16}{8}\), and adding the \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) gives \(2\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{21}{8}\). Now one box is just \(\tfrac{21}{8}\) bags, with no separate pieces to keep track of. Multiply by the \(4\) boxes, $$4\times\frac{21}{8}=\frac{84}{8}.$$ That fraction simplifies. Dividing top and bottom by \(4\) gives \(\tfrac{84}{8}=\tfrac{21}{2}\), and \(\tfrac{21}{2}\) is \(10\) with \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) left over, so the total is \(10\tfrac{1}{2}\) bags. Here is why the separate-pieces road is dangerous. Multiplying gives \(4\times 2=8\) whole bags and \(4\times\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{20}{8}=2\tfrac{1}{2}\) more bags. The fraction part did not stay a fraction, it ballooned into another \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\) bags, and writing the answer as "eight and two and a half" tangled together is begging for a slip. Converting to an improper fraction first never lets that surprise sneak up on you. The four boxes hold \(\boxed{10\tfrac{1}{2}}\) bags of soil.

blocks.x: Full day: \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) km; half-staffed day gets \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of that. Convert, multiply by \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), convert back. How many km, as a mixed number?
hints: You cannot reliably take \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of \(3\) and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) separately and trust it. Turn \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) into a single improper fraction first. Three wholes is \(\tfrac{12}{4}\), so \(3\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{15}{4}\). Now you just owe yourself \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of \(\tfrac{15}{4}\).

Multiply straight across, top times top and bottom times bottom: \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{15}{4}=\tfrac{2\times 15}{3\times 4}=\tfrac{30}{12}\). Now reduce \(\tfrac{30}{12}\) by its biggest common factor, then split that improper fraction into a whole number and a leftover fraction.
blocks.sol: First clear the mixed number out of the way, because the multiply rule wants a plain fraction. The three whole kilometres are \(\tfrac{12}{4}\), and adding the extra \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) gives \(3\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{15}{4}\). Now take \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of that by multiplying straight across, \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{15}{4}=\tfrac{2\times 15}{3\times 4}=\tfrac{30}{12}\). That fraction is honest but bulky, so reduce it. The greatest common factor of \(30\) and \(12\) is \(6\), and \(\tfrac{30}{12}=\tfrac{5}{2}\). Finally turn \(\tfrac{5}{2}\) back into a mixed number, since the question asks for kilometres in that shape. Two halves make one whole and there are five halves in all, so that is two wholes with one half left over, giving \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\). A quick sanity check fits the story too, half a staff should clear a bit more than half of \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\), and \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\) is comfortably in that range. The half-staffed day clears $$\boxed{2\tfrac{1}{2}}$$ kilometres.

blocks.x: Multiplying and dividing mixed numbers. When you multiply two mixed numbers, the whole parts and the fraction parts do not stay neatly separated the way they did with adding. Every piece of one number has to meet every piece of the other, so you cannot just multiply the wholes, multiply the fractions, and stick the answers back together. The reliable move is to drop the mixed form first, rewrite each number as a single improper fraction, and then multiply straight across, or flip the second one and multiply to divide. After that you can fold the result back into a mixed number if the question wants it that way.
Ribbon \(4\tfrac{1}{2}\) m; bows are \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) m each. Convert, divide using flip-and-multiply. How many whole bows?
hints: Before you can divide, the mixed number has to become a single fraction. Four and a half metres is the same as \(\tfrac{9}{2}\) metres, since two halves make each metre and there are nine halves in all. Now you are dividing \(\tfrac{9}{2}\) by \(\tfrac{3}{4}\).

Dividing by \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) means multiplying by its flip, \(\tfrac{4}{3}\). So compute \(\tfrac{9}{2}\times\tfrac{4}{3}\). Multiply the tops, multiply the bottoms, then simplify and read off the whole number of pieces.
blocks.sol: Start by turning the mixed number into a single fraction so the division rule can act on it. Four and a half metres is \(4\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{9}{2}\), because each whole metre is two halves and four and a half metres hold nine halves. The question \(4\tfrac{1}{2}\div\tfrac{3}{4}\) is really asking how many groups of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) fit inside \(\tfrac{9}{2}\). Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal, so flip \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) to get \(\tfrac{4}{3}\) and multiply. $$\frac{9}{2}\div\frac{3}{4}=\frac{9}{2}\times\frac{4}{3}=\frac{9\times 4}{2\times 3}=\frac{36}{6}=6.$$ The answer comes out to a clean whole number, which tells you something nice. The ribbon splits into exactly six \(\tfrac{3}{4}\)-metre pieces with nothing left over. You can check it by going the other way: six pieces of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) metre give \(6\times\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{18}{4}=\tfrac{9}{2}=4\tfrac{1}{2}\) metres, which matches the ribbon you started with. So the count is the number of pieces, and here that count is whole and exact. \(\boxed{6}\)

blocks.x: A sign maker uses \(1\tfrac{5}{8}\) m of ribbon per letter for a \(4\)-letter word, starting from a \(7\) m roll. Convert, multiply, subtract. How many metres of ribbon are left on the roll?
hints: Turn \(1\tfrac{5}{8}\) into eighths first, since every letter uses the same amount. Four equal letters means you can multiply that single fraction by \(4\) instead of adding it four times.

One letter is \(\tfrac{13}{8}\) metres, so four letters use \(4\times\tfrac{13}{8}=\tfrac{52}{8}\) metres. Simplify that, then write \(7\) as a fraction over the same denominator so you can subtract cleanly.
blocks.sol: Start by rewriting the amount one letter uses. A whole is \(\tfrac{8}{8}\), so \(1\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{8}{8}+\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{13}{8}\) metres per letter. Working in improper form keeps the multiplying tidy. Since all four letters are identical, you do not add four separate pieces. You multiply once. \(4\times\tfrac{13}{8}=\tfrac{4\times 13}{8}=\tfrac{52}{8}\). Now simplify, because \(52\) and \(8\) share a factor of \(4\). \(\tfrac{52}{8}=\tfrac{13}{2}=6\tfrac{1}{2}\) metres of ribbon used by the whole word. The last step is the subtraction, and this is where it pays to match denominators rather than guess. Write the full roll as \(7=\tfrac{14}{2}\), then \(\tfrac{14}{2}-\tfrac{13}{2}=\tfrac{1}{2}\) metre. So half a metre is left on the roll. And that answers the maker's real question. Another letter needs \(1\tfrac{5}{8}\) metres, and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) is far short of that, so no extra letter fits. $$7-4\times 1\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{14}{2}-\tfrac{13}{2}=\boxed{\tfrac{1}{2}}\text{ metre}$$

review.x: An anchor leg is logged as \(\tfrac{29}{6}\) of a segment. Rewrite \(\tfrac{29}{6}\) as a mixed number in simplest form.
hints: A mixed number asks how many whole groups of \(6\) sixths fit inside \(29\) sixths, with whatever is left over staying as a fraction. Try dividing \(29\) by \(6\) and watching the remainder.
Since \(6 \times 4 = 24\), four whole units use up \(24\) of the \(29\) sixths. That leaves \(29 - 24 = 5\) sixths still sitting over the bar.
review.sol: An improper fraction packs more than a whole into a single stack over the bar, so converting to a mixed number just means pulling out the whole groups. Each whole unit is \(\tfrac{6}{6}\), so I ask how many sixes fit into \(29\). Dividing, \(29 \div 6 = 4\) with a remainder of \(5\), because \(6 \times 4 = 24\) and \(29 - 24 = 5\). The quotient \(4\) is the whole-number part, and the remainder \(5\) stays over the original denominator as \(\tfrac{5}{6}\). The fraction \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is already in simplest form since \(\gcd(5,6)=1\). Putting the pieces together gives four wholes and five sixths. As a quick check, \(4 + \tfrac{5}{6} = \tfrac{24}{6} + \tfrac{5}{6} = \tfrac{29}{6}\), which matches what we started with. \(\boxed{4\tfrac{5}{6}}\)

review.x: A staircase rises \(6\tfrac{2}{5}\) full turns. Rewrite \(6\tfrac{2}{5}\) as a single improper fraction in fifths.
hints: Each whole turn is five fifths, so first ask how many fifths live inside the 6 whole turns alone.
Multiply the whole number by the denominator, then add the numerator. Keep the denominator 5 the same: \(6\times 5 + 2\) goes on top.
review.sol: A mixed number is just a whole part plus a fraction part, so I want to turn the whole part into fifths and combine. Each full turn is \(\tfrac{5}{5}\), and there are 6 of them, which gives \(6\times 5 = 30\) fifths. The fraction already carries another 2 fifths, so altogether I have \(30 + 2 = 32\) fifths. That is the shortcut you see written as \(6\times 5 + 2 = 32\) on top, with the denominator 5 left unchanged underneath. So \(6\tfrac{2}{5} = \dfrac{32}{5}\), and a quick check confirms it since \(32\div 5 = 6.4 = 6\tfrac{2}{5}\). \(\boxed{\tfrac{32}{5}}\)

review.x: A runner covers \(\tfrac{38}{7}\) laps. \(\tfrac{38}{7}\) falls between two consecutive whole numbers. What is the smaller of those two whole numbers?
hints: Rewrite \(\tfrac{38}{7}\) as a mixed number. Ask how many whole sevens go into \(38\), and what is left over.
Since \(7\times 5=35\) and \(7\times 6=42\), the value \(\tfrac{38}{7}\) lands between \(5\) and \(6\). The smaller of those is your answer.
review.sol: Turn the improper fraction into a mixed number by seeing how many whole sevens fit inside \(38\). Since \(7\times 5=35\) and \(7\times 6=42\), five sevens fit with \(3\) left over, so \(\tfrac{38}{7}=5\tfrac{3}{7}\). That whole part of \(5\) tells you the value has passed \(5\) but has not reached \(6\), because the extra \(\tfrac{3}{7}\) is more than nothing yet less than a full \(1\). So \(\tfrac{38}{7}\) sits between the consecutive whole numbers \(5\) and \(6\). The runner has finished \(5\) complete laps and is partway through her sixth. The smaller of the two is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: A diver's tag reads \(-3\tfrac{2}{5}\) m: the minus grabs the whole amount, so this is \(-3-\tfrac{2}{5}\). Rewrite \(-3\tfrac{2}{5}\) as a single improper fraction.
hints: The minus sign flips the whole reading, not just the 3. Treat \(-3\tfrac{2}{5}\) as \(-\left(3\tfrac{2}{5}\right)\), so first turn the positive \(3\tfrac{2}{5}\) into an improper fraction, then attach the minus.
To convert \(3\tfrac{2}{5}\), multiply the whole number by the denominator and add the numerator. That gives \(3\times 5 + 2\) over \(5\). Keep the negative sign on the final result.
review.sol:
The tricky part is the sign. The reading \(-3\tfrac{2}{5}\) is not three and two fifths with a stray minus on the 3. It is the negative of the whole amount, \(-\left(3\tfrac{2}{5}\right)\), which is why we can read it as \(-3 - \tfrac{2}{5}\).

So I convert the positive piece first, then carry the minus along. To turn \(3\tfrac{2}{5}\) into an improper fraction, I count how many fifths I have. Each whole is \(5\) fifths, so \(3\) wholes are \(15\) fifths, and the extra \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) brings it to \(17\) fifths.

$$3\tfrac{2}{5} = \frac{3\times 5 + 2}{5} = \frac{17}{5}$$

Now I put the negative sign back on the whole thing, giving \(-\tfrac{17}{5}\). A quick sanity check: \(\tfrac{17}{5}\) is between \(3\) and \(4\), so \(-\tfrac{17}{5}\) sits between \(-4\) and \(-3\), exactly where a depth of about \(-3.4\) metres should land.

\(\boxed{-\tfrac{17}{5}}\)

review.x: A flag is planted at \(\frac{23}{4}\) on a number line. What is the largest whole number to the left of the flag?
hints: Rewrite \(\frac{23}{4}\) as a mixed number first. How many whole groups of \(\frac{4}{4}\) fit inside \(\frac{23}{4}\)?
Once you see \(\frac{23}{4}=5\tfrac{3}{4}\), the flag sits between two whole numbers. You want the whole number just to its left, not the one to its right.
review.sol: Turn the improper fraction into a mixed number by dividing. Since \(23 \div 4 = 5\) with a remainder of \(3\), we get \(\frac{23}{4}=5\tfrac{3}{4}\). So the flag lands three quarters of the way past \(5\), somewhere between \(5\) and \(6\). The whole numbers smaller than \(5\tfrac{3}{4}\) are \(5, 4, 3,\) and so on, and the largest of those is the one right below the flag. That whole number is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: A runner starts at a trail marker \(3\tfrac{4}{5}\) km from start, then jogs \(2\tfrac{3}{5}\) km more. How far from start is the runner? Give as a mixed number in simplest form.
hints: Add the whole numbers and the fifths in two separate piles. The wholes give \(3+2=5\). Now handle the fifths on their own.
The fifths add to \(\tfrac{4}{5}+\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{7}{5}\), which is more than one whole. Rewrite \(\tfrac{7}{5}\) as \(1\tfrac{2}{5}\) and carry that extra whole over to your pile of wholes.
review.sol: Split each mixed number into its whole part and its fraction part, then add like with like. The wholes give \(3+2=5\). The fifths give \(\tfrac{4}{5}+\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{7}{5}\). Here is the moment to watch. Seven fifths is already bigger than one whole, so it does not belong sitting next to the fives as a leftover fraction. Trade it in. Since \(\tfrac{7}{5}=\tfrac{5}{5}+\tfrac{2}{5}=1\tfrac{2}{5}\), you carry one whole across. Now the wholes become \(5+1=6\) and the honest leftover is \(\tfrac{2}{5}\). Putting them back together, the runner is \(6\tfrac{2}{5}\) kilometers from the start. As a check, \(3\tfrac{4}{5}=\tfrac{19}{5}\) and \(2\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{13}{5}\), and \(\tfrac{19}{5}+\tfrac{13}{5}=\tfrac{32}{5}=6\tfrac{2}{5}\). \(\boxed{6\tfrac{2}{5}}\)

review.x: Evaluate \(6\tfrac{1}{3}-2\tfrac{2}{3}\) and write the result as a mixed number. The thirds will not subtract cleanly, so you will need to borrow one whole from the \(6\). What is the value?
hints: Line up the wholes and the thirds. You are asked to take away \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), but the top number only has \(\tfrac{1}{3}\), so the fractions do not subtract on their own. That is your signal to borrow.

Borrow one whole from the \(6\). That whole is \(\tfrac{3}{3}\), so add it to the \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) you already have. Now \(6\tfrac{1}{3}\) is rewritten as \(5\tfrac{4}{3}\), and \(\tfrac{4}{3}\) is big enough to subtract \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) from.
review.sol: The thirds are the trouble. We want \(\tfrac{1}{3}-\tfrac{2}{3}\), but \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) is smaller than \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), so there is nothing clean to take away. Instead of stopping, we borrow. Take one whole out of the \(6\), which leaves \(5\), and turn that whole into \(\tfrac{3}{3}\). Stacking it onto the \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) gives \(\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{3}{3}=\tfrac{4}{3}\). So \(6\tfrac{1}{3}\) is the very same amount as \(5\tfrac{4}{3}\). Now everything subtracts in order. The wholes give \(5-2=3\), and the thirds give \(\tfrac{4}{3}-\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{2}{3}\). Putting the pieces together leaves \(3\tfrac{2}{3}\). A quick sanity check with improper fractions agrees, since \(\tfrac{19}{3}-\tfrac{8}{3}=\tfrac{11}{3}=3\tfrac{2}{3}\). \(\boxed{3\tfrac{2}{3}}\)

review.x: Two legs: \(1\tfrac{1}{2}\) miles and \(2\tfrac{1}{4}\) miles. Line up fraction parts over the lcd before adding. What is the total distance as a mixed number?
hints: The bottoms are 2 and 4, and 4 is a common bottom for both. Rewrite \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) as \(\tfrac{2}{4}\) so both fraction parts share the same bottom.
Add the whole numbers and the fourths separately. You get \(1+2=3\) wholes and \(\tfrac{2}{4}+\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{4}\). Since \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) is still under one whole, there is nothing to carry.
review.sol: First give both fraction parts the same bottom. Since \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{2}{4}\), the sum becomes \(1\tfrac{2}{4}+2\tfrac{1}{4}\). Now add the whole parts and the fraction parts on their own. The wholes give \(1+2=3\), and the fourths give \(\tfrac{2}{4}+\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{3}{4}\). That fraction part is less than one whole, so there is no extra whole to carry over. Putting the pieces back together gives \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) miles, which is \(\tfrac{15}{4}\) as an improper fraction. \(\boxed{3\tfrac{3}{4}}\)

review.x: Six planters each need \(1\tfrac{2}{3}\) bags of mulch. Convert to an improper fraction, then multiply by \(6\). How many bags total?
hints: Rewrite the mixed number as a single fraction. Since \(1\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{3}{3}+\tfrac{2}{3}\), it becomes \(\tfrac{5}{3}\). Now you just need \(6\times\tfrac{5}{3}\).
Write the whole number as \(\tfrac{6}{1}\) and multiply across the tops and bottoms, then simplify. Watch what \(\tfrac{30}{3}\) reduces to.
review.sol: Start by turning the mixed number into one fraction so the multiplication is clean. One whole is \(\tfrac{3}{3}\), so \(1\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{3}{3}+\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{5}{3}\). Now multiply by the six planters. Treat \(6\) as \(\tfrac{6}{1}\) and multiply straight across, \(6\times\tfrac{5}{3}=\tfrac{6\times 5}{1\times 3}=\tfrac{30}{3}\). Finally simplify, and since \(30\div 3=10\) the fraction collapses to a whole number. The improper fraction was the shortcut here, it let the six and the three cancel into a tidy count instead of adding \(1\tfrac{2}{3}\) six separate times. The row of planters needs \(\boxed{10}\) bags of mulch.

review.x: One press pass uses \(2\tfrac{2}{3}\) oz of ink; a proof run needs \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of a pass. Convert, then multiply. How many ounces does the proof run use?
hints: A mixed number is easier to multiply once it becomes a single fraction. Rewrite \(2\tfrac{2}{3}\) as an improper fraction by computing \(2\times 3 + 2\) over \(3\).
After you get \(\tfrac{3}{4}\times\tfrac{8}{3}\), multiply straight across to \(\tfrac{24}{12}\), then simplify. Watch for the whole number hiding inside that fraction.
review.sol: Start by turning the mixed number into a single fraction. The whole number 2 is \(\tfrac{6}{3}\), and adding the \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) gives \(2\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{8}{3}\). Now the problem is a clean product of two fractions, \(\tfrac{3}{4}\times\tfrac{8}{3}\). Multiplying across the tops and across the bottoms gives \(\tfrac{3\times 8}{4\times 3}=\tfrac{24}{12}\). That fraction is really a whole number in disguise, since \(24\div 12=2\). So the proof run uses \(\boxed{2}\) ounces of ink.

review.x: A ribbon is \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) m. Cut into \(1\tfrac{1}{2}\) m pieces. Convert both to improper fractions, divide, write the result as a mixed number. How many pieces fit?
hints: Rewrite each mixed number as an improper fraction. Since \(3\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{15}{4}\) and \(1\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{3}{2}\), you are really computing \(\tfrac{15}{4}\div\tfrac{3}{2}\).
To divide by a fraction, multiply by its reciprocal. Flip \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) to \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) and multiply, then simplify before turning the answer back into a mixed number.
review.sol: First change both mixed numbers into improper fractions. For \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\), multiply the whole part by the denominator and add the top, giving \(3\times 4+3=15\), so it is \(\tfrac{15}{4}\). For \(1\tfrac{1}{2}\), the same move gives \(1\times 2+1=3\), so it is \(\tfrac{3}{2}\). Now the problem is \(\tfrac{15}{4}\div\tfrac{3}{2}\). Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal, so flip the second fraction and multiply. $$\frac{15}{4}\div\frac{3}{2}=\frac{15}{4}\times\frac{2}{3}=\frac{30}{12}=\frac{5}{2}$$ Finally, write \(\tfrac{5}{2}\) as a mixed number. Two goes into five twice with one left over, so it is \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\). That matches the picture too, since two full pieces use \(3\) meters and the leftover \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) meter is exactly half of a \(1\tfrac{1}{2}\) meter piece. \(\boxed{2\tfrac{1}{2}}\)

review.x: One loop lap is \(4\tfrac{2}{3}\) km; two runners split it equally. How long is each leg? Give as a mixed number in km.
hints: Splitting a lap into two equal legs means dividing the lap length by 2. First rewrite \(4\tfrac{2}{3}\) as an improper fraction so you have something clean to divide.
Dividing by 2 is the same as multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Multiply, then simplify the fraction before you turn it back into a mixed number.
review.sol:
Start by turning the mixed number into an improper fraction. Three thirds make one whole, so four wholes hold twelve thirds, and adding the extra two thirds gives \(4\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{14}{3}\).

Now split that lap into two equal legs by dividing by 2. Dividing by 2 is the same as multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{2}\), so \(\tfrac{14}{3}\div 2=\tfrac{14}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{14}{6}\).

Both 14 and 6 share a factor of 2, so \(\tfrac{14}{6}=\tfrac{7}{3}\). Since three goes into seven twice with one left over, each leg is \(2\tfrac{1}{3}\) kilometers long. As a quick check, two legs of \(2\tfrac{1}{3}\) add to \(4\tfrac{2}{3}\), the whole lap, so the split is right.

\(\boxed{2\tfrac{1}{3}\text{ km}}\)

review.x: A full batch needs \(2\tfrac{1}{4}\) cups of flour. You make \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of a batch. How many cups do you need? Give as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: A fraction of a recipe means multiplication, so you want \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of \(2\tfrac{1}{4}\). Multiplying a mixed number is much easier once you turn it into an improper fraction first.
Rewrite \(2\tfrac{1}{4}\) as \(\tfrac{9}{4}\), then multiply \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times\tfrac{9}{4}\) by multiplying the tops and the bottoms. Do not forget to simplify the result at the end.
review.sol:
Making \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of a batch means taking \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of the flour, and the word "of" here is a signal to multiply. So you need \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times 2\tfrac{1}{4}\).

Mixed numbers are awkward to multiply directly, so convert first. The whole number \(2\) is the same as \(\tfrac{8}{4}\), and adding the \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) gives \(2\tfrac{1}{4}=\tfrac{9}{4}\).

Now multiply straight across, top times top and bottom times bottom.
$$\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{9}{4}=\frac{1\times 9}{3\times 4}=\frac{9}{12}.$$

Both \(9\) and \(12\) share a factor of \(3\), so \(\tfrac{9}{12}=\tfrac{3}{4}\). You need three quarters of a cup of flour, which makes sense since a third of a batch should use a good deal less than the full \(2\tfrac{1}{4}\) cups.

\(\boxed{\tfrac{3}{4}}\)

review.x: A board is \(5\tfrac{1}{2}\) ft. Two side brackets, each \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\) ft, are cut off. How many feet of board remain?
hints: First find the total length the builder cuts off. Two pieces of \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\) feet each means you double \(1\tfrac{3}{4}\), so write it as \(\tfrac{7}{4}\) and multiply by \(2\).
Once you know the two pieces use up \(3\tfrac{1}{2}\) feet, subtract that from the \(5\tfrac{1}{2}\) foot board. The half on each side cancels neatly, so look at the whole numbers.
review.sol: Start with the two pieces the builder removes. Each is \(1\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{7}{4}\) feet, and there are two of them, so together they use \(2\times\tfrac{7}{4}=\tfrac{14}{4}=\tfrac{7}{2}=3\tfrac{1}{2}\) feet. Now subtract that from the original board. The board is \(5\tfrac{1}{2}\) feet, so what remains is \(5\tfrac{1}{2}-3\tfrac{1}{2}\). The two halves match and cancel, leaving \(5-3=2\) feet. So \(2\) feet of board are left over. \(\boxed{2}\)

blocks.x: Two climbers sit at \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{8}{11}\) of the way up. Eight is more than seven, so the second seems ahead, but elevenths are thinner slivers than ninths, so it is stacking more pieces that are each smaller. More thin pieces against fewer fat pieces is a genuine standoff. This lesson builds a small kit of moves for fights exactly like this, cross-multiplying, matching numerators, and leaning on landmarks, and the real skill is reaching for the one move that turns a given standoff into something you can just read.
North crew: \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) of a path. East crew: \(\tfrac{7}{12}\). Rewrite over a common denominator, find which crew leads, then report the gap as a single fraction in simplest form.
hints: A fraction only tells you "how many pieces" once you know the pieces are the same size. Eighths and twelfths are not, so find one denominator that both \(8\) and \(12\) divide into and rewrite each fraction with it. The smallest one that works is \(24\).

Scale each fraction to twenty-fourths. Multiply \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) by \(\tfrac{3}{3}\) to get \(\tfrac{15}{24}\), and multiply \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) by \(\tfrac{2}{2}\) to get \(\tfrac{14}{24}\). Now both are counts of the same twenty-fourths, so the bigger numerator is farther along. Subtract the smaller count from the larger to find the gap.
blocks.sol: The trap here is comparing \(5\) against \(7\) directly. Those numbers count pieces, but an eighth of the path and a twelfth of the path are not the same length, so the counts are not yet on speaking terms. The fix is to chop both into identical pieces. Since \(8\) and \(12\) both divide \(24\), twenty-fourths do the job. Scale each fraction without changing its value, \(\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{5\times 3}{8\times 3}=\tfrac{15}{24}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{12}=\tfrac{7\times 2}{12\times 2}=\tfrac{14}{24}\). Now every piece is one twenty-fourth of the path, so the fractions really are just counts of the same thing. The north crew has \(15\) of those pieces and the east crew has \(14\), so the north crew is in the lead. To find how far ahead, subtract over the shared denominator, \(\tfrac{15}{24}-\tfrac{14}{24}=\tfrac{15-14}{24}=\tfrac{1}{24}\). Since \(\gcd(1,24)=1\), that fraction is already in simplest form. The north crew is ahead by exactly one twenty-fourth of the path, a gap so thin you could only ever spot it by making the pieces match. \(\boxed{1/24}\)

blocks.x: Compare by common denominator. Two fractions feel hard to compare only because their pieces are different sizes, the way you cannot tell whether twelve dimes beats two quarters until you count everything in cents. Give both fractions a common denominator and every piece becomes the same size, so the question turns into plain counting and the bigger numerator wins, sitting farther right on the line. The least common denominator keeps the numbers small, but any shared denominator works just as well. This method is slower than the shortcuts ahead, yet it never lies and works on any pair of fractions you will ever meet.

The cells line up exactly between the two strips. The single extra shaded cell on the top strip, the fifteenth one, is tinted gold and bracketed below with the label one twenty-fourth, the whole gap between the two fractions, showing that five eighths sits exactly one twenty-fourth past seven twelfths.">
