blocks.cap: Both fractions are laid on one bar sliced into \(24\) equal cells, so every cell is the same width and comparing turns into plain counting. Rebuilt over \(24\), the top strip is \(\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{15}{24}\) with \(15\) cells filled, and the bottom strip is \(\tfrac{7}{12}=\tfrac{14}{24}\) with \(14\) filled. The cells line up exactly between the strips, so the longer shaded bar is simply the one with more cells, which makes \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) the larger. The single gold cell is the whole difference, \(\tfrac{15}{24}-\tfrac{14}{24}=\tfrac{1}{24}\), so \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) sits exactly one twenty-fourth past \(\tfrac{7}{12}\).

blocks.x: Compare \(\tfrac{4}{7}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) via the diagonal products \(4\times 8\) and \(7\times 5\). Which is larger, and what is the gap in simplest form?
hints: The two tops are \(4\times 8=32\) and \(7\times 5=35\). The larger top wins, because both pieces are now measured in the same \(56\)ths. Which fraction does the bigger top belong to?
You have \(\tfrac{32}{56}\) and \(\tfrac{35}{56}\). The gap is just the difference of the tops over \(56\), so compute \(\tfrac{35-32}{56}\) and then reduce.
blocks.sol: Rewrite both fractions over \(56\) without changing their value. The \(\tfrac{4}{7}\) becomes \(\tfrac{4\times 8}{56}=\tfrac{32}{56}\), and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) becomes \(\tfrac{7\times 5}{56}=\tfrac{35}{56}\). Notice the tops, \(32\) and \(35\), are precisely the diagonal products of the original fractions. Once both fractions share the bottom \(56\), the bottom carries no information anymore, so comparing the fractions is the same as comparing those two tops. Since \(35\gt 32\), the fraction \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) sits farther to the right, so \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) is the larger one. The gap is \(\tfrac{35}{56}-\tfrac{32}{56}=\tfrac{3}{56}\). The numbers \(3\) and \(56\) share no common factor, so this is already in simplest form. \(\boxed{3/56}\)

blocks.x: Cross-multiplication for ordering. When you compare \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) and \(\tfrac{c}{d}\) over the shared bottom \(bd\), the two tops become \(a\times d\) and \(b\times c\), and that bottom is the same for both, so it never decides anything. You can skip writing it and just look at the two diagonal products, with the bigger product belonging to the bigger fraction. It is the very same diagonals from before, when \(ad=bc\) told us two fractions landed on the same point. Now they tell us which point sits farther right, as long as both bottoms are positive, so if a denominator is negative, tidy the minus sign up onto the top before cross-multiplying.
Settle the wall: \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) vs \(\tfrac{8}{11}\). Compare \(7\times 11\) against \(9\times 8\). Which is higher, and by how much? Gap in simplest form.
hints: Cross-multiplication sends each top across to the other bottom. Compare \(7\times 11\) with \(9\times 8\), and remember the bigger product belongs to the bigger fraction. That alone tells you which marker is higher.

Once you know \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) is higher, the gap is \(\tfrac{7}{9}-\tfrac{8}{11}\). The common denominator is \(9\times 11=99\), so rewrite both fractions over \(99\) and subtract the numerators. Then check whether the result can be reduced.
blocks.sol: Cross-multiply by sending each numerator across to the opposite denominator. The left marker \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) gives \(7\times 11=77\), and the right marker \(\tfrac{8}{11}\) gives \(9\times 8=72\). Since \(77\gt 72\), the product tied to \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) is the larger one, so \(\tfrac{7}{9}\gt\tfrac{8}{11}\). The left marker sits higher. Now measure the gap. Both products were really numerators over the shared bottom \(9\times 11=99\), so \(\tfrac{7}{9}=\tfrac{77}{99}\) and \(\tfrac{8}{11}=\tfrac{72}{99}\). Subtracting, \(\tfrac{77}{99}-\tfrac{72}{99}=\tfrac{5}{99}\). Check for common factors. The number \(99\) is \(9\times 11\), and \(5\) shares no factor with either, so the fraction is already in simplest form. The left marker beats the right by \(\boxed{5/99}\) of the route.

blocks.x: \(\tfrac{6}{11}\) vs \(\tfrac{6}{13}\): same tops. Elevenths are fatter than thirteenths, so six fat slices win. Find the gap in simplest form.
hints: When two fractions share the same top, the one with the smaller bottom is the larger fraction, because the pieces being counted are bigger. So compare \(\tfrac{6}{11}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{13}\) by their bottoms alone, then subtract the smaller from the larger.

To subtract, rewrite both over the same bottom \(11 \times 13 = 143\). That turns \(\tfrac{6}{11}\) into \(\tfrac{78}{143}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{13}\) into \(\tfrac{66}{143}\). Now subtract the tops and check whether the result can be reduced.
blocks.sol: Both fractions have a top of \(6\), so this is a common-numerator comparison. When the tops match, each fraction is just six copies of one piece, and the size of that piece is set by the bottom. A bottom of \(11\) cuts the canister into eleven pieces, a bottom of \(13\) cuts it into thirteen, and cutting into fewer pieces makes each piece bigger. So an eleventh beats a thirteenth, and six elevenths beats six thirteenths. That makes \(\tfrac{6}{11}\) the larger share. Notice we settled the direction without finding a common bottom at all, just by reading the denominators. Now for the gap. To subtract we do need a shared bottom, so use \(11 \times 13 = 143\). Scaling up, \(\tfrac{6}{11} = \tfrac{6 \times 13}{143} = \tfrac{78}{143}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{13} = \tfrac{6 \times 11}{143} = \tfrac{66}{143}\). Subtract the tops, \(78 - 66 = 12\), which gives \(\tfrac{12}{143}\). Check whether it reduces. Since \(143 = 11 \times 13\) and \(12 = 2 \times 2 \times 3\) share no common factor, \(\gcd(12,143) = 1\) and the fraction is already in simplest form. So the larger plan, \(\tfrac{6}{11}\), beats the other by \(\boxed{12/143}\).

blocks.x: Equal numerators trick. You do not have to match the bottoms, you can match the tops instead. Once two fractions have the same numerator they hold the same number of pieces, so whichever one has the bigger pieces is the bigger fraction. And bigger pieces means a smaller denominator, because cutting a whole into fewer parts leaves each part fatter. So when the tops are equal, the smaller bottom wins, which feels backwards until you remember that an eleventh is a fatter slice than a thirteenth.
Blue \(\tfrac{4}{15}\), red \(\tfrac{3}{10}\). Match tops to \(12\): \(\tfrac{12}{45}\) vs \(\tfrac{12}{40}\). Smaller bottom wins. Which share is larger, and by how much? Gap in simplest form.
hints: You already have the matched-top forms \(\tfrac{12}{45}\) and \(\tfrac{12}{40}\). Same top, so smaller bottom wins. Which bottom is smaller, \(45\) or \(40\)? That tells you the larger fraction. Once you know the order, the gap is just the larger fraction minus the smaller one.

The larger share is \(\tfrac{3}{10}\). To subtract \(\tfrac{3}{10}-\tfrac{4}{15}\), find a common bottom for \(10\) and \(15\), which is \(30\). Rewrite both as thirtieths, then subtract the tops. Simplify if you can.
blocks.sol: Follow the plan and match the tops. The least common multiple of the numerators \(4\) and \(3\) is \(12\). Scale each fraction so its top becomes \(12\). For the blue ribbon, multiply top and bottom by \(3\) to get \(\tfrac{4}{15}=\tfrac{12}{45}\). For the red ribbon, multiply top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{3}{10}=\tfrac{12}{40}\). Now both fractions describe \(12\) equal pieces of something. The blue version breaks its whole into \(45\) parts, the red version into only \(40\) parts. Cutting the same \(12\) pieces from fewer, fatter parts means each piece is bigger, so \(\tfrac{12}{40}\) is the larger fraction. That means the red ribbon, \(\tfrac{3}{10}\), is the bigger share of its roll. Now find the gap by subtracting the smaller from the larger. A common bottom for \(10\) and \(15\) is \(30\). Rewrite both originals as thirtieths, \(\tfrac{3}{10}=\tfrac{9}{30}\) and \(\tfrac{4}{15}=\tfrac{8}{30}\). Subtract the tops, \(\tfrac{9}{30}-\tfrac{8}{30}=\tfrac{1}{30}\). Since \(1\) and \(30\) share no common factor, it is already in simplest form. The red ribbon is the larger share, and it beats the blue ribbon by \(\boxed{1/30}\) of a roll.

blocks.x: Six scores: \(\tfrac{5}{9},\tfrac{4}{9},\tfrac{7}{15},\tfrac{9}{16},\tfrac{11}{20},\tfrac{8}{17}\). Beat \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) when top \(>\) half bottom. How many qualify?
hints: Take one score at a time. For \(\tfrac{5}{9}\), half of the bottom \(9\) is \(\tfrac{9}{2}\), which sits between \(4\) and \(5\). Is the top, \(5\), bigger than that halfway mark? If yes, the score beats \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Do the same quick check for the other five.

For an odd bottom like \(9\), \(15\), or \(17\), the top must reach at least the next whole number above half. Half of \(9\) is \(4\tfrac{1}{2}\), so the top needs to be \(5\) or more. For an even bottom like \(16\) or \(20\), just double the top and compare to the bottom. Mark each score win or lose, then count the wins.
blocks.sol: The whole trick is that \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) of something split into \(b\) parts is \(\tfrac{b}{2}\) of those parts. So a fraction \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) is more than \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) exactly when its top \(a\) is more than half of its bottom \(b\). No common denominators, no arithmetic beyond comparing one number to another. Let us walk the board. For \(\tfrac{5}{9}\), half of \(9\) is \(4\tfrac{1}{2}\), and \(5\) clears it, so this one wins. For \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), half of \(9\) is still \(4\tfrac{1}{2}\), and \(4\) falls short, so it loses. For \(\tfrac{7}{15}\), half of \(15\) is \(7\tfrac{1}{2}\), and \(7\) does not reach it, so it loses. For \(\tfrac{9}{16}\), half of \(16\) is \(8\), and \(9\) beats \(8\), so it wins. For \(\tfrac{11}{20}\), half of \(20\) is \(10\), and \(11\) beats \(10\), so it wins. For \(\tfrac{8}{17}\), half of \(17\) is \(8\tfrac{1}{2}\), and \(8\) lands just under, so it loses. The winners are \(\tfrac{5}{9}\), \(\tfrac{9}{16}\), and \(\tfrac{11}{20}\). Notice how close \(\tfrac{8}{17}\) came, missing by a single half a part, a good reminder that the benchmark catches near misses the eye might wave through. Counting the wins gives \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Landmark comparison. Instead of building a common denominator right away, ask where each fraction sits next to an easy landmark like \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) or a whole \(1\). A fraction beats \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) the moment its top climbs past half its bottom, and it only hits \(1\) when its top finally catches its bottom. So if one fraction parks above the landmark and the other parks below, the bigger one is settled at a glance, with no cross-multiplying and no shared denominator in sight.
Phone 1: \(\tfrac{7}{13}\). Phone 2: \(\tfrac{9}{20}\). Use \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) as landmark, confirm with cross-multiply. By how much is the more-charged phone ahead? Simplest form.
hints: Twice 7 is 14, which clears 13, so \(\tfrac{7}{13}\) sits just above \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Twice 9 is 18, which falls short of 20, so \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) sits just below \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). The one above half is the larger charge. For the exact gap you still need a common denominator.

The winner is \(\tfrac{7}{13}\). To subtract, put both over \(13 \times 20 = 260\). That turns \(\tfrac{7}{13}\) into \(\tfrac{140}{260}\) and \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) into \(\tfrac{117}{260}\). Subtract the tops and check whether the result reduces.
blocks.sol: Start with the landmark, because it splits the pair without any heavy work. A fraction beats \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) exactly when twice its numerator is more than its denominator. For \(\tfrac{7}{13}\), twice 7 is 14, and 14 is more than 13, so \(\tfrac{7}{13}\) sits just above half. For \(\tfrac{9}{20}\), twice 9 is 18, and 18 is less than 20, so \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) sits just below half. One is above the line and one is below, so \(\tfrac{7}{13}\) is the bigger charge and we did not have to compute anything exact yet. Cross-multiplication confirms it. Compare \(7 \times 20 = 140\) against \(9 \times 13 = 117\). Since \(140 \gt 117\), the fraction holding the 140, namely \(\tfrac{7}{13}\), is indeed larger. Now for the exact gap. The cross-multiplication already handed us both numerators over the shared denominator \(13 \times 20 = 260\). So \(\tfrac{7}{13} = \tfrac{140}{260}\) and \(\tfrac{9}{20} = \tfrac{117}{260}\). Subtract along the top, \(140 - 117 = 23\), which gives a difference of \(\tfrac{23}{260}\). Check whether it reduces. The number 23 is prime, and 260 factors as \(2^2 \times 5 \times 13\), which has no 23 in it, so the fraction is already in lowest terms. The more-charged battery leads by \(\boxed{23/260}\).
blocks.label: prob

blocks.x: Tank 1: \(\tfrac{8}{9}\) (missing \(\tfrac{1}{9}\)). Tank 2: \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) (missing \(\tfrac{1}{12}\)). Smaller gap means fuller. Which is fuller, and by how much? Simplest form.
hints: The empty space in the first tank is \(\tfrac{1}{9}\), and in the second it is \(\tfrac{1}{12}\). With the same numerator of 1, the fraction with the bigger bottom number is the smaller piece. So which tank has less empty room, and is therefore fuller?

The fuller tank is the \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) one, since \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) of emptiness is less than \(\tfrac{1}{9}\). The gap in fill levels is exactly the difference between the two empty pieces, \(\tfrac{1}{9}-\tfrac{1}{12}\). Rewrite both over a common bottom of 36 and subtract.
blocks.sol: Comparing \(\tfrac{8}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) head on means wrestling with two different sized pieces. The clever move is to notice that each tank is exactly one piece short of full. The first is missing \(\tfrac{1}{9}\), and the second is missing \(\tfrac{1}{12}\). Both gaps have a numerator of 1, so they are easy to rank. A twelfth of a tank is a thinner sliver than a ninth, so \(\tfrac{1}{12}\lt\tfrac{1}{9}\). The second tank has less empty room, so it is the fuller one, and \(\tfrac{11}{12}\gt\tfrac{8}{9}\). Now for the gap between the fill levels. Here is the neat part. The difference in how full the tanks are is the very same as the difference in how empty they are, because each missing piece is measured from the same full top. So the gap is \(\tfrac{1}{9}-\tfrac{1}{12}\), the difference of the two empty pieces. The smallest common bottom for 9 and 12 is 36, since \(9=3\cdot 3\) and \(12=3\cdot 4\) share a single factor of 3. Rewrite each piece, \(\tfrac{1}{9}=\tfrac{4}{36}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{12}=\tfrac{3}{36}\), then subtract to get \(\tfrac{4}{36}-\tfrac{3}{36}=\tfrac{1}{36}\). Since 1 and 36 share no common factor, it is already in simplest form. The second tank is fuller, by \(\boxed{1/36}\) of a tank.

blocks.x: One-piece-short trick. When two fractions each sit just one piece short of a whole, you do not have to wrestle with the fractions at all. Just look at the piece each one is missing. The fraction with the smaller missing piece has less ground left to cover, so it sits closer to \(1\) and wins. With \(\tfrac{8}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{11}{12}\), the gaps are \(\tfrac{1}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{12}\), and since \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) is the smaller gap, \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) is the larger fraction. Compare the gaps, not the fractions, and the smaller gap belongs to the bigger number.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 214" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two stacked horizontal bars of equal length running from a start line at zero on the left to a bold finish line at one on the right, both aligned so their right ends meet the same finish line. The top bar, labelled eight ninths, is filled with soft shading and its final unshaded sliver, the missing piece up to one, is tinted gold and labelled one ninth. The bottom bar, labelled eleven twelfths, is filled and its final unshaded sliver is visibly thinner, tinted a deeper gold and labelled one twelfth. Because both empty slivers sit against the same finish line, the eye sees at once that the one twelfth gap is shorter, so the bottom bar is fuller and eleven twelfths is the larger fraction."><text fill="var(--gold-deep)" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif">1⁄9</text><line x1="365.83" y1="152" x2="365.83" y2="194" stroke="var(--gold-deep)" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/><text x="365.83" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif">1⁄12</text><text x="150" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif">smaller gap, fuller bar, so 11⁄12 wins</text></svg>
blocks.cap: Both bars run to the same finish line at \(1\), so do not bother measuring the long full part. Measure the short empty part instead. The top bar is missing \(\tfrac{1}{9}\) and the bottom bar is missing only \(\tfrac{1}{12}\), and lined up against the finish line that thinner gap is plain to see. A ninth of the bar is wider than a twelfth of it, since cutting into more pieces makes each piece smaller. The bar with the smaller gap is the fuller one, so \(\tfrac{11}{12}\) wins.

blocks.x: Bars \(\tfrac{13}{14}\) and \(\tfrac{14}{15}\): gaps \(\tfrac{1}{14}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{15}\). Smaller gap is closer to done. Report the gap between the two bars in simplest form.
hints: A bar is further along when the piece still left to fill is smaller. Compare the leftover pieces \(\tfrac{1}{14}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) first, since two unit fractions are easy to rank by their denominators.

The distance between the two bars is exactly the distance between their leftover pieces, because both bars are measured down from the same finish line. So compute \(\tfrac{1}{14}-\tfrac{1}{15}\). Over a common denominator of \(210\) that is \(\tfrac{15}{210}-\tfrac{14}{210}\).
blocks.sol: Both bars are racing toward the same finish line, the whole \(1\). So instead of asking how full each bar is, ask how empty it is. The top bar has \(\tfrac{1}{14}\) left to fill, the bottom bar has \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) left. With unit fractions the smaller denominator means the bigger piece, so \(\tfrac{1}{14}\gt\tfrac{1}{15}\). The bottom bar has the smaller piece remaining, which means the bottom bar at \(\tfrac{14}{15}\) is the one closer to finished. Now the clever part. The gap between the two bars is the same as the gap between their leftover pieces, since both leftovers are measured from that same finish line. Sliding both measurements down from \(1\) does not change the distance between them. So the answer is \(\tfrac{1}{14}-\tfrac{1}{15}\). Subtracting unit fractions has a tidy shortcut. \(\tfrac{1}{14}-\tfrac{1}{15}=\tfrac{15-14}{14\times 15}=\tfrac{1}{210}\). The denominator \(210\) is the very product we were trying to dodge, but here it shows up only once, at the end, instead of forcing us to rewrite both bars as fourteenths-of-fifteenths from the start. The numerator is \(1\), so the fraction is already in simplest form. The two bars are exactly \(\boxed{1/210}\) apart, with the \(\tfrac{14}{15}\) bar in the lead.

blocks.x: Archimedes (c. 250 BC) trapped \(\pi\) between \(\tfrac{223}{71}\) and \(\tfrac{22}{7}\) with no decimals, needing to verify \(\tfrac{223}{71}<\tfrac{22}{7}\), the same tools you used all chapter.
Temperatures \(-\tfrac{3}{5}\) and \(-\tfrac{5}{8}\): both left of \(0\). Warmer is nearer \(0\). Which is the larger value, and how far apart are they? Positive fraction in simplest form.
hints: Forget the signs for a moment and just compare the sizes \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). A common denominator of \(40\) lets you stack them up. Whichever size is bigger is the morning that dipped deeper below the baseline, and a deeper dip means a value farther from \(0\).

Rewrite both sizes over \(40\). You get \(\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{24}{40}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{25}{40}\), so \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) is the bigger size and \(-\tfrac{5}{8}\) is the deeper, more negative reading. The larger value is the shallower one, \(-\tfrac{3}{5}\). For the gap, subtract the two sizes, \(\tfrac{25}{40}-\tfrac{24}{40}\).
blocks.sol: Negative readings flip the usual instinct. With ordinary positive fractions, the bigger one wins, but down here below \(0\) the value that is larger is the one closer to \(0\), not the one with the bigger size. So the plan is to compare the sizes first, then mirror that result across \(0\). Put both sizes over a common denominator of \(40\). That gives \(\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{24}{40}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{25}{40}\). Since \(\tfrac{25}{40}\gt\tfrac{24}{40}\), the size \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) is the bigger one, which means \(-\tfrac{5}{8}\) dipped deeper below the baseline and sits farther to the left. The shallower dip, \(-\tfrac{3}{5}\), sits nearer to \(0\), so \(-\tfrac{3}{5}\gt -\tfrac{5}{8}\) and the warmer morning, the larger value, is \(-\tfrac{3}{5}\). Now the gap. The distance between two points on the number line is the same whether you measure the readings or their mirror images, so the gap is just the difference of the sizes, \(\tfrac{25}{40}-\tfrac{24}{40}=\tfrac{1}{40}\). The numerator \(1\) and denominator \(40\) share no common factor, so it is already in simplest form. The two mornings were a razor-thin \(\tfrac{1}{40}\) of a degree apart. \(\boxed{1/40}\)

blocks.x: Comparing negative fractions. On the right of \(0\) the bigger fraction is the one that reaches farther out, but on the left that whole feeling turns inside out. The negatives are the mirror image of the positives across \(0\), so farther from \(0\) now means deeper below it, and deeper below means smaller. So between two negative fractions the one hugging closer to \(0\) is the larger value, and the more negative one, sitting farther left, is the smaller. The safe habit is to compare the two as if both were positive, using whatever trick you like, then flip the verdict, since the bigger size is the one that drops you farther down.

Three runners are near the finish. Mara is missing \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of the course and Priya is missing \(\tfrac{1}{12}\), so settle those two by the smaller missing piece. Dev is at \(\tfrac{7}{9}\), so place him with a cross-multiply. Order all three, then report the gap between the leader and the runner in last, in simplest form.
hints: Handle Mara and Priya with the missing piece first. Mara still has \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of the course to go and Priya has only \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) to go. A smaller piece left means more finished, so one of them is clearly ahead of the other. For Dev, compare \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) against the others with a cross-multiply rather than guessing.

Compare Dev to Mara by cross-multiplying \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{6}\). You get \(7\times 6 = 42\) on Dev's side and \(5\times 9 = 45\) on Mara's side, so \(\tfrac{7}{9} \lt \tfrac{5}{6}\). Together with the missing-piece result, the order from least to most is Dev, then Mara, then Priya. Now the gap you want is leader minus last, which is \(\tfrac{11}{12} - \tfrac{7}{9}\). Rewrite both over \(36\) and subtract.
blocks.sol:
Start with the two that the missing-piece view settles instantly. Mara is \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of the course short of the finish, and Priya is \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) short. The runner with the smaller piece left has gone farther, and \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) is smaller than \(\tfrac{1}{6}\), so Priya is ahead of Mara.

Now place Dev. Compare \(\tfrac{7}{9}\) with Mara's \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) by cross-multiplying. On Dev's side \(7\times 6 = 42\), on Mara's side \(5\times 9 = 45\). Since \(42 \lt 45\), we get \(\tfrac{7}{9} \lt \tfrac{5}{6}\), so Dev is behind Mara. That already puts Dev behind Priya too, since Mara was behind Priya.

So from least finished to most finished the order is Dev \(\left(\tfrac{7}{9}\right)\), then Mara \(\left(\tfrac{5}{6}\right)\), then Priya \(\left(\tfrac{11}{12}\right)\). The leader is Priya and the runner in last is Dev.

The gap between them is \(\tfrac{11}{12} - \tfrac{7}{9}\). A common denominator for \(12\) and \(9\) is \(36\). Rewrite each fraction, \(\tfrac{11}{12} = \tfrac{33}{36}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{9} = \tfrac{28}{36}\). Subtracting gives \(\tfrac{33}{36} - \tfrac{28}{36} = \tfrac{5}{36}\). Since \(5\) and \(36\) share no common factor, it is already in simplest form.

\(\boxed{5/36}\)

blocks.x: \(\tfrac{98}{99}\) misses \(\tfrac{1}{99}\); \(\tfrac{100}{101}\) misses \(\tfrac{1}{101}\). Smaller missing piece is larger rate. Which rate wins, and what is the gap in simplest form?
hints: Forget the giant denominators for a second. Which missing piece is smaller, \(\tfrac{1}{99}\) or \(\tfrac{1}{101}\)? Fewer equal parts means bigger parts, so \(\tfrac{1}{99}\) is the larger gap. The rate that is missing the larger gap is further from \(1\), and the rate missing the smaller gap is the winner.

The two rates differ by exactly the difference between their missing pieces, \(\tfrac{1}{99}-\tfrac{1}{101}\). Over the common denominator \(99\times 101 = 9999\), that is \(\tfrac{101}{9999}-\tfrac{99}{9999}\). Subtract the tops and check whether the result can be simplified.
blocks.sol: Start with the picture instead of the arithmetic. Both rates are almost a whole, each one a single unit fraction short. The rate \(\tfrac{98}{99}\) falls short by \(\tfrac{1}{99}\), and the rate \(\tfrac{100}{101}\) falls short by \(\tfrac{1}{101}\). Since \(99\) parts are bigger than \(101\) parts of the same whole, \(\tfrac{1}{99}\) is the larger gap. The fraction carrying the larger gap sits further below \(1\), so \(\tfrac{100}{101}\) is the larger rate. No four-digit comparison needed, just a glance at the two missing pieces. Now the gap between the rates. The distance from \(\tfrac{98}{99}\) up to \(1\) is \(\tfrac{1}{99}\) and the distance from \(\tfrac{100}{101}\) up to \(1\) is \(\tfrac{1}{101}\), so the space between the two rates is the difference of those gaps, \(\tfrac{1}{99}-\tfrac{1}{101}\). Put them over the common denominator \(99\times 101 = 9999\). That gives \(\tfrac{101}{9999}-\tfrac{99}{9999} = \tfrac{2}{9999}\). Check for simplifying. Here \(9999 = 9\times 11\times 101\), which has no factor of \(2\), so \(\tfrac{2}{9999}\) is already in lowest terms. The denominator we dreaded computing only had to appear once, at the very end, instead of inside a comparison. So the larger rate is \(\tfrac{100}{101}\), and the two rates differ by \(\boxed{2/9999}\).

review.x: Rosa: \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of her wall. Ben: \(\tfrac{5}{7}\) of his. Find the common denominator, compare, give the gap as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: The smallest number that both 4 and 7 divide into is their product, since 4 and 7 share no common factor. Use that as your common denominator and rewrite each fraction.
Once both fractions have the same bottom number, the one with the larger top number is the bigger share. Subtract the smaller top from the larger top and keep the common denominator.
review.sol: Since \(4\) and \(7\) share no common factor, the smallest shared denominator is \(4 \times 7 = 28\). Rewrite each share over \(28\). For Rosa, \(\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{3 \times 7}{4 \times 7} = \tfrac{21}{28}\). For Ben, \(\tfrac{5}{7} = \tfrac{5 \times 4}{7 \times 4} = \tfrac{20}{28}\). Now the comparison is easy because the pieces are the same size. Rosa has \(21\) of them and Ben has \(20\), so Rosa has painted the larger share. The gap is \(\tfrac{21}{28} - \tfrac{20}{28} = \tfrac{1}{28}\). Since \(1\) and \(28\) share no common factor, this is already in simplest form. The gap between the two shares is \(\boxed{1/28}\).

review.x: Compare \(\tfrac{8}{11}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{10}\) by cross-multiplying. Which is larger, and how much greater is it? Report the gap as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: Cross-multiply by going up the diagonals. The product \(8\times 10\) belongs to \(\tfrac{8}{11}\), and \(11\times 7\) belongs to \(\tfrac{7}{10}\). Compare \(80\) and \(77\) to decide which fraction is bigger.

Those same diagonal products are exactly the numerators you get over the common denominator \(11\times 10=110\). So \(\tfrac{8}{11}=\tfrac{80}{110}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{10}=\tfrac{77}{110}\). Subtract the smaller from the larger over \(110\).
review.sol: Cross-multiplying sends each numerator up the diagonal across the other fraction's denominator. For \(\tfrac{8}{11}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{10}\) the diagonal products are \(8\times 10=80\) and \(11\times 7=77\). Since \(80\gt 77\), the fraction sitting under the larger product wins, so \(\tfrac{8}{11}\) is the larger fraction. Here is why those numbers settle it. Both diagonal products are really the numerators you would get over the shared denominator \(11\times 10=110\). That gives \(\tfrac{8}{11}=\tfrac{80}{110}\) and \(\tfrac{7}{10}=\tfrac{77}{110}\), now wearing the same denominator. The gap is \(\tfrac{80}{110}-\tfrac{77}{110}=\tfrac{3}{110}\). Since \(\gcd(3,110)=1\), this is already in simplest form. So the larger rate beats the smaller by exactly \(\boxed{3/110}\).

review.x: Find \(k\) so that \(\tfrac{6}{k}=\tfrac{15}{20}\). Set diagonal products equal: \(6\times 20=k\times 15\). What is \(k\)?
hints: The cross-multiplication test says two fractions are equal exactly when their diagonal products match. Write out \(6\times 20\) on one side and \(k\times 15\) on the other.
You get \(120 = 15k\). Ask yourself what number times \(15\) gives \(120\).
review.sol: For \(\tfrac{6}{k}\) to equal \(\tfrac{15}{20}\), the cross products have to match. The top-left times the bottom-right equals the bottom-left times the top-right, so \(6\times 20 = k\times 15\). That gives \(120 = 15k\). Now find the \(k\) that fits, \(15k = 120\) means \(k = 120\div 15 = 8\). Quick check, \(\tfrac{6}{8}\) reduces to \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{15}{20}\) also reduces to \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), so the two really are the same fraction. \(\boxed{8}\)

review.x: Compare \(\tfrac{8}{15}\) and \(\tfrac{8}{13}\): same numerator, different denominators. Which is larger? Report how much more that person has as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: The numerators already match at 8. When the top numbers are equal, the fraction with the smaller bottom number is built from bigger pieces, so it is the larger one.
To find the gap, give both fractions the common bottom \(15 \times 13 = 195\), then subtract the smaller from the larger.
review.sol: Both fractions have the same top, 8. Think about what the bottom tells you. Cutting a ribbon into 13 strands makes each strand bigger than cutting it into 15 strands, since you are sharing the same ribbon among fewer pieces. Eight bigger strands beat eight smaller strands, so \(\tfrac{8}{13} \gt \tfrac{8}{15}\). The person with the 13-strand ribbon has more. Now measure the gap. A common bottom for both is \(15 \times 13 = 195\). Rewriting, \(\tfrac{8}{13} = \tfrac{8 \times 15}{195} = \tfrac{120}{195}\) and \(\tfrac{8}{15} = \tfrac{8 \times 13}{195} = \tfrac{104}{195}\). The difference is \(\tfrac{120}{195} - \tfrac{104}{195} = \tfrac{16}{195}\). Check that this is in lowest terms. The number 16 is \(2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2\), and 195 is \(3 \times 5 \times 13\), so they share no common factor and \(\gcd(16, 195) = 1\). The gap is \(\boxed{16/195}\).

review.x: \(\tfrac{6}{35}\) and \(\tfrac{9}{40}\) each rewritten with numerator \(18\): \(\tfrac{18}{105}\) vs \(\tfrac{18}{80}\). Smaller bottom wins. Gap between them in simplest form?
hints: You already have matching tops. With \(\tfrac{18}{105}\) and \(\tfrac{18}{80}\), splitting the jar into \(80\) parts makes each part bigger than splitting it into \(105\) parts, so \(\tfrac{18}{80}\) is the larger amount. That means the second recipe uses more.

To find the gap, subtract the smaller from the larger using a common bottom. A bottom that both \(40\) and \(35\) divide into is \(280\). Rewrite \(\tfrac{9}{40}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{35}\) over \(280\), subtract, then simplify.
review.sol: First decide which recipe wins. The matching tops are already set up, \(\tfrac{6}{35}=\tfrac{18}{105}\) and \(\tfrac{9}{40}=\tfrac{18}{80}\). Same top, so compare bottoms. Cutting the jar into \(80\) pieces gives larger pieces than cutting it into \(105\) pieces, and we take \(18\) of them either way, so \(\tfrac{18}{80}\gt\tfrac{18}{105}\). The second recipe, \(\tfrac{9}{40}\), uses more. Now measure the gap by subtracting. Use a bottom that both \(40\) and \(35\) divide into, and \(280\) works since \(40\times 7=280\) and \(35\times 8=280\). Rewrite each piece. \(\tfrac{9}{40}=\tfrac{9\times 7}{280}=\tfrac{63}{280}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{35}=\tfrac{6\times 8}{280}=\tfrac{48}{280}\). Subtract the smaller from the larger, \(\tfrac{63}{280}-\tfrac{48}{280}=\tfrac{15}{280}\). Both top and bottom share a factor of \(5\), so \(\tfrac{15}{280}=\tfrac{3}{56}\). The second recipe uses \(\tfrac{3}{56}\) more of the jar. \(\boxed{3/56}\)

review.x: Scores: \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), \(\tfrac{5}{11}\), \(\tfrac{10}{19}\), \(\tfrac{6}{13}\), \(\tfrac{9}{17}\). Beat \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) when top \(>\) half the bottom. How many exceed \(\tfrac{1}{2}\)?
hints: For each fraction, ask what half of the bottom number is. For \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), half of \(12\) is \(6\), and the top \(7\) is more than \(6\), so it clears \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Try the same single comparison on each of the other four.

An even cleaner test that avoids halves is to double the top and compare it with the bottom. The fraction beats \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) when twice the top is bigger than the bottom. Run that check on all five and tally the ones that pass.
review.sol: The clean test is to compare twice the top with the bottom. A fraction is more than \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) exactly when twice its top is larger than its bottom, because \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) is the same as having a top equal to half the bottom. Go down the list. For \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), twice \(7\) is \(14\), which is more than \(12\), so it clears the bar. For \(\tfrac{5}{11}\), twice \(5\) is \(10\), which is less than \(11\), so it falls short. For \(\tfrac{10}{19}\), twice \(10\) is \(20\), which is more than \(19\), so it clears. For \(\tfrac{6}{13}\), twice \(6\) is \(12\), which is less than \(13\), so it falls short. For \(\tfrac{9}{17}\), twice \(9\) is \(18\), which is more than \(17\), so it clears. Three of the five passed the test, namely \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), \(\tfrac{10}{19}\), and \(\tfrac{9}{17}\). The count of scores more than \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) is \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: Green trail checkpoint: \(\tfrac{8}{15}\). Orange: \(\tfrac{7}{16}\). Use \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) as a landmark to decide which is farther, then find the gap. Give your answer as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: A fraction is more than \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) exactly when twice its top number is more than its bottom number. Check \(2 \times 8\) against \(15\), and \(2 \times 7\) against \(16\). One fraction lands above the landmark and the other lands below it.
Once you know which fraction is larger, subtract the smaller from the larger. A denominator that both \(15\) and \(16\) divide into is \(240\), since \(15 \times 16 = 240\).
review.sol: Start with the landmark \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). A fraction beats \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) exactly when twice its numerator is more than its denominator. For \(\tfrac{8}{15}\), twice the numerator is \(2 \times 8 = 16\), and \(16\) is more than \(15\), so \(\tfrac{8}{15} \gt \tfrac{1}{2}\). For \(\tfrac{7}{16}\), twice the numerator is \(2 \times 7 = 14\), and \(14\) is less than \(16\), so \(\tfrac{7}{16} \lt \tfrac{1}{2}\). The landmark splits them cleanly, which means \(\tfrac{8}{15}\) is the larger fraction without any common denominator yet. So the green trail checkpoint is farther. Now find the gap. A denominator both \(15\) and \(16\) share is \(15 \times 16 = 240\). Rewrite each fraction. \(\tfrac{8}{15} = \tfrac{8 \times 16}{240} = \tfrac{128}{240}\), and \(\tfrac{7}{16} = \tfrac{7 \times 15}{240} = \tfrac{105}{240}\). Subtract to get the gap. \(\tfrac{128}{240} - \tfrac{105}{240} = \tfrac{23}{240}\). Since \(23\) is prime and does not divide \(240\), the fraction is already in simplest form. \(\boxed{23/240}\)

review.x: Cup 1: \(\tfrac{9}{10}\) full (gap \(\tfrac{1}{10}\)). Cup 2: \(\tfrac{10}{11}\) full (gap \(\tfrac{1}{11}\)). Smaller gap means fuller. Find the gap between fill levels in simplest form.
hints: The leftover gaps are \(\tfrac{1}{10}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{11}\). With the same numerator \(1\), the fraction with the bigger bottom is the smaller piece, so its cup has less empty space and is fuller.
The distance between the two fill levels is exactly the difference between the two gaps, \(\tfrac{1}{10}-\tfrac{1}{11}\). Rewrite both over a common denominator of \(110\) and subtract.
review.sol: Comparing the fills directly is awkward, so compare the gaps at the top instead. The first cup leaves \(\tfrac{1}{10}\) empty and the second leaves \(\tfrac{1}{11}\) empty. Both gaps have numerator \(1\), and when the numerator is fixed, a larger denominator cuts the whole into more pieces, so each piece is smaller. Since \(11 \gt 10\), the gap \(\tfrac{1}{11}\) is smaller than \(\tfrac{1}{10}\). A smaller empty gap means more liquid, so the second cup at \(\tfrac{10}{11}\) is the fuller one. Now for the distance between the levels. The first level sits \(\tfrac{1}{10}\) below the brim and the second sits \(\tfrac{1}{11}\) below the brim, so the space between them is \(\tfrac{1}{10}-\tfrac{1}{11}\). A common denominator is \(110\), giving \(\tfrac{11}{110}-\tfrac{10}{110}=\tfrac{1}{110}\). The gap denominators differed by one step, and that single step is worth exactly \(\tfrac{1}{110}\) of a cup. So the fuller cup is \(\tfrac{10}{11}\), and the levels are \(\boxed{1/110}\) of a cup apart.

review.x: Survey 1: missing \(\tfrac{1}{50}\). Survey 2: missing \(\tfrac{1}{51}\). Smaller missing piece is further along. Gap between completion fractions in simplest form?
hints: A fraction that is one piece short of a whole is bigger when the missing piece is smaller. Compare \(\tfrac{1}{50}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{51}\) to see which survey has less left to do.
The gap between the two completion fractions equals the gap between their missing pieces, because both surveys are measured against the same whole. So the answer is \(\tfrac{1}{50}-\tfrac{1}{51}\).
review.sol: Instead of forcing a giant common denominator, look at what each survey still has left. The survey at \(\tfrac{49}{50}\) is one fiftieth short of done, and the survey at \(\tfrac{50}{51}\) is one fifty-first short of done. A leftover piece of \(\tfrac{1}{51}\) is smaller than a leftover piece of \(\tfrac{1}{50}\), since cutting a whole into more parts makes each part tinier. Less left to do means more done, so the survey at \(\tfrac{50}{51}\) is the one closer to finished. Now the gap. Both surveys are being compared to the same finished survey, so the distance between their completion fractions is exactly the distance between their missing pieces. That gives \(\tfrac{1}{50}-\tfrac{1}{51}\). A common denominator of \(50\) and \(51\) is \(50\times 51=2{,}550\), so \(\tfrac{1}{50}=\tfrac{51}{2550}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{51}=\tfrac{50}{2550}\). Subtracting gives \(\tfrac{51}{2550}-\tfrac{50}{2550}=\tfrac{1}{2550}\). Since \(2{,}550\) has factors of \(2\), \(3\), \(5\), and \(17\) while the numerator is \(1\), the fraction is already in simplest form. The two surveys are a hair apart, and the one at \(\tfrac{50}{51}\) leads by \(\boxed{1/2550}\).

review.x: Temperatures \(-\tfrac{5}{9}\) and \(-\tfrac{4}{7}\). Warmer is closer to \(0\). Which reading is warmer? Report how far apart the readings are as a positive fraction in simplest form.
hints: Forget the signs for a moment and compare the sizes \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) and \(\tfrac{4}{7}\). A common denominator of \(63\) turns them into \(\tfrac{35}{63}\) and \(\tfrac{36}{63}\), so \(\tfrac{4}{7}\) is the bigger size.

A bigger size means the negative reading drops further below \(0\), so \(-\tfrac{4}{7}\) is the colder one and \(-\tfrac{5}{9}\) is the warmer one. The gap between them is just the difference of the two sizes, \(\tfrac{36}{63}-\tfrac{35}{63}\).
review.sol: First compare the two sizes, ignoring the minus signs. The denominators are \(9\) and \(7\), and \(9 \times 7 = 63\), so rewrite both over \(63\). We get \(\tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{35}{63}\) and \(\tfrac{4}{7} = \tfrac{36}{63}\). Since \(36 \gt 35\), the size \(\tfrac{4}{7}\) is larger. Now bring the signs back. A larger size pushes a negative number further from \(0\), so \(-\tfrac{4}{7}\) sits lower than \(-\tfrac{5}{9}\). That makes \(-\tfrac{5}{9}\) the warmer reading, the one nearer to \(0\). To find how far apart the two readings are, subtract the smaller size from the larger size, since the gap between \(-\tfrac{35}{63}\) and \(-\tfrac{36}{63}\) is one \(\tfrac{1}{63}\) step. So the distance is \(\tfrac{36}{63} - \tfrac{35}{63} = \tfrac{1}{63}\), which is already in simplest form. The two readings are \(\boxed{1/63}\) apart.

review.x: Four sprinters: \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), \(\tfrac{5}{8}\), \(\tfrac{11}{18}\), \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Use the fastest comparison tool for each pair. Write the single largest fraction in its original form.
hints: A common denominator that fits 12, 8, 18, and 3 makes all four easy to read at once. The smallest one that works is 72.
Rewrite each fraction over 72 and compare the top numbers. Whichever has the biggest numerator is the leader, and you report that fraction the way it first appeared.
review.sol: Pick a common denominator that all four fit into. The denominators are \(12\), \(8\), \(18\), and \(3\), and the smallest number they all divide is \(72\). Rewrite each runner's distance over \(72\). For Aanya, \(\tfrac{7}{12}=\tfrac{42}{72}\). For Beck, \(\tfrac{5}{8}=\tfrac{45}{72}\). For Cory, \(\tfrac{11}{18}=\tfrac{44}{72}\). For Dev, \(\tfrac{2}{3}=\tfrac{48}{72}\). Now the numerators tell the whole story, since the pieces are the same size. Lined up from least to most they go \(\tfrac{42}{72}\), \(\tfrac{44}{72}\), \(\tfrac{45}{72}\), \(\tfrac{48}{72}\), which is Aanya, then Cory, then Beck, then Dev. The leader is \(\tfrac{48}{72}\), and that came from \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). So the largest of the four, in its original form, is \(\boxed{2/3}\).

review.x: Sand: gains \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), loses \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). Find what remains, compare to \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). By how much does it differ from \(\tfrac{1}{2}\)? Simplest form.
hints: First do the subtraction from lesson 4.6. To work out \(\tfrac{3}{4}-\tfrac{1}{3}\), rewrite both fractions over a common denominator of \(12\) before you take anything away.
Once you have the leftover amount, put it next to \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) using that same denominator of \(12\). The gap between them is just the difference of the two numerators over \(12\).
review.sol: Start with the subtraction. The denominators \(4\) and \(3\) both fit into \(12\), so rewrite each fraction over \(12\). We get \(\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{9}{12}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{12}\). Taking the leak away leaves \(\tfrac{9}{12}-\tfrac{4}{12}=\tfrac{5}{12}\) of a scoop in the jar. Now compare that with \(\tfrac{1}{2}\). Written over the same denominator, \(\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{6}{12}\). Since \(\tfrac{5}{12}\lt\tfrac{6}{12}\), the sand that remains is a little less than half a scoop. The gap is \(\tfrac{6}{12}-\tfrac{5}{12}=\tfrac{1}{12}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) is already in simplest form because \(\gcd(1,12)=1\). So the remaining sand falls short of the halfway mark by \(\boxed{1/12}\) of a scoop.

review.x: Maya: \(\tfrac{13}{16}\) of her goal. Theo: \(\tfrac{17}{20}\). Both past halfway, so use cross-multiplication. By what fraction, in simplest form, is the leader ahead?
hints: The denominators are 16 and 20. Find a number both divide into so you can rewrite both fractions with the same bottom, then they are easy to line up.
The smallest common denominator is 80. Turn \(\tfrac{13}{16}\) and \(\tfrac{17}{20}\) into eightieths, see which numerator is bigger, then subtract to get the gap.
review.sol: The two denominators are 16 and 20. A denominator they share is \(80\), since \(16 \times 5 = 80\) and \(20 \times 4 = 80\). Rewrite each fraction in eightieths. For Maya, \(\tfrac{13}{16} = \tfrac{13 \times 5}{80} = \tfrac{65}{80}\). For Theo, \(\tfrac{17}{20} = \tfrac{17 \times 4}{80} = \tfrac{68}{80}\). Now the comparison is plain. Out of \(80\) equal parts Theo has \(68\) and Maya has \(65\), so Theo is further toward his goal and he is the leader. The gap between them is the difference of the two amounts, \(\tfrac{68}{80} - \tfrac{65}{80} = \tfrac{3}{80}\). Check whether \(\tfrac{3}{80}\) can be reduced. The top is \(3\), and \(80 = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 5\) has no factor of \(3\), so \(\gcd(3,80) = 1\) and the fraction is already in simplest form. The leader is ahead by \(\boxed{3/80}\).

review.x: Mosaic 1: \(\tfrac{123}{124}\) done (missing \(\tfrac{1}{124}\)). Mosaic 2: \(\tfrac{199}{200}\) (missing \(\tfrac{1}{200}\)). Smaller missing piece wins. Report the gap in simplest form.
hints: Comparing the leftover holes is far easier than comparing the filled parts. A mosaic with a smaller missing piece must be the more complete one, so compare \(\tfrac{1}{124}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{200}\) first.

The gap between the two completions is the same as the gap between the two missing pieces. Compute \(\tfrac{1}{124}-\tfrac{1}{200}\) by writing both over their least common denominator, then simplify.
review.sol: Chasing the filled amounts \(\tfrac{123}{124}\) and \(\tfrac{199}{200}\) head on means a denominator of \(124\times 200\), which is huge and easy to fumble. There is a cleaner way in. Each mosaic is one tile from done, so what is left undone is \(\tfrac{1}{124}\) for the first and \(\tfrac{1}{200}\) for the second. With the same numerator of \(1\), the fraction with the larger denominator is the smaller hole. Since \(200\gt 124\), the leftover \(\tfrac{1}{200}\) is smaller than \(\tfrac{1}{124}\), so the second mosaic has less missing and is the more complete one. That means \(\tfrac{199}{200}\gt\tfrac{123}{124}\). Now for the gap. The distance between the two completions equals the distance between the two leftover holes, because filling more of a mosaic just shrinks its hole by the same amount. So the gap is \(\tfrac{1}{124}-\tfrac{1}{200}\). The least common denominator of \(124\) and \(200\) is \(6200\), since \(124=4\times 31\) and \(200=8\times 25\) share only the factor \(4\), giving \(\gcd=4\) and \(\text{lcm}=\tfrac{124\times 200}{4}=6200\). Rewrite each piece, \(\tfrac{1}{124}=\tfrac{50}{6200}\) and \(\tfrac{1}{200}=\tfrac{31}{6200}\). Subtract to get \(\tfrac{50}{6200}-\tfrac{31}{6200}=\tfrac{19}{6200}\). Since \(19\) is prime and does not divide \(6200\), the fraction is already in lowest terms. The second mosaic leads, and the gap between the two is \(\boxed{19/6200}\).
